pattern_issues.key_full: Melacha — Melacha is transformative labor forbidden on Shabbat — including cooking. On Yom Tov most of the 39 melachot remain forbidden (writing, building, kindling a new fire, etc.), but the Torah permits ochel nefesh — preparing food for that festival day, including cooking transferred from a pre-existing flame (Exodus 12:16). You may not cook on Yom Tov for the next day without eruv tavshilin when Shabbat follows. When unsure, ask your rav before acting.

pattern_issues.key_full: tevilat keilim — Tevilat keilim immerses new food utensils manufactured by a non-Jew in a mikveh — recalling the vessels Israel immersed after the Midian war. The obligation follows the manufacturer, not the retailer. Metal and glass usually require a bracha; glazed ceramic often requires immersion without a bracha. Items manufactured by a Jew are generally exempt. Labels "tovel before use" remind you — immersion is quick at a keilim mikveh or community event.

pattern_issues.key: The Fast of Esther (Ta'anit Esther) is observed on 13 Adar, the day before Purim. It commemorates the three-day fast of
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Fast of Esther (Ta'anit Esther) is observed on 13 Adar, the day before Purim. It commemorates the three-day fast of Esther and the Jewish people before she approached King Achashverosh to plead on behalf of her people.

It is a minor fast from dawn until nightfall. If 13 Adar falls on Shabbat, the fast is moved to 11 Adar. Machatzit HaShekel (a half-shekel donation to charity) is given on Purim morning, sometimes before Megillah reading on the night before.

Purim Meshulash — mishloach manot on Sunday (16 Adar)

Deferred from Shabbat because Purim mitzvot are not performed on Shabbat this year.

The mitzvah:
• Send at least two different ready-to-eat foods or drinks to one friend today — one package.
• Deliver before sunset; a messenger is fine.
• Food should be ready to eat without cooking; label sender and recipient.

You should have prepared packages before Shabbat. If not, ask your rav what you may still do today.

Edot HaMizrach communities follow different Omer traditions (Peninei Halakha 05-03-03):
• Many follow Shulchan Arukh O.C. 493:1-2 — mourning until the morning of the 34th day of the Omer.
• Many who follow the Ari act strictly and refrain from haircuts until the day before Shavuot (Kaf HaChaim 493:13, cited in Peninei Halakha 05-03-03).
• Some North African kehillot end mourning on Lag BaOmer.

Music, weddings, and haircuts follow your kehilla's psak — ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key: Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine, in the same place you e
pattern_issues.key_full: Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine, in the same place you eat a meal afterwards. You would not fulfill Kiddush if you recited it and left without eating there. On Shabbat night, the meal should include bread (hamotzi) per most poskim. On Yom Tov or daytime Kiddush, other foods may qualify in some cases; ask your rav. If you make Kiddush in one room, the meal should follow there and not in a different space.

pattern_issues.key: Sephardi — Sephardi Jews trace roots to the Iberian diaspora and related Mediterranean communities. Prayer is usually Nu
pattern_issues.key_full: Sephardi — Sephardi Jews trace roots to the Iberian diaspora and related Mediterranean communities. Prayer is usually Nusach Sefard (Bet Yosef). Halacha generally follows the Shulchan Aruch and poskim such as Rav Ovadia Yosef zt"l. Rice and kitniyot are halachically permitted on Pesach where Ashkenazim follow the kitniyot custom. Do not confuse with "Nusach Sefard" on a Chasidic siddur (an Ashkenazi rite) or with Edot HaMizrach (Middle Eastern / North African nusach).

pattern_issues.key_full: Tzitzit are fringes on four-cornered garments reminding us of all 613 mitzvot. The tallit katan is worn daily — generally over or under the shirt, depending on community custom (many Chassidim, Sephardim, and followers of the Arizal wear it over the shirt, under a vest or jacket); the tallit gadol at Shacharit. Strings must be kosher and tied correctly. Looking at tzitzit during Shema fulfills "you shall see them." Women in some communities wear tzitzit; ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key: bein kodesh l'kodesh — Bein kodesh l'kodesh means "between holy and holy." In havdalah when Shabbat leads into Yom Tov,
pattern_issues.key_full: bein kodesh l'kodesh — Bein kodesh l'kodesh means "between holy and holy." In havdalah when Shabbat leads into Yom Tov, you declare that one level of holiness (Shabbat) ends as another (the festival) begins — not bein kodesh l'chol (holy to weekday). That wording matches the spiritual reality: Saturday night may be still sacred, but the nature of the day changes. Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh is also said after nightfall to permit Yom Tov melacha before Kiddush.

pattern_issues.key: Rosh Chodesh (the New Month) is the first day of each Hebrew month, and in some months the 30th day of the previous mont
pattern_issues.key_full:
Rosh Chodesh (the New Month) is the first day of each Hebrew month, and in some months the 30th day of the previous month as well.

Special observances include: Yaaleh V'Yavo in prayers and Grace After Meals, half Hallel, a Musaf (additional) prayer, and a special Torah reading.

Rosh Chodesh is a semi-holiday — certain fasting and eulogizing are restricted. There is a widespread custom for women to refrain from certain types of work as an extra mark of honor for the day.

pattern_issues.key: Matana al menat lehachzir (מַתָּנָה עַל מְנָת לְהַחְזִיר) is a "gift on condition it is returned." The recipient legally
pattern_issues.key_full: Matana al menat lehachzir (מַתָּנָה עַל מְנָת לְהַחְזִיר) is a "gift on condition it is returned." The recipient legally owns the item for the moment it is in their hands, then returns it afterward. On the first day of Sukkot, if you do not own a lulav and etrog, someone may gift you their set conditionally so you fulfill the Torah obligation of ownership for the mitzvah, then you return it. This is not used for mechirat chametz — selling chametz requires an absolute sale.

pattern_issues.key_full: Matana al menat lehachzir (מַתָּנָה עַל מְנָת לְהַחְזִיר) is a "gift on condition it is returned." The recipient legally owns the item for the moment it is in their hands, then returns it afterward. On the first day of Sukkot, if you do not own a lulav and etrog, someone may gift you their set conditionally so you fulfill the Torah obligation of ownership for the mitzvah, then you return it. This is not used for mechirat chametz — selling chametz requires an absolute sale.

pattern_issues.key_full: Discover the power of Divine protection! 🛡️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d turned Balaam's curses into blessings. Here's something amazing: A powerful sorcerer was hired to curse the Jewish people, but G-d made him speak beautiful blessings instead! Including the famous 'How goodly are your tents, Jacob' that we say every morning! The lesson? When others try to bring you down, G-d can transform their negativity into the biggest blessings!

pattern_issues.key: Kitniyot are legumes, rice, corn, and similar foods that Ashkenazim traditionally avoid on Pesach though they are not To
pattern_issues.key_full: Kitniyot are legumes, rice, corn, and similar foods that Ashkenazim traditionally avoid on Pesach though they are not Torah chametz — an Ashkenazi customary stringency, not a Torah prohibition. They are halachically permitted for Sephardim (though some communities have local customs avoiding specific items). The custom began to prevent confusion with chametz grains. Peanut oil, quinoa, and similar items follow widely different psak — follow your rav, not social media debates.

pattern_issues.key: Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement — fasting, confession, and prayer. Biblically called Shabbat Shabbaton, it carries th
pattern_issues.key_full: Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement — fasting, confession, and prayer. Biblically called Shabbat Shabbaton, it carries the exact same strict labor (melacha) prohibitions as Shabbat, meaning cooking and kindling fire are strictly forbidden (unlike Yom Tov, which permits ochel nefesh). Kol Nidrei opens the eve; Ne'ilah closes the day. The day atones with teshuvah, but serious sins between people still require apology and restitution. Prepare easy break-fast before the fast begins.

pattern_issues.key: tzitzit — Tzitzit are fringes on four-cornered garments reminding us of all 613 mitzvot. The tallit katan is worn daily
pattern_issues.key_full: tzitzit — Tzitzit are fringes on four-cornered garments reminding us of all 613 mitzvot. The tallit katan is worn daily — generally over or under the shirt, depending on community custom (many Chassidim, Sephardim, and followers of the Arizal wear it over the shirt, under a vest or jacket); the tallit gadol at Shacharit. Strings must be kosher and tied correctly. Looking at tzitzit during Shema fulfills "you shall see them." Women in some communities wear tzitzit; ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key: Today is the Fast of Esther (Taanit Esther) — commemorating the fasting Esther called before approaching the king (Esthe
pattern_issues.key_full:
Today is the Fast of Esther (Taanit Esther) — commemorating the fasting Esther called before approaching the king (Esther 4:16).

Minor fast rules with Purim context:
• No eating or drinking from dawn until nightfall.
• Music, showering for pleasure, and leather shoes are permitted — this is a minor fast, not like Tisha B'Av.
• Purim mitzvot begin tonight/tomorrow per your calendar (14 Adar, or 15 in walled cities). This fast is the spiritual preparation before the joy of Purim.
pattern_issues.snippet:

Reglas de ayuno menor con contexto de Purim:
• No comer ni beber desde el amanec

pattern_issues.key: Schach is the plant covering on the sukkah roof — bamboo, branches, or reeds — thick enough that shade exceeds sun, yet
pattern_issues.key_full: Schach is the plant covering on the sukkah roof — bamboo, branches, or reeds — thick enough that shade exceeds sun, yet sparse enough to see stars. It must be from material that grew from the ground and is not attached to the ground while on the sukkah. Finished manufactured items (furniture slats, crate boards, processed ropes) are invalid because they are susceptible to ritual impurity (kabalat tumah). Invalid schach invalidates the mitzvah — build with guidance the first time.

pattern_issues.key: matanot la'evyonim — Matanot la'evyonim are gifts sent to the poor on Purim day so they can rejoice too: at least one gi
pattern_issues.key_full: matanot la'evyonim — Matanot la'evyonim are gifts sent to the poor on Purim day so they can rejoice too: at least one gift to each of two different poor people (minimum of two recipients total). Money is common; food or vouchers work if they can use them that day. Unlike mishloach manot, this mitzvah focuses on need, not friendship. Many shuls collect on Purim morning; verify distribution happens on Purim itself (and on the same day you are celebrating it, i.e. on Shushan Purim).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about lo talin — the prohibition against withholding a worker's wages past their deadline — and how tight the time
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about lo talin — the prohibition against withholding a worker's wages past their deadline — and how tight the time limits actually are! ⏱️ 'The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you overnight until the morning' (Leviticus 19:13). 'Give him his wages on that day, before the sun sets' (Deuteronomy 24:15). These two verses establish both a positive commandment (pay on time) and a negative prohibition (don't delay payment)! The consequences could be dire! Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Master the art of loving rebuke! 💝 The Torah gives us an amazing tool: If you see someone doing something wrong, don't j
pattern_issues.key_full: Master the art of loving rebuke! 💝 The Torah gives us an amazing tool: If you see someone doing something wrong, don't just stay quiet or gossip about it - help them grow! Here's the key: Speak privately, gently, and make it clear you're coming from a place of love. Did you know? The word for rebuke (תוכחה) is related to the word for proof (הוכחה), teaching us to help others see proof of a better way! Today's challenge: If you need to give feedback, do it with kindness and wisdom.

pattern_issues.key: Challah is the bread often prepared for Shabbat and festival meals — often braided and covered until Kiddush. Two loaves
pattern_issues.key_full: Challah is the bread often prepared for Shabbat and festival meals — often braided and covered until Kiddush. Two loaves (lechem mishneh) recall the double portion in the desert. On Friday night, if wine or grape juice is completely unavailable, Kiddush can be recited over the challah loaves, provided one washes hands for bread immediately before reciting Kiddush and replaces the wine blessing with Hamotzi. Bread can never substitute for daytime Shabbat Kiddush on Saturday morning.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about saying the Shema! Good news - you can say it standing, walking, or even while riding your trusty donkey (or
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about saying the Shema! Good news - you can say it standing, walking, or even while riding your trusty donkey (or car, in modern times 😉). Just don't try saying it while lying flat on your back or face-down - that's a no-go. Side-lying is perfectly fine though! And if you're not feeling well or can't turn to your side? No worries - just lean a bit to the side and you're good to go. The key is to say it in a way that shows respect while still being practical for your situation.

pattern_issues.key: The Fast of Gedaliah is observed on 3 Tishrei (pushed to 4 Tishrei when 3 Tishrei falls on Shabbat). It commemorates the
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Fast of Gedaliah is observed on 3 Tishrei (pushed to 4 Tishrei when 3 Tishrei falls on Shabbat). It commemorates the assassination of Gedaliah ben Ahikam, the Jewish governor appointed by Babylonia to administer the land after the destruction of the First Temple.

His death marked the end of the last vestiges of Jewish autonomy in the Land of Israel following the first exile.

It is a minor fast — from dawn until nightfall (not a full 25-hour fast like Yom Kippur or Tisha B'Av).

pattern_issues.key_full: Unlock the power of Hebrew letters! 📚 Today's special letter: Bet (ב) - the first letter of the Torah! Did you know? Invalid schach invalidates the mitzvah — build with guidance the first time.

pattern_issues.key: Yom Kippur — Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement — fasting, confession, and prayer. Biblically called Shabbat Shabbaton,
pattern_issues.key_full: Yom Kippur — Yom Kippur is the Day of Atonement — fasting, confession, and prayer. Biblically called Shabbat Shabbaton, it carries the exact same strict labor (melacha) prohibitions as Shabbat, meaning cooking and kindling fire are strictly forbidden (unlike Yom Tov, which permits ochel nefesh). Kol Nidrei opens the eve; Ne'ilah closes the day. The day atones with teshuvah, but serious sins between people still require apology and restitution. Prepare easy break-fast before the fast begins.

pattern_issues.key: Kiddush b'Makom Seudah — Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine
pattern_issues.key_full: Kiddush b'Makom Seudah — Kiddush b'Makom Seudah means sanctifying Shabbat or Yom Tov, typically with blessings over wine, in the same place you eat a meal afterwards. You would not fulfill Kiddush if you recited it and left without eating there. On Shabbat night, the meal should include bread (hamotzi) per most poskim. On Yom Tov or daytime Kiddush, other foods may qualify in some cases; ask your rav. If you make Kiddush in one room, the meal should follow there and not in a different space.

pattern_issues.key: Learn from history's mistakes! 🏜️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how even after seeing inc
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn from history's mistakes! 🏜️ Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how even after seeing incredible miracles in the desert, our ancestors sometimes lost faith and complained. The biggest mistake? Building a golden calf just 40 days after hearing G-d's voice! Here's the powerful lesson: Even if we make big mistakes, G-d always gives us a chance to return and do better. When you're feeling down about a spiritual slip-up, remember: it's never too late to start fresh!

pattern_issues.key: challah — Challah is the bread often prepared for Shabbat and festival meals — often braided and covered until Kiddush.
pattern_issues.key_full: challah — Challah is the bread often prepared for Shabbat and festival meals — often braided and covered until Kiddush. Two loaves (lechem mishneh) recall the double portion in the desert. On Friday night, if wine or grape juice is completely unavailable, Kiddush can be recited over the challah loaves, provided one washes hands for bread immediately before reciting Kiddush and replaces the wine blessing with Hamotzi. Bread can never substitute for daytime Shabbat Kiddush on Saturday morning.

When (Southern Hemisphere — your location):
The Shulchan Arukh places this blessing in Nissan, when spring arrives in Is
pattern_issues.key_full:
When (Southern Hemisphere — your location):
The Shulchan Arukh places this blessing in Nissan, when spring arrives in Israel. Mainstream poskim rule that the blessing follows the natural blossoming of fruit trees in your locale — not the calendar month alone (Yalkut Yosef; Peninei Halakha). In Australia, South America, southern Africa, and similar regions, local spring falls in Elul and Tishrei (typically September–October). Say it once during that window, as soon as you see the first blossoms.

pattern_issues.key: Birchat Hamapil (Hamapil) is the blessing before sleep entrusting the soul to G-d. Say it as the last words before closi
pattern_issues.key_full: Birchat Hamapil (Hamapil) is the blessing before sleep entrusting the soul to G-d. Say it as the last words before closing your eyes. After reciting it, do not eat, drink, or speak until sleep; quietly repeating familiar Shema or protection verses while dozing is permitted, but classical poskim discourage sitting up for new Torah study or lengthy Tehillim (Biur Halachah O.C. 239:1). Some omit it if they know they may not fall asleep for a long time. It pairs with Shema al hamitah for protection.

pattern_issues.key: Shabbat (the Sabbath) begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars are visible on Saturday night. It commemorate
pattern_issues.key_full:
Shabbat (the Sabbath) begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars are visible on Saturday night. It commemorates G-d resting on the seventh day of Creation and the Exodus from Egypt.

The day is marked by refraining from the 39 categories of creative labor (melachot), as well as by positive observances: candle lighting before sunset, Kiddush over wine, festive meals, prayer, Torah study, and Havdalah at the close of the day.

Shabbat is called a "sign" between G-d and the Jewish people.

The recipient legally owns the item for the moment it is in their hands, then returns it afterward. On the first day of Sukkot, if you do not own a lulav and etrog, someone may gift you their set conditionally so you fulfill the Torah obligation of ownership for the mitzvah, then you return it. This is not used for mechirat chametz — selling chametz requires an absolute sale.

Learn one law about this beautiful mitzvah!

Ideally finish before the Shabbat meal; if not, before Mincha on Shabbat; later fallback windows apply per O.C. 285:4 (some until Wednesday of the following week, some until Simchat Torah). Even one aliyah weekly is a start — consistency matters.

pattern_issues.key: Biur chametz is destroying chametz the morning after bedikat chametz — usually by burning before the fifth halachic hour
pattern_issues.key_full: Biur chametz is destroying chametz the morning after bedikat chametz — usually by burning before the fifth halachic hour of the day. The final Kol Chamira nullification must be completed before that same deadline; a late Kol Chamira after the 5th hour is invalid because chametz is then assur b'hana'ah. Whatever remains must be gone from your ownership. It completes the removal process together with mechirat chametz for sold items. Do not eat breakfast chametz that morning if your custom is to finish biur first.

pattern_issues.key: Biur chametz is destroying chametz the morning after bedikat chametz — usually by burning before the fifth halachic hour
pattern_issues.key_full: Biur chametz is destroying chametz the morning after bedikat chametz — usually by burning before the fifth halachic hour of the day. The final Kol Chamira nullification must be completed before that same deadline; a late Kol Chamira after the 5th hour is invalid because chametz is then assur b'hana'ah. Whatever remains must be gone from your ownership. It completes the removal process together with mechirat chametz for sold items. Do not eat breakfast chametz that morning if your custom is to finish biur first.

pattern_issues.key_full:
Selichot (סליחות) — penitential prayers — begin in Elul for Sefardim and Edot HaMizrach (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 581:1; Peninei Halakha, Laws of the Festivals 15-02-05).

How:
• Said at night (often after chatzos layla / before dawn) or early morning per community.
• Not on Rosh Chodesh Elul itself; then daily through Yom Kippur — follow your kehilla's schedule.
• Includes the 13 Attributes of Mercy (Hashem Hashem) with congregation responses.
• Prepare by checking local synagogue calendar — times shift through Elul.

pattern_issues.key: birchat hamapil — Birchat Hamapil (Hamapil) is the blessing before sleep entrusting the soul to G-d. Say it as the last
pattern_issues.key_full: birchat hamapil — Birchat Hamapil (Hamapil) is the blessing before sleep entrusting the soul to G-d. Say it as the last words before closing your eyes. After reciting it, do not eat, drink, or speak until sleep; quietly repeating familiar Shema or protection verses while dozing is permitted, but classical poskim discourage sitting up for new Torah study or lengthy Tehillim (Biur Halachah O.C. 239:1). Some omit it if they know they may not fall asleep for a long time. It pairs with Shema al hamitah for protection.

pattern_issues.key: Kashering makes utensils fit for kosher or Pesach use. The core principle is kebolo kach polto — as a vessel absorbed fo
pattern_issues.key_full: Kashering makes utensils fit for kosher or Pesach use. The core principle is kebolo kach polto — as a vessel absorbed forbidden taste, so it expels it. Items used in boiling water often need hag'alah (immersion in rolling boiling water); items used directly on fire may need libun (intense heat until they turn red hot). Plastic and glass have highly conflicting rulings between Ashkenazim and Sephardim — and it's less lenient on Pesach. Ask your rav. Ceramic cannot be kashered. Many families keep a separate Pesach set.

pattern_issues.key: Sephardi custom (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 493:1-2; Peninei Halakha 05-03-03): mourning from Pesach until the morning of the 3
pattern_issues.key_full:
Sephardi custom (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 493:1-2; Peninei Halakha 05-03-03): mourning from Pesach until the morning of the 34th day of the Omer (Lamed-Dalet). Music on Lag BaOmer in honor of R. Shimon bar Yochai is permitted, but weddings and haircuts remain restricted until the 34th morning per prevalent Sephardi psak (Rav Ovadia Yosef, Yechaveh Daat 3:31). Some communities (e.g. Turkey, Egypt) end mourning on Lag BaOmer — follow your kehilla.

Ask your rav which tradition you follow and when restrictions begin and end.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the incredible power of praying for everyone! 🙏 When you daven (pray) to Hashem for blessings, health, and p
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the incredible power of praying for everyone! 🙏 When you daven (pray) to Hashem for blessings, health, and parnassa (livelihood) or for anything good for all of Am Yisrael (the Jewish people) — not just for yourself — the Sages teach that your prayer is more likely to be answered! It’s like a spiritual win-win: you help yourself by helping everyone else. Plus, who doesn't want to live in a world where no one is stressed? So next time you need some blessings, broaden your scope and pray for the whole community!

pattern_issues.key: Lag BaOmer is the 33rd day of the Omer count (18 Iyar). It marks the yahrzeit (anniversary of passing) of Rabbi Shimon b
pattern_issues.key_full:
Lag BaOmer is the 33rd day of the Omer count (18 Iyar). It marks the yahrzeit (anniversary of passing) of Rabbi Shimon bar Yochai, the author of the Zohar, as well as a pause in the mourning period observed during the Omer.

The restrictions of the Omer period (no haircuts, no weddings, no music) are lifted on Lag BaOmer. In Israel, large bonfires are lit, especially in Meron at Rabbi Shimon's tomb.

It is also a day celebrated for the cessation of a plague that killed 24,000 students of Rabbi Akiva during the Omer period.

pattern_issues.key: Shehecheyanu thanks G-d who "has kept us alive, sustained us, and brought us to this time." It is said on the first nigh
pattern_issues.key_full: Shehecheyanu thanks G-d who "has kept us alive, sustained us, and brought us to this time." It is said on the first night of a festival, and when wearing important new clothes, eating a seasonal fruit for the first time since it came back into season, and many other joyous occasions. Do not recite it on the final day(s) of Pesach (7th in Israel, 8th in the Diaspora) — those days are considered part of the original festival. On Yaknehaz nights it comes at the end of the combined Kiddush–havdalah sequence for the new Yom Tov.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the priestly garments — the special uniforms the Kohanim (priests) would wear when they served G-d in the Be
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the priestly garments — the special uniforms the Kohanim (priests) would wear when they served G-d in the Beit Hamikdash. A regular Kohen wore a four-piece set, while the Kohen Gadol (High Priest) wore an epic eight-piece ensemble that included a golden head-plate and a breastplate embedded with twelve precious gemstones! Did you know? The rabbis teach that if so much as a single hair or a tiny insect intervened between these holy garments and the Kohen's skin, his service was invalid! Talk about separation anxiety!

pattern_issues.key: Lashon hara is speaking negatively about another Jew even when the information is true and even in casual conversation,
pattern_issues.key_full: Lashon hara is speaking negatively about another Jew even when the information is true and even in casual conversation, unless there's a real useful purpose like protecting someone from a harmful situation. The Torah compares it to murder because it destroys reputations, friendships, and community trust. The Chofetz Chaim systematized its laws: many situations permit speech for protection or constructive need, but for entertainment or no reason it's never permitted. Guarding speech is one of the fastest ways to grow in holiness.

pattern_issues.key: Erev Tisha B'Av (8 Av afternoon): stop learning Torah except sad topics; eat the final meal (seudah hamafseket) before t
pattern_issues.key_full:
Erev Tisha B'Av (8 Av afternoon): stop learning Torah except sad topics; eat the final meal (seudah hamafseket) before the fast. Tallit and tefillin are worn at Shacharit on Erev Tisha B'Av — the restriction applies on Tisha B'Av day itself.

Tisha B'Av (9 Av): full 25-hour fast; kinot at Shacharit without tallit and tefillin; don them at Mincha after halachic chatzos (use your zmanim app). Sit on low stools until chatzos on 9 Av.

Shabbat Chazon (the Shabbat before 9 Av): Shabbat is observed normally — meat and wine are permitted.

pattern_issues.key: Sefirat HaOmer counts forty-nine days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking freedom to receiving the T
pattern_issues.key_full: Sefirat HaOmer counts forty-nine days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking freedom to receiving the Torah. Each night, preferably after nightfall, you bless and announce the day and week count. Missing a full day may affect whether you can say the blessing the remaining nights — ask your rav. During the Omer, many communities observe mourning customs (no music, weddings, haircuts) until Lag BaOmer or Shavuot, depending on minhag to commemorate the plague that killed many of Rabbi Akiva's students during this time.

pattern_issues.key: Tashlich is a Rosh Hashana custom of casting breadcrumbs or symbolic sins into flowing water, reciting Micah 7:19. It is
pattern_issues.key_full: Tashlich is a Rosh Hashana custom of casting breadcrumbs or symbolic sins into flowing water, reciting Micah 7:19. It is not in the Talmud like shofar, but is widespread. When the first day of Rosh Hashana falls on Shabbat, tashlich is postponed to Sunday — a structural rule across mainstream Ashkenazi and Sephardic practice, not a local preference. The Sages enacted this to prevent carrying machzorim through a public domain without a kosher eruv. It is symbolic teshuvah — the real work is inward change and apology to people you hurt.

pattern_issues.key_full: Tashlich is a Rosh Hashana custom of casting breadcrumbs or symbolic sins into flowing water, reciting Micah 7:19. It is not in the Talmud like shofar, but is widespread. When the first day of Rosh Hashana falls on Shabbat, tashlich is postponed to Sunday — a structural rule across mainstream Ashkenazi and Sephardic practice, not a local preference. The Sages enacted this to prevent carrying machzorim through a public domain without a kosher eruv. It is symbolic teshuvah — the real work is inward change and apology to people you hurt.

pattern_issues.key_full: Do the mitzvah of Torah study! Here's a fun way to start - read this tiny but mighty verse: וּבְנֵי־דָ֖ן חֻשִֽׁים (U'vnei Dan Chusheem - Translation: And the sons of Dan: Chusheem). 📜 Fun fact: This is actually the shortest verse in the entire Torah! Even reading just one verse connects you to infinite wisdom. How cool is that? Also, Dan had only one son, Chusheem (and he happened to be deaf), but by the time the tribes were counted later on in the Torah, he had the second largest tribe! When you're blessed by G-d, anything is possible! ✡

pattern_issues.key: Shehecheyanu — Shehecheyanu thanks G-d who "has kept us alive, sustained us, and brought us to this time." It is said on
pattern_issues.key_full: Shehecheyanu — Shehecheyanu thanks G-d who "has kept us alive, sustained us, and brought us to this time." It is said on the first night of a festival, and when wearing important new clothes, eating a seasonal fruit for the first time since it came back into season, and many other joyous occasions. Do not recite it on the final day(s) of Pesach (7th in Israel, 8th in the Diaspora) — those days are considered part of the original festival. On Yaknehaz nights it comes at the end of the combined Kiddush–havdalah sequence for the new Yom Tov.

pattern_issues.key_full: Shnayim Mikra v'Echad Targum — Shnayim Mikra v'Echad Targum is completing the weekly Torah portion twice in Hebrew and once in translation — Targum Onkelos or Rashi's commentary, which many use as a substitute to better understand the plain meaning (Shulchan Arukh and Mishnah Berurah O.C. 285:2). Ideally finish before the Shabbat meal; if not, before Mincha on Shabbat; later fallback windows apply per O.C. 285:4 (some until Wednesday of the following week, some until Simchat Torah). Even one aliyah weekly is a start — consistency matters.

pattern_issues.key: Tu B'Av is the 15th of Av. It is described in the Talmud (Taanit 26b) as one of the two happiest days in the Jewish cale
pattern_issues.key_full:
Tu B'Av is the 15th of Av. It is described in the Talmud (Taanit 26b) as one of the two happiest days in the Jewish calendar (along with Yom Kippur).

Historically, it marked several positive events, including the end of the plague that killed those who accepted the report of the spies in the wilderness. In the era of the Temple, it was a day when unmarried women would dance in the vineyards.

Today, Tu B'Av is observed as a minor holiday of joy. Tachanun is not recited. Many communities treat it as an auspicious day for marriage and love.

pattern_issues.key: Be an Amen enthusiast! 🗣️ Did you know? Saying Amen to a blessing is confirming that what the first person said is true
pattern_issues.key_full: Be an Amen enthusiast! 🗣️ Did you know? Saying Amen to a blessing is confirming that what the first person said is true - the letters alef (א), mem (מ), nun (נ) are an acronym for G-d, King, who is faithful. Confirm that G-d is great and the sages say you steal the show! The Talmud says responding Amen is greater than making the blessing itself. Today's mission: Keep your ears open for blessings and be ready with your spiritual encore - don't miss an Amen! And remember, you're confirming that G-d is great and He does what He says He will do!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Musaf — the additional prayer said on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and Yom Tov! 🎺 The Musaf ('additional') Amidah
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Musaf — the additional prayer said on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and Yom Tov! 🎺 The Musaf ('additional') Amidah corresponds to the extra korban that was brought in the Temple on these special days. On a regular weekday, only the Tamid offering was brought. On Shabbat there was an ADDITIONAL offering of two lambs. On Rosh Hashanah, Rosh Chodesh, and other festival days there were numerous additional korbanot. Today we commemorate these with the Musaf Amidah, said after Shacharit. Each occasion has its own unique Musaf text. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: lashon hara — Lashon hara is speaking negatively about another Jew even when the information is true and even in casual
pattern_issues.key_full: lashon hara — Lashon hara is speaking negatively about another Jew even when the information is true and even in casual conversation, unless there's a real useful purpose like protecting someone from a harmful situation. The Torah compares it to murder because it destroys reputations, friendships, and community trust. The Chofetz Chaim systematized its laws: many situations permit speech for protection or constructive need, but for entertainment or no reason it's never permitted. Guarding speech is one of the fastest ways to grow in holiness.

A song of ascents. When the Lord returned the returnees to Zion, we were like dreamer
pattern_issues.key_full:
On days when tachanun is omitted:

A song of ascents. When the Lord returned the returnees to Zion, we were like dreamers. Then our mouths will be filled with laughter and our tongues with songs of praise. Then they will say among the nations, 'The Lord has done great things for them.' The Lord has done great things for us; we were joyful. Return, O Lord, our captivity like rivulets in arid land. Those who sow with tears will reap with song. He who goes along weeping, carrying the valuable seeds, will come back with song, carrying his sheaves.

pattern_issues.key: Chabad (Alter Rebbe / Arizal): haircut and shaving restrictions continue the entire 49 days through Erev Shavuot — adult
pattern_issues.key_full:
Chabad (Alter Rebbe / Arizal): haircut and shaving restrictions continue the entire 49 days through Erev Shavuot — adults do not take haircuts on Lag BaOmer (the sole exception is upsherin for a 3-year-old boy). Lag BaOmer is a day of intense joy with music, bonfires, and celebration, but haircut restrictions remain until Shavuot.

Music is generally avoided through Shavuot per Chabad practice, with Lag BaOmer as a day without music restrictions. Weddings follow your Chabad rabbi's guidance.

Ask your Chabad rabbi for details on your community.

pattern_issues.key: Step into the story of the Exodus: Learn about Maggid (the Seder storytelling) and the Rambam's incredible ruling! 📖 The
pattern_issues.key_full: Step into the story of the Exodus: Learn about Maggid (the Seder storytelling) and the Rambam's incredible ruling! 📖 The Torah commands, 'And you shall tell your children on that day, saying: This is because of what Hashem did *for me* when I came out of Egypt' (Exodus 13:8). The Rambam (Hilchot Chametz U'Matzah 7:6) takes this literally: 'Every person is obligated to see themselves *as if* they personally came out of Egyptian slavery!' It's not just a history lesson about our ancestors; it's a simulated experience every seder night. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Tachanun at Shacharit (weekdays): After the morning Amidah — longer on Mondays and Thursdays (Vehu Rachum and the full p
pattern_issues.key_full:
Tachanun at Shacharit (weekdays): After the morning Amidah — longer on Mondays and Thursdays (Vehu Rachum and the full penitential section), shorter on other weekdays. Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, festivals, Chanukah, and other days your siddur lists.

Tachanun at Mincha: Some Ashkenaz communities especially emphasize saying Tachanun at Mincha if it was skipped at Shacharit that day. On a normal weekday, Tachanun at Mincha is already part of the standard service (often the shorter form). Omitted on the same festive days — Mincha itself is still said.

pattern_issues.key: Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory; the daytim
pattern_issues.key_full: Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory; the daytime reading is the primary fulfillment (mitzvat ha'yom), while the night reading was instituted later. Hearing every word is the mitzvah; noise at Haman's name is custom. Shehecheyanu: Ashkenazim recite before the first evening and first daytime readings (Rema O.C. 692:1); Sephardim recite only at night — the night blessing covers the day (Shulchan Arukh; Yalkut Yosef). If you miss words, ask whether you must hear that section again.

pattern_issues.key: Sefirat HaOmer — Sefirat HaOmer counts forty-nine days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking freedom t
pattern_issues.key_full: Sefirat HaOmer — Sefirat HaOmer counts forty-nine days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking freedom to receiving the Torah. Each night, preferably after nightfall, you bless and announce the day and week count. Missing a full day may affect whether you can say the blessing the remaining nights — ask your rav. During the Omer, many communities observe mourning customs (no music, weddings, haircuts) until Lag BaOmer or Shavuot, depending on minhag to commemorate the plague that killed many of Rabbi Akiva's students during this time.

pattern_issues.key: Strengthen your trust in G-d! Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d provided manna - mira
pattern_issues.key_full: Strengthen your trust in G-d! Take a moment to remember (one of the 10 daily remembrances) how G-d provided manna - miraculous food that fell from heaven - every single day in the desert. Here's something amazing: Each person got exactly what they needed - no more, no less! And it tasted like whatever they wanted! The lesson? Just like G-d provided then, He provides for us now, though often in less obvious ways. When worried about livelihood, remember: G-d has infinite ways to provide for us! Don't turn to not-kosher avenues of attaining livelihood!

pattern_issues.key_full: Ever wonder about some wild Torah laws? 🔮 Check this out: The Torah talks about something called 'Ov' - where people would burn incense, wave myrtle branches, and try to hear voices from... skulls! (Yes, you read that right!) The Rambam explains that someone doing this would hear voices seemingly coming from under the ground or even their armpits! While this sounds like something from a spooky movie, it shows how the Torah protects us from harmful spiritual practices. Today's mission: Appreciate how Torah guides us to connect with G-d in proper ways.

Ashkenazi custom observes a longer, stricter mourning throughout the Three Weeks, intensifying dur
pattern_issues.key_full:
${threeWeeksIntro()}

Ashkenazi custom observes a longer, stricter mourning throughout the Three Weeks, intensifying during the Nine Days.

From 17 Tammuz (general Three Weeks):
• Haircuts & shaving: prohibited for the entire Three Weeks.
• Music: instrumental music is not listened to throughout the period.
• Weddings: not held.
• Shehecheyanu: traditionally not recited on new clothes or new fruits; permitted on Shabbat.

From 1 Av (Nine Days): restrictions intensify — see the Nine Days checklist item for meat, wine, laundry, bathing, and home practices.

De 17 Tamuz (general Tres Semanas):
• Cortes de pelo

pattern_issues.key_full: **Super-Special Mitzvah**: Light an oil candle (such as a floating wick in a small cup of oil) for the elevation of the souls of the "Neshamot Hagalmudot" (הנשמות הגלמודות) -the forlorn departed souls in Heaven who need just a small merit to get into the Garden of Eden (Gan Eden). Rabbis say these poor souls are just missing a small merit to be let into the eternal pleasure of Gan Eden,  and if you light a candle in their merit, you could help them get in! Then these souls are extremely grateful, and once they're in they can pray for you for all your wishes!

pattern_issues.key: Take a Shabbat prep course! Did you know that preparing for Shabbat is itself a special mitzvah? Halacha teaches us that
pattern_issues.key_full: Take a Shabbat prep course! Did you know that preparing for Shabbat is itself a special mitzvah? Halacha teaches us that the actions we take before Shabbat are imbued with special holiness. Pick one prep mission today: maybe setting the table beautifully, cooking a special dish, finding special Shabbat clothes, or tidying your home. Each act, done with the intention of honoring Shabbat, transforms mundane tasks into mitzvot. It's like building a spiritual ramp to welcome the Queen Shabbat! Source: Rambam, Hilchot Shabbat 30:1; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 250.

pattern_issues.key: Megillah — Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory;
pattern_issues.key_full: Megillah — Megillah means scroll; on Purim it is Esther's book read twice — night and day. Both readings are obligatory; the daytime reading is the primary fulfillment (mitzvat ha'yom), while the night reading was instituted later. Hearing every word is the mitzvah; noise at Haman's name is custom. Shehecheyanu: Ashkenazim recite before the first evening and first daytime readings (Rema O.C. 692:1); Sephardim recite only at night — the night blessing covers the day (Shulchan Arukh; Yalkut Yosef). If you miss words, ask whether you must hear that section again.

pattern_issues.key: Experience the power of Mezuzah! 🏠 The Rambam explains that every time you pass a Mezuzah, it reminds you of G-d's prese
pattern_issues.key_full: Experience the power of Mezuzah! 🏠 The Rambam explains that every time you pass a Mezuzah, it reminds you of G-d's presence and awakens you from spiritual sleep! The word 'mezuzah' itself has a numerical value of 65 in gematria, which is also the value of the word 'hekhal,' meaning 'chamber' or 'palace.' This connection suggests that the mezuzah transforms a house into a miniature palace, a dwelling place for the Divine Presence. Additionally, the text of the mezuzah contains 713 letters, which is the numerical value of the word 'teshuvah,' meaning 'repentance.'

Longer on Mondays and Thursdays; shorter on other weekdays. Posture and exact wording vary by kehilla (Iraqi, Syrian, Moroccan, etc.) — follow your siddur. Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, festivals, Chanukah, and other days in your siddur.

pattern_issues.key: Understand true and false prophecy! 🔮 The Rambam reveals something fascinating in Chapter 10: If a prophet predicts some
pattern_issues.key_full: Understand true and false prophecy! 🔮 The Rambam reveals something fascinating in Chapter 10: If a prophet predicts something bad will happen and it doesn't - that doesn't make them false! Why? Because G-d is merciful and might cancel the decree if people repent, just like with Nineveh. But here's the key: If a prophet predicts something good and it doesn't happen, they're definitely false! Because G-d never cancels good prophecies (except once, during the First Temple's destruction). Today's mission: Remember that G-d's mercy can always override negative decrees.

In the Diaspora it fal
pattern_issues.key_full:
Simchat Torah celebrates the completion and immediate restart of the annual Torah reading cycle.

In the Diaspora it falls on 23 Tishrei (day after Shemini Atzeret). In Israel it is observed together with Shemini Atzeret on 22 Tishrei.

Customs:
• Hakafot — joyous processional circuits with Torah scrolls, singing and dancing
• Final parsha V'Zot HaBeracha read; Bereishit begun again
• Festive Kiddush and meals; Yizkor in many Ashkenaz communities on Shemini Atzeret/Simchat Torah

It is a full Yom Tov in the Diaspora; in Israel it shares the day with Shemini Atzeret.

pattern_issues.key: Eating matzah on Pesach after the Seder night(s) is a mitzvah many observe, though not with the same strict obligation a
pattern_issues.key_full:
Eating matzah on Pesach after the Seder night(s) is a mitzvah many observe, though not with the same strict obligation as the first Seder night(s).

Levels:
• First Seder night: Torah obligation (k'zayit) for men and women.
• Rest of Pesach including Chol HaMoed: Rabbinic command (if you have a second seder) and optional mitzvah to eat matzah every day of Pesach. Most will eat matzah at all Yom Tov and Shabbat meals.

How:
• Use shmurah matzah for the seder if available.
• A kezayit per meal is sufficient.
• No chametz or kitniyot (per your custom) the entire Pesach.

pattern_issues.key: Torah study (Talmud Torah) means setting aside time to learn G-d's teaching — Chumash, Tanach, Mishnah, Talmud, halacha,
pattern_issues.key_full: Torah study (Talmud Torah) means setting aside time to learn G-d's teaching — Chumash, Tanach, Mishnah, Talmud, halacha, or musar. The Mishnah says it outweighs all other mitzvot combined (Peah 1:1). Pirkei Avot teaches that the Shechinah dwells when words of Torah are spoken (Avot 3:2–3); the Talmud lists Torah among the three protectors of life (Sotah 21a). Tradition describes words of Torah ascending as merit and advocacy on high. Men and women have different scopes of obligation, but every Jew who learns bonds with Hashem. Even a few focused minutes daily counts.
pattern_issues.snippet: El estudio de la Torá (Talmud Torá) significa dejar de lado el tiempo para aprender la enseñanza de D-os: Jumash, Tanaj, Mishná, Talmud, halajá o musar. La Mishná dice que supera todos los otros mitzv

pattern_issues.key_full:
Purim Meshulash — matanot la'evyonim on Friday only (14 Adar)

Today (Friday daytime) — not on Shabbat:
• Give at least one gift to each of two different poor people (minimum of two recipients total).
• Each gift should enable a modest Purim meal (money is common; amounts vary by community).
• Many give after the daytime Megillah reading.

You may use a trustworthy messenger or organization that distributes today. If you cannot find recipients, ask your rabbi or shul Friday morning.

Sunday is for mishloach manot and the seudah — those should already be prepared before Shabbat.

pattern_issues.key: Torah study — Torah study (Talmud Torah) means setting aside time to learn G-d's teaching — Chumash, Tanach, Mishnah, Ta
pattern_issues.key_full: Torah study — Torah study (Talmud Torah) means setting aside time to learn G-d's teaching — Chumash, Tanach, Mishnah, Talmud, halacha, or musar. The Mishnah says it outweighs all other mitzvot combined (Peah 1:1). Pirkei Avot teaches that the Shechinah dwells when words of Torah are spoken (Avot 3:2–3); the Talmud lists Torah among the three protectors of life (Sotah 21a). Tradition describes words of Torah ascending as merit and advocacy on high. Men and women have different scopes of obligation, but every Jew who learns bonds with Hashem. Even a few focused minutes daily counts.

pattern_issues.key: Havdalah (separation) is the ceremony performed after Shabbat ends — Saturday night when three stars appear — marking th
pattern_issues.key_full:
Havdalah (separation) is the ceremony performed after Shabbat ends — Saturday night when three stars appear — marking the transition back to the weekdays.

The ceremony has four blessings: wine, fragrant spices (besamim), fire, and the Havdalah blessing (Hamavdil). The spices comfort the soul for the departure of the extra Shabbat soul (neshama yeteira).

Important: you may not perform any melachah (Shabbat-forbidden labor) after Shabbat ends until you have heard Havdalah or said the phrase "Baruch HaMavdil bein Kodesh l'chol" (Blessed is He Who separates the holy from the mundane).

pattern_issues.key: Tashlumin is making up a missed Amidah at the very next service: missed Shacharit at Mincha, missed Mincha at Maariv, mi
pattern_issues.key_full: Tashlumin is making up a missed Amidah at the very next service: missed Shacharit at Mincha, missed Mincha at Maariv, missed Maariv at tomorrow's Shacharit. Pray the current Amidah first, pause, then the makeup. If you accidentally say the makeup first, that Amidah counts as the current obligation and the second counts as tashlumin — no third Amidah needed (Mishnah Berurah 108:2). This applies only if you missed the prayer by accident or unavoidable delay — if you missed intentionally, there is no tashlumin. Missed holiday Shacharit is made up at Mincha, not at Musaf (SA O.C. 108:9).

pattern_issues.key_full: Relive the most epic moment in history! 🗻 Take a moment to remember when we stood at Mount Sinai (one of the 10 daily remembrances). Picture it: The entire mountain was on fire, there was thunder and lightning, and then... complete silence as we heard G-d's voice! Did you know? Our souls actually left our bodies when we heard G-d speak - it was too intense! After the first two commandments, we asked Moshe to be our intermediary. Here's something amazing: The rabbis teach that your soul was there too, and remembering this moment might very well reawaken that incredible spiritual connection!

pattern_issues.key: Honor the departed properly! 🕯️ The Rambam teaches in Chapter 11 that we shouldn't attempt to communicate with the dead
pattern_issues.key_full: Honor the departed properly! 🕯️ The Rambam teaches in Chapter 11 that we shouldn't attempt to communicate with the dead to ask them for information. In the book of Samuel, the king of Israel, Shaul, attempted to communicate with the departed prophet Samuel. The spirit of Samuel appeared to him and rebuked him for this transgression, and well, let's just say it was downhill from there for Shaul. This teaches us that 1. There is an afterlife, 2. We should not ask the spirits there for information, and 3. If you're trying to get advice, just call your mother—she's waiting for you to call anyway!

pattern_issues.key: Purim is observed on 14 Adar (15 Adar in walled cities — Shushan Purim). It commemorates the miraculous salvation of the
pattern_issues.key_full:
Purim is observed on 14 Adar (15 Adar in walled cities — Shushan Purim). It commemorates the miraculous salvation of the Jewish people in Persia as recorded in Megillat Esther.

Four mitzvot of the day:
• Mikra Megillah — hearing Megillat Esther read aloud twice (night and day)
• Mishloach Manot — sending a food gift of at least two ready-to-eat foods to at least one friend
• Matanot L'evyonim — giving charity to at least two poor people
• Mishteh — having a festive Purim meal

It is also customary to dress in costumes. Purim is not a Yom Tov — work is not formally forbidden, though many abstain.
pattern_issues.snippet:

pattern_issues.key: Learn the #1 Constant Mitzvah! Did you know there are six special mitzvot you can fulfill every single moment, no matter
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn the #1 Constant Mitzvah! Did you know there are six special mitzvot you can fulfill every single moment, no matter where you are or what you're doing? The very first is to know that Hashem (God) exists! This isn't just believing; it's about recognizing and internalizing that God is the Creator of everything and is actively involved in sustaining and guiding the entire world, right now! Take a moment, look around you, and marvel at the intricate design and vastness of the universe, and reflect on the miracle of your own existence. This constant awareness deepens your relationship with the Creator.

pattern_issues.key: Ashkenaz Selichot begin on Motzei Shabbat before Rosh Hashana, with at least four days of Selichot before the holiday (P
pattern_issues.key_full:
Ashkenaz Selichot begin on Motzei Shabbat before Rosh Hashana, with at least four days of Selichot before the holiday (Peninei Halakha 15-02-05; Rama). If Rosh Hashana falls on Monday or Tuesday, Selichot begin the previous Motzei Shabbat.

How:
• First night is often a large community gathering (Motzei Shabbat).
• Said standing; many wear tallit if already worn for Maariv or at morning Selichot.
• Arrive early — services are long and moving.
• Continue daily until Yom Kippur (including Erev Rosh Hashana, fast day schedules differ).
• Check your shul for exact times (usually late night or early morning).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about tzedakah priorities — Jewish law has a specific order for who gets your charity money first! 🎯 The Shulchan
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about tzedakah priorities — Jewish law has a specific order for who gets your charity money first! 🎯 The Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh Deah 251) establishes a clear priority order: (1) Your OWN basic needs come first — a person is not required to make themselves destitute to give tzedakah. You must maintain your basic living standards. (2) YOUR HOUSEHOLD — spouse, children, parents dependent on you. (3) RELATIVES who are poor — closer relatives take priority over distant ones. (4) Your CITY or community — the poor of your city before the poor of another city. (5) Israeli poor before the poor of other countries.

pattern_issues.key: Psalm 27 (לְדָוִד ה' אוֹרִי וְיִשְׁעִי) is added to Shacharit during Elul and Tishrei — a season of drawing close to G-d
pattern_issues.key_full:
Psalm 27 (לְדָוִד ה' אוֹרִי וְיִשְׁעִי) is added to Shacharit during Elul and Tishrei — a season of drawing close to G-d before and through the Days of Awe.

When:
• Said after the morning service (often after Shacharit Amidah, before concluding prayers — follow your siddur).
• Start and end dates vary by minhag — this item follows your nusach setting.

Why:
• "The L-rd is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" (Tehillim 27:1) — trust in divine protection through judgment season.
• A widespread custom in Elul–Tishrei; widely adopted across Ashkenaz, Sephard, and Edot HaMizrach with different calendars.
pattern_issues.snippet:

pattern_issues.key: Psalm 27 (לְדָוִד ה' אוֹרִי וְיִשְׁעִי) is added to Shacharit during Elul and Tishrei — a season of drawing close to G-d
pattern_issues.key_full:
Psalm 27 (לְדָוִד ה' אוֹרִי וְיִשְׁעִי) is added to Shacharit during Elul and Tishrei — a season of drawing close to G-d before and through the Days of Awe.

When:
• Said after the morning service (often after Shacharit Amidah, before concluding prayers — follow your siddur).
• Start and end dates vary by minhag — this item follows your nusach setting.

Why:
• "The L-rd is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" (Tehillim 27:1) — trust in divine protection through judgment season.
• A widespread custom in Elul–Tishrei; widely adopted across Ashkenaz, Sephard, and Edot HaMizrach with different calendars.
pattern_issues.snippet:
Le psaume 27 est ajouté à Shacharit pendant Eloul et Tishri — saison de rapprochement de D. avant et pendant les Yamim Noraim.

Quand : après le service du matin (souvent après l'Amidah de Shacharit).

pattern_issues.key: Chabad follows the standard Ashkenazi Selichot calendar — beginning on Motzei Shabbat before Rosh Hashana (or the prior
pattern_issues.key_full:
Chabad follows the standard Ashkenazi Selichot calendar — beginning on Motzei Shabbat before Rosh Hashana (or the prior Motzei Shabbat when Rosh Hashana falls early in the week), not on Rosh Chodesh Elul.

During Elul before Selichot begin:
• Blow the shofar daily after Shacharit (except Erev Rosh Hashana and when Rosh Hashana is Shabbat).
• Recite three extra chapters of Tehillim after Shacharit (Baal Shem Tov custom).

When Selichot begin:
• Follow your local Chabad minyan — often late night or before Shacharit.
• Use Nusach Ari for Selichot.
• Increase in Torah, teshuvah, and tzedakah through the month of Elul.

pattern_issues.key_full:
After Yom Kippur ends at nightfall${tzeit?.let { " (approx. $it tonight)" } ?: ""}, it is a mitzvah to eat a proper meal — not only a quick bite.

What to do:
• Complete Maariv and Havdalah (Havdalah includes a candle that was lit throughout Yom Kippur, wine, and no spices).
• Eat a festive break-fast — many prepare food in advance because cooking restrictions on Yom Kippur end at nightfall.
• Chabad tradition (Baal HaTanya) teaches that one's livelihood (parnassa) for the year is especially connected to this meal — eat with joy and spiritual intention.

This item becomes available at nightfall after the fast ends.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about ona'ah — the Torah prohibition against price fraud — and the fascinating 1/6 rule that determines exactly wh
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about ona'ah — the Torah prohibition against price fraud — and the fascinating 1/6 rule that determines exactly when it kicks in! 💼 The Torah says 'Do not wrong one another' (Leviticus 25:17). The Sages specify this applies to financial transactions: if you sell something at a price MORE than 1/6 above its market value, or buy it MORE than 1/6 BELOW market value — that's ona'ah, and the transaction can halachically be reversed. If something is very difficult to obtain in a certain area of the world, then the person selling it has more free rein to set prices, since they effectively set the price for that region!

pattern_issues.key: Yom Tov (literally "good day") refers to the major Jewish festivals: Pesach, Shavuot, Rosh Hashana, Sukkot, and Shemini
pattern_issues.key_full:
Yom Tov (literally "good day") refers to the major Jewish festivals: Pesach, Shavuot, Rosh Hashana, Sukkot, and Shemini Atzeret/Simchat Torah. Yom Kippur has its own distinct laws closer to Shabbat.

Yom Tov shares most of Shabbat's restrictions, but three key differences are permitted: cooking and baking for that day's needs, kindling a flame from a pre-existing flame (not striking a new one), and carrying between domains for the sake of Yom Tov needs.

All other creative labors forbidden on Shabbat remain forbidden on Yom Tov. The many details of Yom Tov law are complex and vary by situation. Always consult your rabbi.

pattern_issues.key:
Who must fast:
Jewish adults from bar/bat mitzvah age who are healthy enough to fast. Children are trained gradually — a
pattern_issues.key_full:
Who must fast:
Jewish adults from bar/bat mitzvah age who are healthy enough to fast. Children are trained gradually — ask your rav. Women who are pregnant, nursing, or recently gave birth, and anyone who is ill, should ask a posek — many eat in measured amounts (shiurim) or are fully exempt.

If you ate by mistake:
If you forgot and ate or drank unintentionally, you may continue fasting once you remember — the fast remains valid (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 568:1).

If you cannot fast:
Do not endanger your health. Ask a rav about shiurim (small amounts at intervals), postponing the fast, or exemption. Pikuach nefesh overrides fasting.

pattern_issues.key: Mincha (Edot HaMizrach): The service opens with Korbanot (Parshat HaTamid), then Ashrei, the Amidah, Tachanun on weekday
pattern_issues.key_full:
Mincha (Edot HaMizrach): The service opens with Korbanot (Parshat HaTamid), then Ashrei, the Amidah, Tachanun on weekdays, and Aleinu — per your kehilla's siddur.

Tachanun (Edot HaMizrach): At Shacharit on weekdays — longer on Mondays and Thursdays; shorter on other days (Vidui and the Thirteen Attributes per your siddur). At Mincha, recited after the Amidah including Vidui (Ashamnu) and the Thirteen Attributes. Following the Arizal (per Kaf HaChaim and Yalkut Yosef), do not perform nefilat apayim at Mincha — recite sitting upright, including Psalm 25 (LeDavid Alecha). Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, holidays, and standard festive days.

pattern_issues.key: Tu B'Shvat (the 15th of Shvat) is the New Year for Trees — one of the four "new years" mentioned in the Mishnah. It mark
pattern_issues.key_full:
Tu B'Shvat (the 15th of Shvat) is the New Year for Trees — one of the four "new years" mentioned in the Mishnah. It marks the date used for calculating the age of fruit trees for tithing purposes.

Tu B'Shvat is not a fast day, and work is permitted. It is customary to eat fruits of Israel, especially the seven species: wheat, barley, grapes, figs, pomegranates, olives, and dates. Many communities hold a "Tu B'Shvat seder" with fruits and wine.

In Kabbalistic tradition (particularly from the 16th-century Safed mystics), Tu B'Shvat became associated with the spiritual rectification of the world through eating fruits with intention.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the melacha of Zorea — planting on Shabbat! 🌱 This isn't just about dropping seeds in soil. The melacha incl
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the melacha of Zorea — planting on Shabbat! 🌱 This isn't just about dropping seeds in soil. The melacha includes any action that promotes or sustains plant growth: from grafting branches to simply watering your garden. In the Mishkan, this was vital for growing the grains and dye-herbs needed for the sacred space. Because watering actively fosters growth, it is completely forbidden on Shabbat—meaning even indoor houseplants cannot be watered, no matter how dry the soil looks. We water them beforehand and step back. The deeper lesson: Shabbat is about letting the world be, not pushing it to produce on our schedule. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key:
Erev Pesach (14 Nissan) is the day of final Pesach preparation before the Seder.

Key preparations:
• Finish eating cham
pattern_issues.key_full:
Erev Pesach (14 Nissan) is the day of final Pesach preparation before the Seder.

Key preparations:
• Finish eating chametz by the end of the morning (check your local zmanim).
• Bedikat chametz — search for chametz by candlelight after nightfall the night before (or according to your community's schedule).
• Biur chametz — destroy remaining chametz the next morning.
• Sell chametz through your rabbi if needed.
• Prepare the Seder plate, matzah, wine, and Haggadot for the first night(s).

The Seder is held after nightfall on 15 Nissan (and the second night in the Diaspora). No chametz may be eaten after the morning deadline on Erev Pesach.

pattern_issues.key: At its core, a mitzvah is a command from Hashem that acts as a direct connection point between you and Him. The word its
pattern_issues.key_full: At its core, a mitzvah is a command from Hashem that acts as a direct connection point between you and Him. The word itself is related to a term for "binding together," meaning every time you do a mitzvah, you are plugging yourself directly into the Divine. It covers the big stuff like Torah obligations and formal prayer, but it also includes the everyday moments—like making a blessing over a glass of wine or just having a candid, heart-to-heart talk with Hashem in your own words. Ultimately, a mitzvah is about taking an ordinary, physical moment and turning it into a real relationship with your Creator by following what He wants you to do.

pattern_issues.key: Shavuot is observed on 6 Sivan (two days in the Diaspora). It celebrates the giving of the Torah at Mount Sinai, which t
pattern_issues.key_full:
Shavuot is observed on 6 Sivan (two days in the Diaspora). It celebrates the giving of the Torah at Mount Sinai, which took place seven weeks after the Exodus.

It is customary to:
• Study Torah through the night (Tikkun Leil Shavuot)
• Eat dairy foods — various reasons are given, including that the Jewish people had not yet received the Torah's laws of meat preparation
• Read Megillat Ruth, whose themes of Torah commitment echo the acceptance of the Torah
• Hear the Ten Commandments read in synagogue

Shavuot comes exactly 50 days after the second night of Pesach, at the end of the Sefirat HaOmer (Counting of the Omer). It is a full Yom Tov.

pattern_issues.key: Simchat Yom Tov (שִׂמְחַת יוֹם טוֹב) is the Torah mitzvah "V'samachta b'chagecha" — rejoice in your festival (Devarim 16
pattern_issues.key_full: Simchat Yom Tov (שִׂמְחַת יוֹם טוֹב) is the Torah mitzvah "V'samachta b'chagecha" — rejoice in your festival (Devarim 16:14). The head of household facilitates joy for each family member in the way that brings them happiness (Pesachim 109a; SA OC 529:2): wife — new clothing or jewelry l'fi mamono (within means); children — treats (today candies and toys; Talmud: grains and nuts); men — festive meat and wine meals. Halacha requires including the poor — joy only among yourselves without helping the needy is "joy of one's stomach," not mitzvah joy. At the least it's recommended to provide the poor with tzedaka so they can enjoy the holiday as well.

pattern_issues.key_full: mitzvah — At its core, a mitzvah is a command from Hashem that acts as a direct connection point between you and Him. The word itself is related to a term for "binding together," meaning every time you do a mitzvah, you are plugging yourself directly into the Divine. It covers the big stuff like Torah obligations and formal prayer, but it also includes the everyday moments—like making a blessing over a glass of wine or just having a candid, heart-to-heart talk with Hashem in your own words. Ultimately, a mitzvah is about taking an ordinary, physical moment and turning it into a real relationship with your Creator by following what He wants you to do.

pattern_issues.key: If you have clothes or garments with wool in them, get them checked for shaatnez (a forbidden mixture of wool and linen)
pattern_issues.key_full: If you have clothes or garments with wool in them, get them checked for shaatnez (a forbidden mixture of wool and linen) 🧵. If no need, learn something about shaatnez: The prohibition is linked to the story of Cain and Abel. Kayin, a farmer, brought produce (symbolized by linen) as an offering to G-d, and Hevel, a shepherd, offered animals (symbolized by wool). When G-d favored Abel's offering, it led to jealousy and conflict. Shaatnez teaches us to respect differences rather than fight them. This separation is only resolved in the Temple service, where wool and linen coexist in the priestly garments, symbolizing ultimate unity under divine purpose 👔.

pattern_issues.key: Get creative with Mishloach Manot (Purim food packages)! 📦😄 On Purim day, you are obligated to send food portions to at
pattern_issues.key_full: Get creative with Mishloach Manot (Purim food packages)! 📦😄 On Purim day, you are obligated to send food portions to at least *one* other Jewish adult to enhance friendship. But what counts? Here are the halachic requirements: (1) Minimum of *two different types* of ready-to-eat food or drink (e.g., a cookie and a juice box). No raw meat! (2) The package *must* be sent on Purim day itself (not before or after). (3) Ideally, it should be sent via a *messenger*, fulfilling the nuanced commandment of 'sending' (mishloach) rather than just delivering it yourself. Learn about it now and think about who you want to delight and enhance friendship with next Purim!

pattern_issues.key: Simchat Yom Tov — Simchat Yom Tov (שִׂמְחַת יוֹם טוֹב) is the Torah mitzvah "V'samachta b'chagecha" — rejoice in your fe
pattern_issues.key_full: Simchat Yom Tov — Simchat Yom Tov (שִׂמְחַת יוֹם טוֹב) is the Torah mitzvah "V'samachta b'chagecha" — rejoice in your festival (Devarim 16:14). The head of household facilitates joy for each family member in the way that brings them happiness (Pesachim 109a; SA OC 529:2): wife — new clothing or jewelry l'fi mamono (within means); children — treats (today candies and toys; Talmud: grains and nuts); men — festive meat and wine meals. Halacha requires including the poor — joy only among yourselves without helping the needy is "joy of one's stomach," not mitzvah joy. At the least it's recommended to provide the poor with tzedaka so they can enjoy the holiday as well.

pattern_issues.key: Mechirat chametz is selling chametz you want to keep to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach so it is not yours du
pattern_issues.key_full: Mechirat chametz is selling chametz you want to keep to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach so it is not yours during the holiday. Authorize the sale well before Erev Pesach — most rabbis stop accepting forms by the night before, even though ownership transfers on Erev Pesach morning. It must be an absolute sale, not a conditional gift (matana al menat lehachzir). Chametz sold should be stored away. When including year-round dishes, rabbis typically sell only the absorbed chametz flavor in the vessels — not the physical dishes themselves — to avoid requiring tevilat kelim after Pesach. Shortly after Pesach, ownership reverts per the contract your rabbi uses.

pattern_issues.key: Discover #2 of the Constant Mitzvot: Don't believe in any other powers! 🚫 This isn't just about avoiding idol worship; i
pattern_issues.key_full: Discover #2 of the Constant Mitzvot: Don't believe in any other powers! 🚫 This isn't just about avoiding idol worship; it's about deeply internalizing that Hashem is the *only* true power in the universe. Everything – from the smallest atom to the grandest galaxy – is completely under His control. Nothing can act without His permission! Take a moment to recognize that every event, every challenge, and every blessing you experience is directly from Him, with no intermediary forces or independent powers. This profound belief brings immense trust and peace of mind. Source: Exodus 20:3; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Negative Commandment 2; Mishneh Torah, Avodat Kochavim 2:1.

pattern_issues.key: Pesach Sheni (the Second Passover) falls on 14 Iyar. It originated from a request in the Torah (Numbers 9:6–13) by Jews
pattern_issues.key_full:
Pesach Sheni (the Second Passover) falls on 14 Iyar. It originated from a request in the Torah (Numbers 9:6–13) by Jews who were ritually impure and could not bring the Passover offering — G-d granted them a second chance one month later.

Today, Pesach Sheni is a minor holiday. It is not observed with full Yom Tov restrictions. The main custom is to eat matzah on this day. Tachanun (the penitential prayer) is not recited.

A classic Chabad teaching — attributed to the Lubavitcher Rebbeim (Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn, citing his father the Rebbe Rashab) — holds that Pesach Sheni means "ein davar avud": nothing is lost; it is always possible to correct and return.

pattern_issues.key: Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward. You take a baked
pattern_issues.key_full: Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward. You take a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (often meat, fish, or an unpeeled hard-boiled egg), recite the blessing and declaration from your siddur or machzor in a language you understand, and thereby may continue food preparation on Yom Tov for Shabbat — which would otherwise be forbidden because you may not cook on one holy day for the next. Store the designated foods in a safe, labeled spot — if they are eaten before you finish Friday cooking for Shabbat, the eruv is void. It also reminds the home to prepare for Shabbat, not only for the festival.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Oneg Shabbat! 🍽️ Did you know that taking pleasure in Shabbat (Oneg Shabbat) isn't just a nice custom, but a
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Oneg Shabbat! 🍽️ Did you know that taking pleasure in Shabbat (Oneg Shabbat) isn't just a nice custom, but a fundamental part of its holiness and a mitzvah in itself? This includes enjoying delicious food, a restful atmosphere, engaging in Torah study, prayer, and quality family time. The Talmud (Shabbat 118a) teaches that one who delights in Shabbat is rewarded with abundant blessings. So, go ahead and savor some treats, take that Shabbat nap, and truly relax! Every act of enjoyment, when done with the intention of honoring the day, becomes a mitzvah-filled experience. Learn one halacha about Shabbat meals or practices and level up your next Shabbat table!

pattern_issues.key: Make a difference: Give Tzedakah (charity) — even a small coin! 💸 The Torah teaches that every act of giving, no matter
pattern_issues.key_full: Make a difference: Give Tzedakah (charity) — even a small coin! 💸 The Torah teaches that every act of giving, no matter how small, has immense power to change the world for the better! Giving tzedakah is not just about helping the needy; it's a profound spiritual act that connects us to Hashem, who is the ultimate Giver. Even putting a single coin into a tzedakah box with a sincere heart can bring tremendous blessings and elevate your spiritual energy. The Rambam (Hilchot Matanot Aniyim 10:1) states that the mitzvah of tzedakah is equal in weight to all other mitzvot combined! So, next time you see a tzedakah box, remember the immense power in that small act of giving.

When this year:

pattern_issues.key_full:
Purim Meshulash — Megillah on Friday (14 Adar)

When this year:
• Thursday night after tzeit — first reading (you should have heard it then).
• Today (Friday) — second reading during the daytime (usually after Shacharit, before sunset) — a universal obligation, not optional.

How (same laws as regular Purim):
• Hear every word from a kosher megillah scroll; men and women are equally obligated.
• Stand for the blessings; customs at Haman's name vary by shul.

${PurimBrachotText.MEGILLAH_BLESSINGS_COMMON}

Reminder: matanot la'evyonim are also today (Friday), not Shabbat. Mishloach manot and the seudah are Sunday — prepare packages before Shabbat if you have not already.

pattern_issues.key:
${threeWeeksIntro()}

Chabad follows strict Ashkenazi mourning customs, with specific emphasis from the Lubavitcher Rebb
pattern_issues.key_full:
${threeWeeksIntro()}

Chabad follows strict Ashkenazi mourning customs, with specific emphasis from the Lubavitcher Rebbe on spiritual growth during this period.

From 17 Tammuz (general Three Weeks):
• Haircuts, music & weddings: prohibited throughout the entire Three Weeks.
• Shehecheyanu: avoided entirely, except on Shabbat or when required for a mitzvah (e.g. brit milah).
• Torah & charity: increase Torah study — especially subjects about the Holy Temple's layout and construction — and give extra tzedakah during this time.

From Rosh Chodesh Av (Nine Days): restrictions intensify — see the Nine Days checklist item for meat, wine, laundry, bathing, and home practices.

pattern_issues.key: The Fast of 10 Tevet commemorates the day Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, began the siege of Jerusalem — the event that
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Fast of 10 Tevet commemorates the day Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, began the siege of Jerusalem — the event that ultimately led to the destruction of the First Temple.

It is a minor fast from dawn until nightfall. It is also observed as a general Kaddish day for Holocaust victims whose date of death is unknown.

Note: This is the only fast in the calendar that is never postponed if it falls on a Friday (because the biblical verse requires observing it "on this very day"). When it lands on a Friday, continue fasting directly into Friday night — welcome Shabbat while fasting — and break the fast only after nightfall following the recitation of Friday night Kiddush.
pattern_issues.snippet:
El ayuno del 10 de Tevet conmemora el día en que Nabucodonosor comenzó el asedio de Jerusalén — evento que llevó a la destrucción del Primer Templo.

pattern_issues.key: The Fast of 10 Tevet commemorates the day Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, began the siege of Jerusalem — the event that
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Fast of 10 Tevet commemorates the day Nebuchadnezzar, king of Babylon, began the siege of Jerusalem — the event that ultimately led to the destruction of the First Temple.

It is a minor fast from dawn until nightfall. It is also observed as a general Kaddish day for Holocaust victims whose date of death is unknown.

Note: This is the only fast in the calendar that is never postponed if it falls on a Friday (because the biblical verse requires observing it "on this very day"). When it lands on a Friday, continue fasting directly into Friday night — welcome Shabbat while fasting — and break the fast only after nightfall following the recitation of Friday night Kiddush.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Choresh — plowing and preparing the ground on Shabbat! 🚜 This melacha covers loosening, leveling, or improvi
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Choresh — plowing and preparing the ground on Shabbat! 🚜 This melacha covers loosening, leveling, or improving the earth: from plowing a field and digging in a garden bed to aerate soil, to preparing a plot for planting. It traces back to the Mishkan, where the ground was plowed to cultivate herbs for tapestry dyes and wheat for the Showbread. Practical takeaway: this rule extends beyond actual gardening. Because leveling or rutting the earth is forbidden, we are careful not to drag heavy benches or strollers across soft outdoor dirt on Shabbat, as this inevitably carves a furrow. On Shabbat, we step back from reshaping the face of the earth itself. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Contemplate #5 of the Constant Mitzvot: Be in awe of Hashem! 🤩 This isn't just about fear; it's about recognizing God's
pattern_issues.key_full: Contemplate #5 of the Constant Mitzvot: Be in awe of Hashem! 🤩 This isn't just about fear; it's about recognizing God's immense power, majesty, and transcendence, inspiring profound reverence. Imagine the splitting of the Red Sea or standing at Mount Sinai – that sense of overwhelming grandeur is what Yirat Hashem (Awe of G-d) is all about! This awareness helps us appreciate the significance of every mitzvah and realize that every action we take matters. It guides us to make choices that are aligned with spiritual truth and connect us to what's truly important in life. Source: Deuteronomy 6:13; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Positive Commandment 4; Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 2:2.
