pattern_issues.key: Muktzeh refers to items that are "set aside" and may not be moved on Shabbat. Unlike the 39 melachot which come from the
pattern_issues.key_full:
Muktzeh refers to items that are "set aside" and may not be moved on Shabbat. Unlike the 39 melachot which come from the Mishkan's construction, Muktzeh is a Rabbinic enactment to protect the spirit of Shabbat rest.

The main principle: any object whose primary purpose is forbidden on Shabbat — like a pen, hammer, or smartphone — may generally be moved only to use the space it occupies, or if you need it for a Shabbat-permitted purpose.

Items that are completely non-functional on Shabbat (like money, rocks, or broken objects that lost utility before Shabbat) generally may not be moved at all. To prepare, store wallets, work tools, and electronics out of reach before Shabbat begins.

pattern_issues.key: Be a comfort to others: Visit or call someone who is sick! 🤝 The mitzvah of Bikur Cholim (visiting the sick) is a profou
pattern_issues.key_full: Be a comfort to others: Visit or call someone who is sick! 🤝 The mitzvah of Bikur Cholim (visiting the sick) is a profound act of Chesed (kindness)! The Gemara (Nedarim 39b) teaches that visiting the sick removes one-sixtieth of their illness. It's about bringing comfort, hope, and companionship to someone in need. Even a phone call, video chat, or a thoughtful message can help them! The Rambam (Hilchot Aveilut 14:6) clarifies that the mitzvah includes praying for the sick person, speaking words of encouragement, and assisting with their needs. If you don't know anyone who is sick, you can still fulfill the spirit of this mitzvah by learning its laws and reflecting on its importance.

pattern_issues.key: Deep dive: Study one line of Torah today and REALLY get it! 🧠 The Sages teach that Torah contains both revealed (Nigleh)
pattern_issues.key_full: Deep dive: Study one line of Torah today and REALLY get it! 🧠 The Sages teach that Torah contains both revealed (Nigleh) and hidden (Nistar) dimensions, and even a single line can hold infinite wisdom! Don't just skim; choose one pasuk (verse) or a short halacha and immerse yourself. Read the commentaries (like Rashi, Ramban, or a contemporary commentary) and truly ponder its deeper meanings. How does it apply to your life? What new insight can you glean? This focused, thoughtful approach to learning transforms a simple reading into a profound spiritual experience, connecting you directly to Hashem's infinite wisdom. Source: Pirkei Avot 2:16; Zohar, Parshat Yitro; Talmud, Eruvin 13a.

pattern_issues.key: mechirat chametz — Mechirat chametz is selling chametz you want to keep to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach so
pattern_issues.key_full: mechirat chametz — Mechirat chametz is selling chametz you want to keep to a non-Jew through your rabbi before Pesach so it is not yours during the holiday. Authorize the sale well before Erev Pesach — most rabbis stop accepting forms by the night before, even though ownership transfers on Erev Pesach morning. It must be an absolute sale, not a conditional gift (matana al menat lehachzir). Chametz sold should be stored away. When including year-round dishes, rabbis typically sell only the absorbed chametz flavor in the vessels — not the physical dishes themselves — to avoid requiring tevilat kelim after Pesach. Shortly after Pesach, ownership reverts per the contract your rabbi uses.

pattern_issues.key: Chanukah (the Festival of Lights) is observed for eight nights beginning on 25 Kislev. It commemorates the miracle of th
pattern_issues.key_full:
Chanukah (the Festival of Lights) is observed for eight nights beginning on 25 Kislev. It commemorates the miracle of the Temple menorah that burned for eight days on only one day's worth of oil, following the Maccabees' victory over the Greeks and rededication of the Temple.

Key observances:
• Lighting the Chanukiah (menorah) each night — one light on the first night, adding one each successive night
• Lights are placed in a visible location to publicize the miracle
• Hallel and Al HaNisim prayer are added to services
• It is customary to eat foods fried in oil (latkes, sufganiyot) and play dreidel

Chanukah is not a Yom Tov — regular weekday activities including work are permitted.

pattern_issues.key:
Second Seder prep (Diaspora this year):
• Saturday daytime is the first day of Yom Tov (Shabbat). Saturday night begins
pattern_issues.key_full:
Second Seder prep (Diaspora this year):
• Saturday daytime is the first day of Yom Tov (Shabbat). Saturday night begins the second day of Yom Tov — not a regular Motzei Shabbat weekend.
• Kiddush at the second Seder includes Yaknehaz (integrated Havdalah: wine, Kiddush, candle, Havdalah text, Shehecheyanu — no spices).
• You may NOT cook, chop, or prepare on Shabbat day for the second Seder. Hachana from weekday across Shabbat to a later Yom Tov is forbidden. Eruv tavshilin does not apply when Yom Tov falls on Shabbat — it is only when Yom Tov immediately precedes Shabbat. All food prep, cooking, and table setting for the second night must wait until Shabbat fully ends Saturday night.

pattern_issues.key: Eruv tavshilin — Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward.
pattern_issues.key_full: Eruv tavshilin — Eruv tavshilin is a symbolic meal set aside on Erev Yom Tov when Shabbat follows immediately afterward. You take a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (often meat, fish, or an unpeeled hard-boiled egg), recite the blessing and declaration from your siddur or machzor in a language you understand, and thereby may continue food preparation on Yom Tov for Shabbat — which would otherwise be forbidden because you may not cook on one holy day for the next. Store the designated foods in a safe, labeled spot — if they are eaten before you finish Friday cooking for Shabbat, the eruv is void. It also reminds the home to prepare for Shabbat, not only for the festival.

pattern_issues.key:
The Nine Days (from Rosh Chodesh Av until after Tisha B'Av) follow strict Ashkenazi mourning in Chabad practice.

From R
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Nine Days (from Rosh Chodesh Av until after Tisha B'Av) follow strict Ashkenazi mourning in Chabad practice.

From Rosh Chodesh Av:
• Meat & wine: prohibited entirely, except on Shabbat or at a seudat mitzvah (e.g. brit milah, siyum).
• Laundry, bathing & home: traditional Ashkenazi prohibitions against laundering, bathing for pleasure, home improvements, and buying new garments are observed.

${nineDaysSharedHalacha()}

After the fast (10 Av): follow accepted Chabad psak on meat, wine, music, laundry, and bathing until chatzos on 10 Av — ask your rav if unsure.

Nine Days Havdalah: use wine or grape juice; Ashkenazi-style custom often gives the cup to a child (ages 6–9) when possible.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Dosh — threshing and extracting resources on Shabbat! 🌾 In the ancient world, threshing meant beating stalks
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Dosh — threshing and extracting resources on Shabbat! 🌾 In the ancient world, threshing meant beating stalks of wheat to separate the grain from its outer chaff—a vital step in the Mishkan to gather flour for the Showbread. Today, this melacha shows up in surprising ways, specifically when extracting liquid from solid food. Squeezing grapes or olives for their juice is a direct, Torah-level violation, and the Sages extended this to other fruits, which is why freshly squeezing oranges or grapefruits is forbidden on Shabbat. The rules are precise, but the theme is clear: on Shabbat, we don't 'process' raw nature to extract its hidden contents. We enjoy it exactly as it is. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key:
Chol HaMoed Sukkot — the intermediate days of Sukkot — is not full Yom Tov, but it is not ordinary weekdays either.

Key
pattern_issues.key_full:
Chol HaMoed Sukkot — the intermediate days of Sukkot — is not full Yom Tov, but it is not ordinary weekdays either.

Key points:
• Full Hallel each day of Sukkot including Chol HaMoed.
• Yaaleh V'yavo in Amidah and bentching; Tachanun is omitted.
• Lulav and etrog each weekday (not on Shabbat); meals in the sukkah when applicable.
• Melacha is restricted but less than Yom Tov — work needed to avoid loss may be permitted; ask your rav.
• Simchat moed — nicer meals, time in the sukkah, Torah learning.
• Shaving and haircuts are generally prohibited on Chol HaMoed unless under specific exceptions — ask your rav.
• Hoshana Raba (21 Tishrei) is the seventh day of Sukkot with special hoshanot customs.

pattern_issues.key: Transform your worries into prayers! 🕊️ Rabbi Nachman of Breslov taught a revolutionary approach to anxiety: instead of
pattern_issues.key_full: Transform your worries into prayers! 🕊️ Rabbi Nachman of Breslov taught a revolutionary approach to anxiety: instead of letting worried thoughts spiral, *transform* them into heartfelt conversations with Hashem through Hitbodedut! Whatever is bothering you – big or small, trivial or overwhelming – speak to G-d about it in your own words, out loud if possible. This powerful practice takes negative, draining energy and redirects it toward spiritual growth, profound trust, and a deeper connection to the Divine. Try it right now: pick one worry that's on your mind and talk it through with Hashem. You'll be amazed at the clarity and peace it brings! Source: Likutei Moharan II, 25; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key_full: Fulfill the beautiful mitzvah of Mezuzot! 🚪 If you don't have kosher Mezuzot on your doorways yet, it's a Torah commandment to get them affixed! Already have them? Make sure they're checked by a reliable Sofer at appropriate intervals (ask a rabbi) to ensure they remain kosher! Humidity, temperature changes, or even tiny cracks in the parchment or ink can render them invalid. Many people have the beautiful custom to touch the Mezuzah when they pass through a doorway and then kiss their fingers, helping us remember Hashem and His unity, and sanctifying our coming and going. The sages teach that the Mezuzah brings Divine protection to the home. Source: Deuteronomy 6:9; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 285-291.

pattern_issues.key_full: Be a peacemaker: Make peace between people! 🕊️ The mitzvah of Rodef Shalom (pursuing peace) is one of the greatest mitzvot in Judaism, described as one of the things 'a person eats its fruits in this world, and the principal remains for him in the World to Come.' (Peah 1:1). Whether it's helping to resolve an argument between friends, encouraging acceptance between different groups, or simply diffusing tension in a conversation, peacemaking is an act that brings immense spiritual reward. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 6b) praises those who pursue peace. Look for a small opportunity today to bring harmony into your environment, be it at home, at work, or in your community. Even a gentle word can make a huge difference!

• Hakafot — dancing with Torah scrolls; finish the annual cycle and begin Bereshit.
• Festive meals and drinking (responsibly) in many communities.
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Full Hallel and Musaf.
• In Israel, Simchat Torah coincides with Shemini Atzeret (one day).

pattern_issues.key: Experience #4 of the Constant Mitzvot: Love Hashem! ❤️ This isn't just a feeling; it's an active mitzvah to constantly c
pattern_issues.key_full: Experience #4 of the Constant Mitzvot: Love Hashem! ❤️ This isn't just a feeling; it's an active mitzvah to constantly cultivate a deep love for God. How do you do that? By reflecting on all the incredible kindnesses Hashem bestows upon you every single moment – from keeping your heart beating to orchestrating every detail of your life for your ultimate good. The more you recognize His constant presence and boundless benevolence, the more your love and appreciation for Him will naturally grow. Take a moment now to list five specific amazing/miraculous things Hashem has done for you – and thank Him with love! Source: Deuteronomy 6:5; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Positive Commandment 3; Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 2:2.

pattern_issues.key:
Chol HaMoed Pesach — the intermediate days of Passover — is not full Yom Tov, but it is not ordinary weekdays either.

K
pattern_issues.key_full:
Chol HaMoed Pesach — the intermediate days of Passover — is not full Yom Tov, but it is not ordinary weekdays either.

Key points:
• No chametz; eat kosher-for-Passover food only. Matzah is eaten on many days.
• Half Hallel at Shacharit (not Full Hallel — Ashkenazic custom permits a bracha on Partial Hallel; Sephardic custom often does not).
• Yaaleh V'yavo in Amidah and bentching; Tachanun is omitted.
• Melacha is restricted but less than Yom Tov — work needed to avoid loss may be permitted; ask your rav.
• Simchat moed — nicer meals, family time, Torah learning; avoid treating the day like a regular workweek.
• Shaving and haircuts are generally prohibited on Chol HaMoed unless under specific exceptions — ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key_full: Master #6 of the Constant Mitzvot: Don't be led astray by your eyes and heart! 👀❤️ This mitzvah (Numbers 15:39) reminds us to guard our thoughts and desires from pulling us away from Torah values and Hashem. It's an ongoing spiritual battle! When you notice yourself being drawn towards something that doesn't align with kedusha (holiness) or when your mind wanders into non-Torah philosophies, you have an immediate opportunity to fulfill this mitzvah. Staying focused on Torah, its wisdom, and its immense rewards is the ultimate key to true happiness and connection. This mitzvah requires constant vigilance and self-control. Source: Numbers 15:39; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Negative Commandment 3; Mishneh Torah, Avodat Kochavim 2:3.

pattern_issues.key: Discover a powerful prayer hack: persistent davening! 🗣️ Ever feel like your prayers aren't getting through? The Sages t
pattern_issues.key_full: Discover a powerful prayer hack: persistent davening! 🗣️ Ever feel like your prayers aren't getting through? The Sages teach us the importance of persistent prayer. While there's no magic number like '20 times in a row,' the core idea is to truly pour out your heart and not give up. Think of it like a child asking a parent repeatedly for something they really need! Before you even ask for your desires, the kabbalists teach a powerful sequence: (1) Praise Hashem for His greatness, (2) Thank Him for all the blessings you already have. This helps open the 'gates of prayer' and puts you in a mindset of gratitude and trust. Keep davening with faith, and never despair! Source: Based on various rabbinic teachings on tefillah (prayer).

pattern_issues.key: Tisha B'Av (the 9th of Av) is the saddest day in the Jewish calendar. Both the First and Second Temples were destroyed o
pattern_issues.key_full:
Tisha B'Av (the 9th of Av) is the saddest day in the Jewish calendar. Both the First and Second Temples were destroyed on this date, along with numerous other tragedies throughout Jewish history.

It is a full 25-hour fast (sundown to nightfall), with the same five afflictions as Yom Kippur: no eating, drinking, bathing, anointing, leather shoes, or marital relations.

Melachah (work) is permitted but mourning customs apply: no Torah study (except on sad topics like Lamentations and Job), no greeting others, no music. Megillat Eichah (the Book of Lamentations) is read at night; Kinot (dirges) are recited in the morning.

The three weeks from 17 Tammuz until Tisha B'Av are called "The Three Weeks," a period of increasing mourning.

pattern_issues.key_full: Elevate your day: Express gratitude out loud to Hashem! 🙏 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov and countless sages teach that actively thanking Hashem for *everything* – even challenges – is a profound spiritual practice. It transforms harsh judgments into mercy and opens spiritual gates to blessings! Right now, try saying 'Thank You, Hashem!' for three *specific* things in your life. Be detailed: instead of a general 'thanks for food,' try 'Thank You, Hashem, for the delicious and nourishing breakfast I had this morning that gave me energy, and for all the amazing flavors infused in it!' Speaking your gratitude aloud creates a powerful spiritual channel and shifts your entire perspective. Source: Likutei Moharan I, 79; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Kotzer — reaping and detaching growth from its source! ✂️ This is one of the most everyday melachot: picking
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Kotzer — reaping and detaching growth from its source! ✂️ This is one of the most everyday melachot: picking fruit from a tree, plucking a flower, or detaching anything from its source of growth. It traces back to the Mishkan, where wheat and dye-herbs were harvested. The rule is absolute: if it grows, you cannot sever it. This even includes picking herbs from a potted plant on your kitchen windowsill. To protect this law, the Sages even forbade making direct use of a tree on Shabbat—so climbing trees, leaning on them, or hanging hammocks is out. Ever wonder why people don't pick flowers or climb trees on Shabbat? This is why. Shabbat teaches us to enjoy creation without constantly taking from it or disrupting it. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Level up your day: Learn one halacha (Jewish law) daily! 📚 The Sages teach that consistent Torah learning – even just on
pattern_issues.key_full: Level up your day: Learn one halacha (Jewish law) daily! 📚 The Sages teach that consistent Torah learning – even just one practical halacha a day – is a powerful way to stay connected to Hashem and His profound wisdom throughout your life. It's like a daily spiritual vitamin! Pick a topic that sparks your curiosity: Shabbat laws, the intricate blessings on food, business ethics, Kashrut, or how to treat others with respect. Whether it's from a Mishnah, Gemara, Shulchan Aruch, or a contemporary sefer, small, steady learning builds an unshakable foundation for a meaningful Jewish life. Did you know? Daily Torah study, particularly of halacha, is considered one of the acts that 'has no measure' – meaning its spiritual reward is truly infinite!

pattern_issues.key:
The Purim seudah (סעודת פורים) is one of the four Purim mitzvot (Esther 9:22; Peninei Halakha 05-16-02).

When:
• During
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Purim seudah (סעודת פורים) is one of the four Purim mitzvot (Esther 9:22; Peninei Halakha 05-16-02).

When:
• During Purim day — before sunset (many hold the meal in the afternoon after mitzvot are underway).

How:
• A festive meal with bread (hamotzi — many use two rolls), meat, wine, and joy. Do not use matzah — there is no Purim custom for matzah, and many avoid matzah in the weeks before Pesach so the Seder taste stays distinct (Rema O.C. 471:2).
• Include words of Torah or thanks to Hashem — the meal is a mitzvah, not only a party.
• Drinking wine is a widespread custom but not required to excess; celebrate responsibly.

Plan the menu and timing so matanot la'evyonim and mishloach manot are handled earlier in the day when possible.
pattern_issues.snippet:

Plan the menu and timing so matanot la'evyonim and mishloach manot are handled earlier in the day when possible.

pattern_issues.key: Light up the night: Learn about Chanukah candles and Pirsumei Nisa (Publicizing the Miracle)! 🕎 The entire purpose of li
pattern_issues.key_full: Light up the night: Learn about Chanukah candles and Pirsumei Nisa (Publicizing the Miracle)! 🕎 The entire purpose of lighting Chanukah candles isn't just for personal enjoyment, but to publicly proclaim the miracle of the oil! This guiding principle shapes every halacha: PLACEMENT: Ideally, place your Chanukiah at the doorway of your home, on the *left* side (so the Mezuzah is on the right, and the Chanukiah on the left – you're surrounded by mitzvot!). If you live above the ground floor, a window visible from the street is the preferred spot. In times of danger, when public display wasn't safe, one would light indoors on a table. TIMING: Candles are lit around nightfall (Tzet HaKochavim) and must burn for at least 30 minutes into the night.

pattern_issues.key_full: Uncover the good point — practice Azamra! 🎵 This is one of Rabbi Nachman of Breslov's most transformative teachings: even in a person who seems completely lost or flawed, you *must* search until you find a single spark of goodness within them. By focusing on that good point, judging them favorably, you can actually help elevate both them and yourself spiritually! 'Azamra' means 'I will sing,' because by finding and celebrating that hidden good, you create a melody of praise to Hashem. Try this powerful exercise today: pick someone (or even look within yourself!) and consciously identify one genuinely positive quality. Watch how this shift in perspective can transform your entire outlook! Source: Likutei Moharan I, 282; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Level up your character: Study Pirkei Avot! 📚 Pirkei Avot, the 'Ethics of the Fathers,' is a treasure trove of timeless
pattern_issues.key_full: Level up your character: Study Pirkei Avot! 📚 Pirkei Avot, the 'Ethics of the Fathers,' is a treasure trove of timeless wisdom for daily life and middot (character development). Each short teaching offers profound, practical guidance on how to be a truly good person, treat others with kindness and integrity, and serve Hashem with humility and intention. These aren't just ancient sayings; they are foundational Jewish values that have shaped our ethical framework for over 2,000 years! Make it a weekly habit: pick your favorite lesson from any chapter and reflect on how you can apply its wisdom to your own life today. It's a spiritual workout for your soul! Source: Mishnah, Pirkei Avot; Talmud, Bava Kama 30a (regarding the importance of ethical conduct).

pattern_issues.key: Supercharge your prayers with Tzedakah (Charity)! 💰 It's a powerful tradition, taught by great sages, to give charity *b
pattern_issues.key_full: Supercharge your prayers with Tzedakah (Charity)! 💰 It's a powerful tradition, taught by great sages, to give charity *before* prayer and Torah study. Why? Giving tzedakah opens the 'gates of compassion' in Heaven and elevates your spiritual endeavors! Rabbi Nachman connected tzedakah to mystical light, explaining that every act of giving, even a small coin with a sincere heart, creates a channel for Divine illumination to flow into the world. It’s like preparing the ground for your spiritual seeds to grow! Before your next prayer session or learning, try dropping a coin into a tzedakah box (or giving online) and notice how it deepens your connection to Hashem. Source: Talmud, Bava Batra 10a; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 92:1; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key_full: Practice Hitbodedut — talk to Hashem in your own words! 🌳 Did you know Rabbi Nachman of Breslov taught that this is among the 'highest levels' of prayer? Hitbodedut is setting aside dedicated time (ideally in a quiet, private place like a field or forest, or even just your room!) to speak intimately with Hashem, just like you would with your best friend. Pour out your heart in your native language about anything and everything – your worries, dreams, gratitude, questions, or just your day. Rabbi Nachman emphasized that this unscripted, spontaneous prayer 'rises to a very high place' and creates tremendous spiritual delight and connection. Even 5-10 minutes a day can transform your spiritual life! Source: Likutei Moharan II, 25; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Yom Yerushalayim (28 Iyar) marks the reunification of Jerusalem during the Six-Day War in 1967 (5727). It is not a Yom T
pattern_issues.key_full:
Yom Yerushalayim (28 Iyar) marks the reunification of Jerusalem during the Six-Day War in 1967 (5727). It is not a Yom Tov — melacha is fully permitted.

Customs vary by community:

Religious Zionist / Dati Leumi: Hallel is recited (with or without a bracha, depending on the posek and community). Tachanun is omitted. Some communities recite special tefillot.

Sephardic (Rav Ovadia Yosef): Hallel is not recited for the same reason as Yom Ha'atzmaut — not established by Chazal. Practice varies.

Chabad and Charedi communities: Generally no special observance. Tachanun is said as usual.

Yom Yerushalayim is observed by fewer communities than Yom Ha'atzmaut, and there is no universally accepted halachic obligation. Ask your rav about your community's custom.

pattern_issues.key: Discover the Tikkun HaKlali — Rabbi Nachman's powerful ten psalms! 📖 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov revealed a specific spirit
pattern_issues.key_full: Discover the Tikkun HaKlali — Rabbi Nachman's powerful ten psalms! 📖 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov revealed a specific spiritual remedy known as the Tikkun HaKlali (General Rectification), consisting of ten chapters of Tehillim (Psalms 16, 32, 41, 42, 59, 77, 90, 105, 137, 150). He taught that this special collection has immense power to rectify the soul, particularly in cases of spiritual impurity or distress. What's truly remarkable? Rabbi Nachman stated that the revelation of this unique remedy was 'a very wonderful and awesome rectification' that had remained hidden since the time of Creation! You can recite these psalms anytime you need spiritual cleansing, strength, or a profound connection to Hashem. Source: Likutei Moharan I, 205; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Explore #3 of the Constant Mitzvot: Know that Hashem is One! ☝️ This isn't just about a numerical unity; it's about unde
pattern_issues.key_full: Explore #3 of the Constant Mitzvot: Know that Hashem is One! ☝️ This isn't just about a numerical unity; it's about understanding that God is absolutely unique, indivisible, and utterly beyond our comprehension – the ultimate singularity! He is the source of all existence, which is completely unified in His essence and power. When you say the first line of Shema Yisrael, focus deeply on this profound truth. Many have the custom to close their eyes during this line to block out distractions and concentrate entirely on Hashem's absolute unity. You can fulfill this mitzvah any time by contemplating God's oneness and His singular control over the universe. Source: Deuteronomy 6:4; Rambam, Sefer HaMitzvot, Positive Commandment 2; Mishneh Torah, Yesodei HaTorah 1:7.

pattern_issues.key: Say the Shema — the ultimate declaration of Jewish faith! 🗣️ 'Hear O Israel, the Lord our G-d, the Lord is One.' This ic
pattern_issues.key_full: Say the Shema — the ultimate declaration of Jewish faith! 🗣️ 'Hear O Israel, the Lord our G-d, the Lord is One.' This iconic verse is arguably the most important sentence a Jew says in their entire life! It's often the first sentence a Jewish child learns, and often the last words spoken before passing on. We say it twice daily (morning and night) as a Torah commandment. The halacha requires saying Shema with enough kavanah (concentration) to understand its meaning, at least for the first verse, and clearly enough to hear yourself. A powerful story: Rabbi Akiva, while being tortured to death by the Romans, declared that he had yearned his whole life to fulfill 'love G-d with all your soul' even if it meant giving his life – and he died with 'Echad' (One) on his lips.

pattern_issues.key: Zimmun is said only when at least three Jewish men who have reached bar mitzvah (age 13 or older) ate together in the ha
pattern_issues.key_full:
Zimmun is said only when at least three Jewish men who have reached bar mitzvah (age 13 or older) ate together in the halachic sense that calls for Birkat Hamazon as one group; someone eating alone—or any gathering that does not meet this—does not recite Zimmun. When ten such men are present, include the parenthetical wording (“our God,” etc.).

My masters, let us bless!
Others respond: May the name of the Lord be blessed from now and forever.
Leader says: With the permission of our masters and teachers, let us bless (if 10: our God) He of whose food we have eaten.
Others respond: Blessed be (our God) He of whose food we have eaten and through whose goodness we live.
Leader repeats: Blessed be (our God) He of whose food we have eaten and through whose goodness we live.

pattern_issues.key: Hallel (praise) is a selection of Psalms 113–118 recited as praise and thanksgiving to G-d on Yom Tov (festivals), Rosh
pattern_issues.key_full:
Hallel (praise) is a selection of Psalms 113–118 recited as praise and thanksgiving to G-d on Yom Tov (festivals), Rosh Chodesh (the new month), and Chanukah.

Full Hallel is recited on Shavuot, all seven days of Sukkot (including Chol HaMoed Sukkot), Shemini Atzeret, Simchat Torah, and all eight days of Chanukah.

Pesach — Full vs. Half Hallel:
• Outside Israel (Diaspora): Full Hallel on the first two nights/days of Pesach (Yom Tov); Half Hallel from Chol HaMoed onward through the last days.
• In Israel: Full Hallel only on the first day of Pesach; Half Hallel from the second day onward.
The reason for Half Hallel from Chol HaMoed: we do not fully celebrate while the Egyptians drowned in the sea.

Half Hallel (certain paragraphs omitted) is also recited on Rosh Chodesh.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the mitzvah of Halvayat HaMeit, the sacred act of accompanying the deceased to their final resting place! 📜
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the mitzvah of Halvayat HaMeit, the sacred act of accompanying the deceased to their final resting place! 📜 This is famously called chesed shel emet, or true kindness, because it is the purest form of giving where the recipient can never repay the favor. The Rambam explains that this is a full Torah obligation rooted in the commandment to love your neighbor as yourself! 💙 Even walking just a few steps with a coffin is considered a massive mitzvah that brings incredible honor to the departed soul. People often even interrupt their Torah study to join a funeral procession and pay their respects. 🏛️ This act of escorting the deceased shows that every single Jewish person is a vessel of holiness and deserves to be treated with dignity until the very end. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key_full:
Today is Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement.

The fast:
• Began at sunset last night (many communities accepted it earlier with tosefet — adding time from erev onto Yom Kippur) and ends at nightfall tonight${tzeit?.let { " (approx. $it)" } ?: ""}.
• Five afflictions: no eating/drinking, no leather shoes, no bathing for pleasure, no anointing, no marital relations.
• Many wear white; married men who normally wear a kittel do so; tallit is worn all day.
• The day is spent in prayer — Kol Nidrei was last night before sunset (hatarat nedarim cannot be done on Yom Kippur itself), then Shacharit, Musaf, Mincha, Neilah, then Maariv and Havdalah after nightfall.

After nightfall: eat the Motzei Yom Kippur meal (see your checklist). Chabad tradition connects this meal to parnassa for the year.

pattern_issues.key: Learn the Rambam's surprisingly specific health guidelines! 🍽️ Written over 900 years ago, many of the Rambam's (Maimoni
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn the Rambam's surprisingly specific health guidelines! 🍽️ Written over 900 years ago, many of the Rambam's (Maimonides) health guidelines in Hilchot De'ot 4 are remarkably validated by modern medicine. He teaches: 'Maintaining a healthy body is part of serving Hashem, because it is impossible to understand or know Hashem if one is ill.' Here's his practical wisdom: Don't eat until you're stuffed! Stop eating when about 75% full. Eat slowly and in a seated position. Avoid drinking liquids excessively during meals. Don't sleep immediately after eating; wait a few hours. Prioritize sleep during the night; daytime napping is generally discouraged. Exercise is essential – the Rambam asserts that lack of physical movement is a primary cause of illness. Avoid foods that are clearly harmful.

pattern_issues.key: During Sefirat HaOmer we keep customs of mourning (aveilut) because Rabbi Akiva's 24,000 students died in a plague durin
pattern_issues.key_full:
During Sefirat HaOmer we keep customs of mourning (aveilut) because Rabbi Akiva's 24,000 students died in a plague during this period between Pesach and Shavuot (Talmud, Yevamot 62b). Their deaths ceased on Lag BaOmer — which is why many communities ease some restrictions then, while others continue until Shavuot or the morning of the 33rd day of the Omer.

Why we mourn: The Omer is the path from physical freedom (Pesach) to spiritual receiving of Torah (Shavuot). The plague cut short Torah transmission — so we temper joy with restraint until we reach Matan Torah.

Common customs (timing varies — ask your rav):
• No live music (recordings and a cappella rules vary by posek)
• No weddings
• No haircuts for part or all of the Omer

Follow your community's start and end dates for these practices.

pattern_issues.key: Ever dreamed of being a king? 👑 Discover the surprising halachot (laws) of a Jewish King! Unlike other monarchs, a Jewis
pattern_issues.key_full: Ever dreamed of being a king? 👑 Discover the surprising halachot (laws) of a Jewish King! Unlike other monarchs, a Jewish King had strict limits to keep him humble and focused on Hashem. For example, he couldn't acquire too many wives (to avoid his heart turning away from God - a maximum of 18, according to the Talmud), or too many horses (so he'd rely on Divine protection, not military might), or excessive silver and gold (to prevent arrogance). His primary role wasn't just ruling, but being a spiritual shepherd, a scholar immersed in Torah, and a righteous judge. These laws teach us that true leadership in Judaism is about humility, integrity, and putting the welfare of the people (and God's will) first. Even if you're not royalty, you can try to embody these kingly qualities in your own life!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Kiddush Hashem and Chillul Hashem — the positive commandment to sanctify G-d's Name and the severe prohibiti
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Kiddush Hashem and Chillul Hashem — the positive commandment to sanctify G-d's Name and the severe prohibition against desecrating it! The Rambam defines both: KIDDUSH HASHEM: A Torah scholar or recognizably observant Jew who acts with exceptional integrity, patience, and fairness creates a Kiddush Hashem. Example: the Rambam says if a Torah scholar goes to the market, deals honestly, speaks pleasantly even when wronged, and people say 'What a beautiful person — what a beautiful Torah their teacher taught them!' — that is Kiddush Hashem. CHILLUL HASHEM: The opposite. A recognizably religious person who cheats, insults, or acts improperly causes a Chillul Hashem. The SEVERITY: Chillul Hashem is one of the most severe prohibitions, which some say can only be atoned for by death! Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Learn the Rambam's stunning ruling on free will — and how he deals with the philosophical problem that seems to make it
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn the Rambam's stunning ruling on free will — and how he deals with the philosophical problem that seems to make it impossible! 🧭 The Rambam writes: 'Know that every person has the ability to choose to be righteous like Moses or wicked like Jeroboam... This is a great fundamental principle and a pillar of Torah. If G-d decreed that a person would be righteous or wicked, or if there was something in a person's nature that forced them one way... how could teshuvah even exist?' Then he makes his strongest statement: 'This is a secret that cannot be fully understood according to human intelligence.' G-d knows what will happen (divine foreknowledge) — yet each person is completely free. The Rambam says this apparent contradiction is like asking how G-d exists without time — it's beyond our conceptual framework!

pattern_issues.key: Serve Hashem with Simcha (Joy)! 😊 This isn't just a nice idea; Rabbi Nachman of Breslov (Likutei Moharan I, 24) teaches
pattern_issues.key_full: Serve Hashem with Simcha (Joy)! 😊 This isn't just a nice idea; Rabbi Nachman of Breslov (Likutei Moharan I, 24) teaches that performing mitzvot with joy elevates them to the highest spiritual levels, connecting them to the Keter (the 'crown' of creation)! He emphasized that joy is the key to spiritual breakthroughs and moving forward in life when you feel stuck. Think of it: a mitzvah done with a smile, song, or even a little dance is far more impactful! Your mission today: choose one mitzvah and perform it with extra happiness – whether it's giving tzedakah with a cheerful heart, singing during Torah study, or truly celebrating after prayer. Rabbi Nachman taught that joy isn't optional; it's how we access profound spiritual growth and closeness to Hashem! Source: Likutei Moharan I, 24; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Want to be a superstar kid (or adult)? 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Discover the incredibly important mitzvah of Kibud Av Va'em (Honoring you
pattern_issues.key_full: Want to be a superstar kid (or adult)? 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Discover the incredibly important mitzvah of Kibud Av Va'em (Honoring your Father and Mother)! It's one of the Ten Commandments, and it's more than just saying 'thank you.' It's about deep respect, ensuring their comfort, caring for them as they age, and bringing them nachas (joy/pride) through your actions. This mitzvah teaches gratitude, humility, and recognizing the chain of tradition. What's super cool? The Gemara (Kiddushin 31a) teaches that there's NO limit to honoring parents – every moment is an opportunity! Even if they've passed on, we honor them by learning Torah, performing mitzvot, and dedicating acts of kindness in their memory. So, think of one specific act you can do today to make your parents proud, whether they're with you or in the Olam Haba (World to Come)!

pattern_issues.key:
${threeWeeksIntro()}

Sephardic and Edot HaMizrach communities, following Shulchan Arukh, generally take a more lenient
pattern_issues.key_full:
${threeWeeksIntro()}

Sephardic and Edot HaMizrach communities, following Shulchan Arukh, generally take a more lenient approach than Ashkenazim during the early Three Weeks.

From 17 Tammuz (general Three Weeks):
• Haircuts & shaving: permitted during most of the Three Weeks; shaving is usually prohibited only during the week in which Tisha B'Av falls (shavuah she'chal bo).
• Music: live or recorded music is avoided.
• Weddings: some communities avoid weddings from 17 Tammuz; others are lenient and avoid them only from Rosh Chodesh Av — follow your kehilla.
• Shehecheyanu: avoided on new items for the duration of the period.

From Rosh Chodesh Av or the week of Tisha B'Av: additional restrictions apply — see the Nine Days checklist item. Some communities (e.g. Syrian, Mashadi) are stricter on meat and wine from Rosh Chodesh Av.

pattern_issues.key: Pesach begins on 15 Nissan. In the Diaspora it lasts eight days; in Israel seven days. It commemorates the Exodus from E
pattern_issues.key_full:
Pesach begins on 15 Nissan. In the Diaspora it lasts eight days; in Israel seven days. It commemorates the Exodus from Egypt.

Chametz (leavened grain products) must be completely removed from one's home before Pesach. There is a Torah-level prohibition on owning or eating chametz during Pesach. Matzah is eaten in its place.

The Seder is held on the first two nights (in the Diaspora). The Haggadah is read, telling the story of the Exodus. Four cups of wine are drunk; matzah, maror (bitter herbs), and other symbolic foods are eaten.

The first two and last two days are full Yom Tov; the middle days (Chol HaMoed) have lighter restrictions. Full Hallel is recited on the first two days of Pesach (Yom Tov); Half Hallel is recited from Chol HaMoed onward and on the final Yom Tov days. In Israel, Half Hallel begins from the second day.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the quality-control laws for offerings in the Beit Hamikdash! An animal offered to the Master of the Univers
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the quality-control laws for offerings in the Beit Hamikdash! An animal offered to the Master of the Universe had to be perfect. The Rambam lists dozens of physical defects (also called a 'mum') that disqualify an animal. If an animal has a permanent defect like a missing limb or a broken bone, it can never be offered, while temporary defects like a healing skin condition only disqualify the animal until it heals. Other disqualifying features include an eye that is too large or too small, being too old, or even just smelling bad! The deeper message here is that what we offer represents what we value. You wouldn't give a spoiled gift to a king, and the Temple standards remind us that divine service is all about genuine investment and giving your absolute best, not just your leftovers! Think about that for just a minute!

pattern_issues.key: Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement) falls on 10 Tishrei. It is the holiest day of the Jewish year — a full fast from sundo
pattern_issues.key_full:
Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement) falls on 10 Tishrei. It is the holiest day of the Jewish year — a full fast from sundown to nightfall (approximately 25 hours) and a day of intensive prayer and introspection.

Five forms of affliction (inuyim) are observed: no eating or drinking, no bathing, no applying oils/creams, no leather shoes, no marital relations.

Key prayers: Kol Nidrei (the night before), Yizkor (Ashkenaz custom on Yom Kippur morning — many Sefard communities omit or observe different memorial customs), the Neilah closing prayer, and the final single shofar blast at the conclusion of the fast.

Unlike a regular Yom Tov, Yom Kippur shares many of Shabbat's additional restrictions — including a strict prohibition on carrying outdoors (unless inside a kosher eruv), exactly like Shabbat. Melachah (labor) is fully forbidden.
pattern_issues.snippet: Yom Kipur (el Día de la Expiación) cae en 10 Tishrei. Es el día más sagrado del año judío, un ayuno completo del sol al anochecer (aproximadamente 25 horas) y un día de intensa oración e introspección

pattern_issues.key: Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement) falls on 10 Tishrei. It is the holiest day of the Jewish year — a full fast from sundo
pattern_issues.key_full:
Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement) falls on 10 Tishrei. It is the holiest day of the Jewish year — a full fast from sundown to nightfall (approximately 25 hours) and a day of intensive prayer and introspection.

Five forms of affliction (inuyim) are observed: no eating or drinking, no bathing, no applying oils/creams, no leather shoes, no marital relations.

Key prayers: Kol Nidrei (the night before), Yizkor (Ashkenaz custom on Yom Kippur morning — many Sefard communities omit or observe different memorial customs), the Neilah closing prayer, and the final single shofar blast at the conclusion of the fast.

Unlike a regular Yom Tov, Yom Kippur shares many of Shabbat's additional restrictions — including a strict prohibition on carrying outdoors (unless inside a kosher eruv), exactly like Shabbat. Melachah (labor) is fully forbidden.

pattern_issues.key:

By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat and wept when we remembered Zio
pattern_issues.key_full:
On ordinary weekdays when Tachanun is said, say:

By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat and wept when we remembered Zion. Upon the willows in its midst we hung our harps. For there our captors asked us for words of song, and our tormentors [asked of us] mirth: 'Sing for us from Zion's song.' How shall we sing the Lord's song on alien soil? If I forget you, O Jerusalem, let my right hand forget its skill. Let my tongue cleave to my palate if I do not remember you, if I do not bring up Jerusalem at the beginning of my joy. Remember, O Lord, against the Edomites the day of Jerusalem, who said, 'Raze it, raze it to its very foundation!' O daughter of Babylon, who is destined to be plundered, praiseworthy is he who repays you your recompense for what you did to us. Praiseworthy is he who will take and dash your infants against the rock.

pattern_issues.key: Torah learning bonds you with Hashem — the sages teach that the Shechinah dwells wherever words of Torah are spoken (Avo
pattern_issues.key_full:
Torah learning bonds you with Hashem — the sages teach that the Shechinah dwells wherever words of Torah are spoken (Avot 3:2) and that Torah study protects your life (Sotah 21a). Birkat HaTorah opens that door each day.

Before learning Torah for the first time each day, women say Birkat HaTorah — the blessings over Torah study. This applies when you learn halacha or any Torah text. Women's obligation in Birkat HaTorah is widely accepted among poskim; the precise level (Torah vs rabbinic) is debated — ask your rav.

Say the three blessings once in the morning (from after halachic midnight / chatzos halayla onward). They cover all Torah learning for the rest of the day.

After the blessings, many siddurim include short Torah passages. Even a few minutes of halacha learning afterward fulfills your daily study obligation (see Torah Study).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Me'ilah — misappropriating Temple property — and why using holy items for personal benefit is treated so ser
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Me'ilah — misappropriating Temple property — and why using holy items for personal benefit is treated so seriously in halacha! ⚠️ In Temple times, when something was declared 'hekdesh' (consecrated to G-d), it became completely off-limits for personal use. Me'ilah (trespass/misappropriation) is what happens when you benefit from that property. Examples: eating from the Temple's showbread yourself, using a consecrated animal to carry your cargo, taking oil from the Temple's store of oil. The consequences: repay the full value PLUS an additional fifth (adding a fifth so it would be 25% of 125% instead of 20% of 100%), AND bring a korban asham (guilt offering). Fascinating detail: the violation only kicks in when you actually BENEFIT — so accidentally touching Temple property doesn't trigger it, but eating from it does. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Tzeit (tzeit hakochavim) is halachic nightfall — when three medium stars appear and the day ends for night mitzvot like
pattern_issues.key_full: Tzeit (tzeit hakochavim) is halachic nightfall — when three medium stars appear and the day ends for night mitzvot like counting the Omer, bedikat chametz, Chanukah candles, Purim, and the end of Shabbat. It is not identical to sunset; communities follow different standards — from three medium stars to fixed minutes after sunset, and in some Diaspora communities much later (e.g. Rabbeinu Tam). Note: While standard Shabbat and weekday Erev Yom Tov candles must be lit before sunset, whenever a festival begins on a Saturday night (Motzei Shabbat), the Yom Tov candles strictly must be lit after sunset, only once nightfall (tzeit) has arrived, using a pre-existing flame — lighting them before sunset on Shabbat is a severe violation. Shavuot candles also wait for tzeit. The app shows local zmanim; when in doubt on a borderline mitzvah, ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key: Protect the world: Learn about Bal Tashchit! 🌍 This powerful Torah prohibition (Deuteronomy 20:19) forbids wanton, unnec
pattern_issues.key_full: Protect the world: Learn about Bal Tashchit! 🌍 This powerful Torah prohibition (Deuteronomy 20:19) forbids wanton, unnecessary destruction. While its source refers to not destroying fruit trees during a siege, the Sages expanded it to a broader principle: we must not waste or destroy anything useful without a constructive purpose. This includes cutting down fruit-bearing trees without good reason, tearing usable clothing, breaking functional vessels, wasting food for no reason, etc. Importantly, Bal Tashchit applies to *wanton* destruction; removing a fruit tree to build a house is permitted, as there's a productive purpose. The deeper lesson, as explained by the Sefer HaChinuch, is to cultivate a character of care and appreciation for G-d's creation, rather than thoughtless carelessness. It trains us to be stewards of the world, not destroyers.

pattern_issues.key: Remember this powerful truth: There is absolutely NO despair in the world! 💪 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov's most famous decl
pattern_issues.key_full: Remember this powerful truth: There is absolutely NO despair in the world! 💪 Rabbi Nachman of Breslov's most famous declaration — 'Ein shum ye'ush ba'olam klal!' — is a game-changer. It means that no matter how far someone feels they've fallen, no matter how many mistakes they've made, return to Hashem is always possible. Every challenge has a solution, every misstep can be rectified, and spiritual renewal is always just a moment away. When discouragement strikes, repeat this phrase aloud in Hebrew or English and let it infuse you with strength and hope. Rabbi Nachman based this on deep Kabbalistic insights: despair presumes your situation is permanent, but that fundamentally misunderstands the fluid, dynamic reality of Divine mercy. Redemption can literally happen in the blink of an eye! Source: Likutei Moharan II, 78; Rabbi Nachman's Teachings.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about one of the main vessels of the Beit HaMikdash — the holy Menorah. Did you know that there is a fascinating d
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about one of the main vessels of the Beit HaMikdash — the holy Menorah. Did you know that there is a fascinating debate among the Sages regarding how often the Menorah in the Beit Hamikdash was actually lit? While it is commonly understood that the lamps were lit once daily toward evening to burn through the night—a view championed by the Raavad and Rashba—the Rambam (Maimonides) argues that the Menorah was actually lit twice a day, both morning and evening, to ensure the light remained truly continuous. Adding a layer of mystery, the Talmud and the Alter Rebbe explain that even if the other lamps were extinguished, the Western Lamp (there's much discussion about which of the 7 candles this actually was) was kept burning throughout the entire day as a miraculous testimony that the Divine Presence rested among the people of Israel! Learn more!

This app is a learning companion, not a rabbi — it does not give halachic rulings.

This checklist does not contain all
pattern_issues.key_full:
This app is a learning companion, not a rabbi — it does not give halachic rulings.

This checklist does not contain all the mitzvot in the entire Torah. It covers standard daily mitzvot that observant Jews commonly practice — waking, prayer, blessings, meals, Torah study, Shabbat preparation, and similar foundations.

With your permission, the app uses GPS or a city you choose to calculate Jewish calendar times and when you can fulfill different mitzvot throughout the day (for example morning, afternoon, and evening prayer windows, candle lighting, and Shabbat-related times). Location is kept on your device only for zmanim and the calendar.

If you are new to Judaism, take it slow and do what you can. Build steady habits without overwhelm, and always ask an Orthodox rabbi you trust when something is unclear or when your situation needs personal guidance.

Our God and God of our fathers, may there rise, come, reach, appear, be favored, be heard, be regarded, and be remembered before You our remembrance and consideration, and the remembrance of our fathers, and the remembrance of Messiah son of David Your servant, and the remembrance of Jerusalem Your holy city, and the remembrance of all Your people the house of Israel, for deliverance, for goodness, for grace, for kindness, for mercy, for life, and for peace on this day of:
[On Rosh Chodesh: the New Moon]
[On Passover: the Festival of Matzot]
[On Sukkot: the Festival of Sukkot]
Remember us, Lord our God, on this day for good; consider us on this day for blessing; save us on this day for life. With a word of salvation and mercy spare us and be gracious to us; have mercy on us and save us, for our eyes are turned to You, because You are God, the gracious and merciful King.

pattern_issues.key:
While men are obligated in three structured prayer times, women are required to pray at least once a day.

What prayer (
pattern_issues.key_full:
While men are obligated in three structured prayer times, women are required to pray at least once a day.

What prayer (tefillah) is:
Tefillah (תְּפִלָּה) comes from the Hebrew root meaning to judge oneself or reflect. Prayer is both a mitzvah and our most direct channel of communication with G-d. The rabbis derived from the phrase "to serve G-d with all your heart" (Deuteronomy 11:13) that this refers to prayer — the service of the heart.

What to say:
The minimum for women includes praise of G-d, personal requests, and thanks. Many women say the full Amidah (Shemoneh Esrei). Others fulfill the obligation with a heartfelt personal prayer in any language.

For newcomers:
Start with what you can manage consistently. Even a few sincere sentences every morning — thanking G-d, asking for what you need — is a genuine fulfillment. Over time, learn more from a siddur (prayer book).

pattern_issues.key: The Nine Days and the week of Tisha B'Av (shavuah she'chal bo) are the strictest part of summer mourning for Sephardic c
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Nine Days and the week of Tisha B'Av (shavuah she'chal bo) are the strictest part of summer mourning for Sephardic communities following Shulchan Arukh — generally more lenient than Ashkenazim until the week of Tisha B'Av.

From Rosh Chodesh Av (1 Av):
• Haircuts: usually prohibited from Rosh Chodesh Av, or only during the actual week of Tisha B'Av — follow your rav.

From the week in which Tisha B'Av falls (shavuah she'chal bo):
• Meat & wine: prohibited from the start of that week (not necessarily the full Nine Days). Some communities (e.g. Syrian, Mashadi) are strict from Rosh Chodesh Av.
• Laundry & bathing: restrictions on washing clothes and bathing for pleasure apply during the week of Tisha B'Av.

${nineDaysSharedHalacha()}

After the fast: many Sephardim follow similar post-fast restrictions until chatzos on 10 Av — confirm with your rav before resuming meat and wine.

pattern_issues.key: Hoshana Raba is the 21st of Tishrei — the seventh and final day of Sukkot. While it is technically Chol HaMoed (an inter
pattern_issues.key_full:
Hoshana Raba is the 21st of Tishrei — the seventh and final day of Sukkot. While it is technically Chol HaMoed (an intermediate day), it carries special significance as the final "sealing" of the divine judgment begun on Rosh Hashana.

Synagogue service — customs vary by nusach:
• Ashkenaz: extended service with seven hakafot (circuits) with the lulav and aravot; willow branches (hoshanot) beaten on the ground after the circuits; lulav taken for the last time
• Sefard: similar hoshanot circuits in many communities; willow-beating customs vary — follow your siddur
• Chabad: hakafot with hoshanot; aravot may be beaten five times on the ground (not the lulav itself) — see your Chabad machzor
• Yemenite: distinct hoshanot liturgy and customs

Greeting someone with "a gutten kvittel" (a good inscription) is an Ashkenaz custom, referring to the final sealing of one's decree for the year.

pattern_issues.key: Hadlakat Nerot is lighting candles before Shabbat or Yom Tov — welcoming holiness into the home. Women traditionally lig
pattern_issues.key_full: Hadlakat Nerot is lighting candles before Shabbat or Yom Tov — welcoming holiness into the home. Women traditionally light; men if no woman is present. Ashkenazi and Chabad custom: daughters in the home may light their own candles with a bracha. Sephardic custom (Shulchan Arukh; Rav Ovadia Yosef): daughters under their parents' roof do not recite a bracha on separate candles if the mother already lit — that would be a bracha levatala. Ashkenazim often light then bless with eyes covered; Sephardim may bless first. Shabbat candles must be lit before sunset (not before Plag HaMincha). Weekday Erev Yom Tov candles are also lit before sunset. When Yom Tov begins on Motzei Shabbat, Yom Tov candles are lit only after tzeit, from a pre-existing flame. On other Yom Tov nights, before the holiday begins you may strike a new match; once Yom Tov has started, light only from a pre-existing flame.

pattern_issues.key:
Arba Minim (ארבעה מינים) — the Four Species — are taken each day of Sukkot (except Shabbat).

The four:
• Lulav — closed
pattern_issues.key_full:
Arba Minim (ארבעה מינים) — the Four Species — are taken each day of Sukkot (except Shabbat).

The four:
• Lulav — closed palm branch (at least 3 tefachim)
• Etrog — citron, beautiful and mostly intact (pitom if present should be intact)
• Hadasim — three myrtle branches
• Aravot — two willow branches

How to observe (everyone):
• Assemble the lulav: Bind the lulav, hadassim, and aravot together (per your hoshanah holder / koisan). Ensure the central spine of the lulav extends upward at least one handbreadth (tefach — around 4 inches) higher than the tops of the myrtle and willow branches (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 650:1).
• Check kashrut before Yom Tov — especially etrog and that leaves are fresh enough.
• When: Daytime; many do it before Shacharit at home or in shul. Not on Shabbat.
• $daysNote

If a species is missing or invalid, ask your rabbi — there are limited substitutions in pressing cases.

pattern_issues.key:
Mishloach manot (משלוח מנות) — sending portions of food — increases friendship and joy on Purim (Esther 9:19).

The mitz
pattern_issues.key_full:
Mishloach manot (משלוח מנות) — sending portions of food — increases friendship and joy on Purim (Esther 9:19).

The mitzvah (Rambam, Shulchan Arukh 695:4; Aish; Peninei Halakha 05-16-04):
• Send at least two different ready-to-eat foods or drinks to one friend on Purim day — one mishloach manot package.
• Women are equally obligated; many send to a woman friend and men to a man (Rema). Sending to additional friends is praiseworthy.
• Examples: wine and cookies, fruit and pastry — clearly two types, not one combined dish.

How to do it:
• Deliver on Purim day before sunset — by you or a messenger (shul lists, kids, or neighbors are fine)
• Food should be ready to eat without cooking
• Label sender and recipient; mishloach manot should be identifiable

Tips: You need not reciprocate every package you receive the same day. Plan ahead on Erev Purim so deliveries are not rushed at the last minute.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about a surprising ruling: answering Amen can be GREATER than reciting the bracha itself! 🙏 The Talmud (Berakhot 5
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about a surprising ruling: answering Amen can be GREATER than reciting the bracha itself! 🙏 The Talmud (Berakhot 53b) records a debate: is the one who recites a blessing greater, or the one who answers Amen? Rabbi Yose ruled: 'The one who answers Amen is greater than the one who recites the blessing!' Because Amen is an affirmation — you are declaring that what was just said is completely true. When you say Amen to a blessing, you are endorsing it and sharing in its merit. The Talmud compares it to two workers: the one who leads (recites the blessing) goes first into battle, but the one who responds (answers Amen) is the stronger fighter who reinforces the victory. The Zohar teaches that 'AMEN' stands for 'El Melech Ne'eman' — G-d, faithful King. So when you answer Amen, you're not just saying 'yes' — you're proclaiming G-d as the faithful King. Try to give your next 'amen' a little oomph!

pattern_issues.key: Mincha (Chabad / Nusach Ari): The communal service begins with Ashrei — not Korbanot (Sefer HaMinhagim, Mincha). The gab
pattern_issues.key_full:
Mincha (Chabad / Nusach Ari): The communal service begins with Ashrei — not Korbanot (Sefer HaMinhagim, Mincha). The gabbai or chazan opens the minyan with Psalm 145; the congregation does not wait for or jointly recite Korbanot at Mincha.

Korbanot privately (optional): The Alter Rebbe (Shulchan Arukh HaRav, O.C. 234, Kuntres Acharon 2) writes that after Mincha Gedolah an individual who is meticulous may read the Tamid and Ketoret passages on his own — a personal stringency (midat chasidut), not part of the communal service. Hayom Yom (11 Nissan) treats this as private preparation before joining the minyan.

Tachanun (Chabad): At Shacharit on ordinary weekdays — longer on Mondays and Thursdays (Vehu Rachum), shorter on other days. At Mincha, typically the shorter Tachanun (Vayomer David el Gad). Omitted on Rosh Chodesh, Yom Tov, Chanukah, Purim, and other special occasions — Mincha itself is still said.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Kiddush and
pattern_issues.key_full:
Shemini Atzeret (22 Tishrei in the Diaspora) — eighth day of Sukkot.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Kiddush and festive meals.
• Sukkah in the Diaspora: Due to safek dyoma (halachic doubt which day is which), Diaspora Ashkenazim are required to eat all major meals in the sukkah on Shemini Atzeret, though leishev basukkah is omitted entirely. Sephardic and Chabad customs vary — confirm with your rav.

Davening:
• Liturgical shift: During Musaf today, the entire Jewish world officially transitions to the winter prayer cycle, universally inserting "Mashiv HaRuach U'Morid HaGeshem" into the second blessing of the Amidah. Tefillat Geshem is recited in Musaf.
• Yizkor — Ashkenaz communities recite memorial prayers.
• Full Hallel and Musaf; Yom Tov Amidah.

Simchat Torah is tomorrow (23 Tishrei) in the Diaspora — see that day's checklist item for hakafot and Torah joy.

Tonight: light Yom Tov candles; no lulav on Shemini Atzeret.

pattern_issues.key: The word mitzvah (מִצְוָה) literally means "commandment." In Judaism, mitzvot are the 613 commandments given by G-d thro
pattern_issues.key_full:
The word mitzvah (מִצְוָה) literally means "commandment." In Judaism, mitzvot are the 613 commandments given by G-d through the Torah, and also some extra mitzvot which our rabbis, through Divine inspiration, added on. 

While mitzvah means commandment, it also carries the deeper meaning of "connection." By performing a mitzvah, we fulfill G-d's will and connect with the Divine. It's like following instructions from a loved one; by doing so, you strengthen your bond with them. 

G-d is constantly sending down pure Heavenly Light which sustains the world and everything in it. If you go against His will, it's like putting an umbrella between yourself and this Light. But by performing mitzvot you can connect with the Heavenly Goodness that is G-d Himself, and experience Heaven on Earth- in a state you might like to call "Mitz Mode." It might not happen right away, but do a few mitzvot and see how you feel. This is just the beginning...

pattern_issues.key:
Psalms and poetic passages recited before the Shema and Amidah to prepare the heart and mind for prayer.

What they are:
pattern_issues.key_full:
Psalms and poetic passages recited before the Shema and Amidah to prepare the heart and mind for prayer.

What they are:
Pesukei DeZimra (פְּסוּקֵי דְזִמְרָא — Verses of Song) is a collection of psalms and passages from the Hebrew Bible said before the central prayers each morning. The word zimra means song or melody.

Why we say them:
The Talmud compares jumping straight into the Amidah without preparation to barging into a king's presence without first requesting an audience. Pesukei DeZimra is our way of gradually elevating our focus, shifting from ordinary thought to spiritual awareness, before standing before G-d in prayer.

The minimum for when you are short on time:
• Baruch She'amar (opening blessing)
• Ashrei (Psalm 145)
• Yishtabach (closing blessing)

The full version also includes Psalms 146-150 and other hymns. If you arrive at synagogue late, skip Pesukei DeZimra to say the Shema and Amidah with the congregation on time.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about ribbis, the prohibition against charging or paying interest in a Jewish-to-Jewish financial transaction. The
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about ribbis, the prohibition against charging or paying interest in a Jewish-to-Jewish financial transaction. The Torah states: You shall not give him your money upon interest, nor give him your food for increase (Leviticus 25:37). While the Torah prohibition applies to charging interest, the Sages expanded the prohibition to include many indirect forms of benefit and paying interest. Core Principles: Charging or paying interest between two Jewish parties is strictly forbidden without a valid Heter Iska. The Torah prohibition of ribbis applies exclusively to lending money to another Jew; charging interest to a non-Jew is permitted by Halacha (Deuteronomy 23:21). The prohibition applies not only to cash, but also to non-monetary benefits, services, or gifts connected to a loan. A Heter Iska is a legal and halachic contract that restructures a loan into a business partnership, allowing for returns that are halachically permissible.

pattern_issues.key: Sefirat HaOmer is the mitzvah to count the 49 days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking the Exodus to
pattern_issues.key_full:
Sefirat HaOmer is the mitzvah to count the 49 days from the second night of Pesach until Shavuot — linking the Exodus to receiving the Torah.

When to count:
• Count each night after tzeit (nightfall), before dawn.
• Many Ashkenazim count after Maariv; many Sephardim and Chabad also count after Maariv. Follow your community.

The blessing:
• Before counting on the first night, recite the blessing "…al sefirat ha'omer."
• Then say the count for that night (e.g. "Today is one day of the Omer").

If you forgot at night:
• Count the next day during the daytime without a bracha.
• If you do this before sunset, you can continue counting on subsequent nights with a bracha.
• You only lose the blessing permanently if you miss an entire 24-hour cycle (both night and the following day) — ask your rav.

The Omer period is also a time of mourning in many communities (no haircuts, weddings, or live music) until Lag BaOmer or Shavuot, depending on custom.

pattern_issues.key:
Matanot la'evyonim (מתנות לאביונים) helps every Jew celebrate Purim with food and joy (Esther 9:22).

The mitzvah (Penin
pattern_issues.key_full:
Matanot la'evyonim (מתנות לאביונים) helps every Jew celebrate Purim with food and joy (Esther 9:22).

The mitzvah (Peninei Halakha 05-16-03):
• Give at least one gift to each of two different poor people (minimum of two recipients total) during Purim daytime.
• Each gift should enable a modest Purim meal — money is common (Peninei Halakha: roughly enough for about three slices of bread or your community's minimum; amounts vary).

How to do it:
• Give during Purim daytime only (not at night); many give after the daytime Megillah reading (follow your minhag).
• Ideally before your Purim seudah so recipients can use it for the meal.
• You may give through a trustworthy messenger, gemach, or organization that distributes on Purim day — verify funds reach the poor that day
• If you cannot find recipients, ask your rabbi or shul — many collect on Purim morning

Who qualifies: Someone who lacks resources for Purim — your rav can guide you if unsure.

${arbaMinimSharedBody(profile)}

Women — recommended mitzvah (not obligatory):
• Women are exempt from this time-bound commandment, but participating is highly popular in both Ashkenazi and Sephardi communities. This checklist marks it as recommended — not a strict daily obligation like men's first-day mitzvah.

Do women need their own set?
• No. Most women use a shared family set (usually owned by a husband or father) or a synagogue set.

The ownership rule (for men):
• In Israel: on the first day of Sukkot only, a man must own the set he waves (matana al menat lehachzir if needed). In the Diaspora, lakhem applies on Day 1 and Day 2; borrowing without a gift is permitted from the first day of Chol HaMoed (Day 3) onward.

Bracha:
• $brachaLine

How to wave:
• $waveLine
• Hold etrog in left, lulav (with hadassim and aravot) in right — or together per your siddur.

Each day of Sukkot (except Shabbat) you may fulfill this recommended mitzvah again.

pattern_issues.key:
The Nine Days (from 1 Av until after Tisha B'Av) are the strictest part of summer mourning in Ashkenazi custom.

From 1
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Nine Days (from 1 Av until after Tisha B'Av) are the strictest part of summer mourning in Ashkenazi custom.

From 1 Av:
• Meat & wine: forbidden entirely, except on Shabbat or at a seudat mitzvah (e.g. brit milah, siyum — ask your rav).
• Laundry: washing, ironing, and wearing freshly laundered outer clothing are prohibited.
• Bathing: bathing or showering for pleasure is prohibited.
• Home & shopping: buying new clothes or beginning major home improvements or repairs is forbidden.

${nineDaysSharedHalacha()}

After the fast (10 Av): Ashkenazi custom continues meat, wine, music, laundry, and bathing for pleasure until chatzos (halachic midday) on 10 Av.

Nine Days Havdalah: On Motzei Shabbat during the Nine Days, use wine or grape juice for Havdalah. Ashkenazi custom (Rema O.C. 551:10): ideally a child (ages 6–9) drinks the cup; if none is present, the one reciting Havdalah drinks it — the mitzvah of Havdalah overrides the custom of restraint.

pattern_issues.key: Choose your words wisely: Guard your speech from Lashon Hara! 🤐👄 This mitzvah is incredibly powerful: Lashon Hara (liter
pattern_issues.key_full: Choose your words wisely: Guard your speech from Lashon Hara! 🤐👄 This mitzvah is incredibly powerful: Lashon Hara (literally 'evil tongue,' meaning derogatory or damaging speech about another person, even if true) is one of the gravest transgressions in Judaism, equivalent to idolatry, forbidden relations, and bloodshed! (Talmud, Arachin 15b)! The saintly Chofetz Chaim dedicated his life to teaching that guarding one's tongue transforms not only the speaker but also protects us from harm and elevates our souls. It's not just about avoiding outright lies or gossip; it means refraining from *any* negative talk about others, even if factual, unless it's for a constructive, halachically sanctioned purpose (like preventing harm). Your words create worlds: choose to build, not to destroy. Today's challenge: aim to speak only positive things about others, or, when in doubt, hold back criticism entirely. It's a daily spiritual workout for your mouth and mind!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about kavod sefarim — the halachic obligation to treat holy books with respect — and the surprising level of detai
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about kavod sefarim — the halachic obligation to treat holy books with respect — and the surprising level of detail this goes into! 📚 The Rambam rules that writings of Torah have inherent sanctity. Practical rules from the Shulchan Aruch and poskim: (1) Don't place a holy book face-down — this is disrespectful. (2) Don't sit on a surface that has a Torah book on it. (3) Don't use a Torah book as a pillow or headrest, or for any of your own needs like blocking rain or the sunlight from your eyes. (4) Hierarchy matters: a Chumash (Five Books) has higher sanctity than Nach (Prophets/Writings); Nach has higher sanctity than a Gemara or other books. Higher sanctity goes on TOP. (5) When a sefer falls to the ground, it is customary to kiss it upon picking it up. (6) If a sefer becomes too worn to read, it cannot be thrown away — it must go to genizah (respectful storage/burial). Many communities have a genizah box for worn-out religious texts. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Get ready for a wake-up call! 🎺 Explore the awesome mitzvah of blowing the Shofar on Rosh Hashanah! This isn't just abou
pattern_issues.key_full: Get ready for a wake-up call! 🎺 Explore the awesome mitzvah of blowing the Shofar on Rosh Hashanah! This isn't just about making noise; it's a powerful spiritual alarm clock for our souls, urging us toward introspection and Teshuva (repentance). The Shofar's blasts have deep meaning: Tekiah is a long, unbroken blast, symbolizing clarity, certainty, and our unified connection to Hashem. Shevarim consists of three shorter, broken blasts, evoking a sigh or cry, representing our brokenness and heartfelt regret for our shortcomings. Teruah is a series of at least nine rapid, staccato blasts, like rapid sobbing, signifying a desperate plea for Divine mercy and a readiness for spiritual awakening. The sequence of these sounds (Tekiah-Shevarim-Teruah-Tekiah, etc.) is crucial. Imagine these ancient sounds piercing the air on the Day of Judgment, stirring your soul to return to your Creator. Source: Numbers 29:1; Talmud, Rosh Hashanah 33b; Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim 590.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Zoreh — winnowing and scattering on Shabbat! 💨 In the Mishkan, winnowing was a critical step in making the S
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Zoreh — winnowing and scattering on Shabbat! 💨 In the Mishkan, winnowing was a critical step in making the Showbread: throwing threshed grain into the air so the wind could blow the light, worthless chaff away from the heavy, valuable kernels. While the core melacha focuses on separating food from non-food using the wind (or your breath), the Jerusalem Talmud expands this concept to include scattering any substance into the wind, even if no food is involved. Because leading halachic authorities adhere to this stricter understanding, the practical applications are eye-opening: you should not shake a tablecloth outside after a meal if the wind will catch and scatter the crumbs, nor should you blow dust off a book cover, as forcing a stream of air to scatter particles mimics the classic act of winnowing. The ultimate lesson of Zoreh is that on Shabbat, we don't use the elements—like the wind or our own breath—to sort, refine, or scatter things. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Zoreh — winnowing and scattering on Shabbat! 💨 In the Mishkan, winnowing was a critical step in making the Showbread: throwing threshed grain into the air so the wind could blow the light, worthless chaff away from the heavy, valuable kernels. While the core melacha focuses on separating food from non-food using the wind (or your breath), the Jerusalem Talmud expands this concept to include scattering any substance into the wind, even if no food is involved. Because leading halachic authorities adhere to this stricter understanding, the practical applications are eye-opening: you should not shake a tablecloth outside after a meal if the wind will catch and scatter the crumbs, nor should you blow dust off a book cover, as forcing a stream of air to scatter particles mimics the classic act of winnowing. The ultimate lesson of Zoreh is that on Shabbat, we don't use the elements—like the wind or our own breath—to sort, refine, or scatter things. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key:
Musaf is an additional Amidah prayer added to the morning service on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and all Jewish holidays.

Wh
pattern_issues.key_full:
Musaf is an additional Amidah prayer added to the morning service on Shabbat, Rosh Chodesh, and all Jewish holidays.

What Musaf is:
Musaf (מוּסָף — literally "additional") is an extra Amidah — the same standing prayer as the rest of the service — but with a special middle blessing describing the Temple offerings (korbanot) that were brought on that day in Temple times, and a prayer for the restoration of the Temple service.

When Musaf is said:
• Every Shabbat
• Rosh Chodesh (new month)
• All Yom Tov holidays
• Chol HaMoed (intermediate days of Pesach and Sukkot)

Why we say it:
In Temple times, special extra offerings (musaf korbanot) were brought on these holy days. Since the Temple was destroyed, we replace the physical offerings with prayer — reciting what was offered and praying for the Temple to be rebuilt.

Timing:
Said after Shacharit (the main morning service), ideally before the end of the 7th halachic hour; bedi'eved valid until sunset (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 286:1).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the obligation to check your mezuzot and tefillin — and how often the Shulchan Aruch says you must do it! 🛠️
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the obligation to check your mezuzot and tefillin — and how often the Shulchan Aruch says you must do it! 🛠️ Mezuzot: The Shulchan Aruch (Yoreh Deah 291:1) requires checking mezuzot twice every seven years. Some say if they are safe indoors in normal climates they can remain unchecked provided they were kosher initially. But in humid or coastal environments (where the parchment is more likely to be damaged by moisture), you should check more frequently. Signs your mezuzah needs immediate checking: the outer case is cracked, water got inside, it fell down. Tefillin: your personal tefillin should also be checked at least twice every 7 years by a professional. If the tefillin get wet or fall into water— check immediately. If they've been stored unused for a long time — check. The practical challenge: checking requires opening the boxes, examining the parchment letters, and resealing the batim (boxes). Only someone knowledgeable and trained can do this. Stay holy, fam!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the ultimate spiritual "high-voltage" connection! ⚡ Tefillin are way more than just leather boxes—they are a
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the ultimate spiritual "high-voltage" connection! ⚡ Tefillin are way more than just leather boxes—they are a physical bond between your mind, your heart, your actions, and G-d Himself! Inside these hand-crafted parchment scrolls are four specific sections of the Torah that proclaim the unity and all-powerfulness of Hashem. 📜 When the Lubavitcher Rebbe was once asked where modern-day technology like computers could be found in the Torah, he said "Tefillin!" 💻 He explained that just as a computer is a synthesis of different machines connected by cables to work as one, Tefillin syncs your brain, your heart, and your hands so they all find harmony in a single coordinated purpose. 🌐 The halacha for wearing them is precise to ensure the "signal" is clear. First, you place the "Shel Yad" on the bicep of your weaker arm, tilted toward your heart. 🦾 Then the "Shel Rosh" is placed exactly above the hairline, centered between the eyes. Learn more about how to put on tefillin!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Bishul — the famous 'cooking' melacha on Shabbat! 🔥 While the original Mishnah calls this category baking be
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Bishul — the famous 'cooking' melacha on Shabbat! 🔥 While the original Mishnah calls this category baking because it relates to making the Mishkan's Showbread, it covers any way you use fire-generated heat to improve an item, tracing back to when workers boiled herbs to create colorful tapestry dyes. The basic law is that you cannot cook, bake, fry, or boil any uncooked food or liquid on Shabbat using a heat source that reaches 'yad soledet bo'—the temperature where your hand would instinctively jump back from the heat. However, because 'there is no cooking after cooking' (Ein Bishul Achar Bishul), you are generally permitted to re-warm dry, solid foods that were already fully cooked before Shabbat began (but you may require putting this onto something else so it doesn't 'look' like cooking). Reheating cold liquids, on the other hand, is completely forbidden because heat dramatically alters and improves them. These laws are very complex so you should probably learn more 👇!
