pattern_issues.key: Learn the Rambam's precise definition of teshuvah — and why verbal confession (vidui) is not optional! 🔄 The Rambam rule
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn the Rambam's precise definition of teshuvah — and why verbal confession (vidui) is not optional! 🔄 The Rambam rules (Hilchot Teshuvah 1:1): teshuvah is a positive Torah commandment whenever one has sinned — whether a minor or major sin. The full process consists of four components: (1) Azivat HaChait — abandoning the sin. You actually STOP doing it, not just feel bad. (2) Charatah — genuine regret. Not just 'I got caught' regret, but authentic recognition that you did something wrong. (3) Kabalat l'atid — committing to change. Deciding that given the same circumstances, you would not repeat the sin. (4) Vidui — verbal confession out loud. Just feeling regret in your heart is NOT sufficient for teshuvah! The vidui must include: naming the specific sin, expressing regret, and committing to change according to the Rambam. He adds: repeating the same sin after teshuvah doesn't mean the earlier teshuvah was invalid — it just means you need to do teshuvah again. And again. As many times as necessary. The door never closes. For sins between people, there's a FIFTH requirement: you must first make restitution or seek forgiveness from the person you wronged. G-d does not forgive interpersonal sins without the person forgiving you first. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: The laws of family purity govern the intimate relationship between husband and wife and are among the most important mit
pattern_issues.key_full:
The laws of family purity govern the intimate relationship between husband and wife and are among the most important mitzvot in the Torah.

What they are:
Taharat HaMishpacha (טָהֳרַת הַמִּשְׁפָּחָה — literally "family purity") is a cycle of physical separation and mikveh immersion that structures the intimacy of Jewish marriage.

The basic cycle:
• During niddah (נִדָּה — menstrual period and its aftermath), the husband and wife refrain from physical contact
• After the period ends, the wife counts seven "clean days" (shivah nekiyim) during which she checks that bleeding has fully stopped
• After the seven clean days, she immerses in a mikveh (ritual pool)
• After immersion, the couple resumes their relationship

Why these laws:
Jewish tradition teaches that these laws bring holiness, freshness, and blessing to marriage. Couples who practice them often describe the monthly rhythm of separation and reunion as one of the most surprisingly positive aspects of Jewish life.

How to learn:
This system is complex and must be learned properly before marriage — or as soon as you begin observance. Take a Taharat HaMishpacha course from a qualified teacher (rebbetzin, kallah teacher, or through organizations like Nishmat or local Jewish community programs).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Nazirite vow — a unique spiritual journey! 🍇✂️💀 A Nazir (Nazirite) voluntarily undertakes three powerful
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Nazirite vow — a unique spiritual journey! 🍇✂️💀 A Nazir (Nazirite) voluntarily undertakes three powerful abstentions for a minimum of 30 days: (1) No Grape Products: Absolutely nothing from the vine – no grapes, wine, grape juice, or even grape seeds/skins. This 'fence around the fence' prevents even a hint of indulgence. (2) No Haircuts: The Nazir allows their hair to grow wild, a visible sign of their dedication. At the vow's end, the hair is shaved and brought as an offering. (3) No Contact with the Dead: Even close family members (like a parent or child) cannot be touched, emphasizing a profound level of purity. The ultimate challenge: if the Nazir becomes ritually impure from a corpse, the clock resets! They must shave, bring sacrifices, and start the entire count over. The Torah calls a Nazir 'kadosh' (holy), sparking a fascinating debate among the Sages: Is this extra holiness praiseworthy? The Talmud embraces both perspectives. While no longer widely practiced (because there would be no way to offer the sacrifices required at the end of the nazirite vow period, since there's no Temple) the nazir teaches us about profound self-discipline and the pursuit of holiness. Source: Numbers 6; Rambam, Hilchot Nezirut 1; Talmud, Nazir.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Tithe-to-Table system that turns every Israeli snack into a holy mission! 🧺 In the land of Israel, your
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Tithe-to-Table system that turns every Israeli snack into a holy mission! 🧺 In the land of Israel, your fruits and veggies aren't just food—they are part of a massive spiritual cycle. Before you can take a single bite, you have to de-classify the produce from its restricted state called Tevel by separating several specific portions. 🏛️ The holy order of operations starts with Terumah Gedolah, a portion of the crop originally given to the Kohanim. Because it is super holy, only Kohanim in a state of purity could eat it (no one is in a state of purity today so we don't eat this nowadays). 🕊️ Then comes Maaser Rishon, where ten percent of what remains goes to the Levites. Next is the swing tithe called Maaser Sheni or Ani. In years one, two, four, and five of the Shemittah cycle, you would take another ten percent to Jerusalem for a holy feast! But in years three and six, that portion becomes Maaser Ani and goes straight to the poor. 🍎🤝 Even today, if you are shopping in an Israeli shuk, you might encounter Tevel, which is food that hasn't been processed yet. Modern halacha keeps the tradition alive by separating small symbolic amounts. It is like a spiritual tax that reminds us that the land doesn't belong to us, it belongs to Hashem!

pattern_issues.key:
Shavuot — receiving the Torah at Sinai. Yom Tov from tonight.

Tonight & tomorrow:
• Do not light candles, begin Maariv,
pattern_issues.key_full:
Shavuot — receiving the Torah at Sinai. Yom Tov from tonight.

Tonight & tomorrow:
• Do not light candles, begin Maariv, or make Kiddush until full nightfall (tzeit) — see the timing note above. Shavuot is the only festival that may not begin before tzeit.
• Light Yom Tov candles at tzeit.
${shehecheyanuErevLines(HebrewCalendarEngine.SHAVUOS, tomorrowCal, profile)}
• Dairy is a cherished Shavuot minhag (cheesecake, blintzes). A festive meat meal with wine fulfills the primary mitzvah of Simchat Yom Tov (O.C. 529:2); many families have dairy first, then a full meat Yom Tov meal.
• All-night Torah learning (Tikkun Leil Shavuot) is a widespread custom tonight.
• Staying up all night: If you remain awake the entire night studying, standard Ashkenazi practice is to hear the morning blessings and Torah blessings from someone who slept, to avoid halachic doubts. However, according to Chabad custom (and several Sephardic authorities), you personally recite the entire sequence of morning and Torah blessings yourself after dawn (alot hashachar), even with zero sleep.
• Read Megillat Rut in many communities (tomorrow).
• Full Yom Tov davening with Full Hallel and Musaf; Akdamut/Megillat Rut per minhag.

No melacha; treat meals and prayer with joy and Torah focus.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about tumat meit — ritual impurity from contact with death — and how it's actually the strongest form of impurity
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about tumat meit — ritual impurity from contact with death — and how it's actually the strongest form of impurity in all of Jewish law! ☠️ A corpse is called the 'father of fathers of impurity' (avi avot hatumah) — the absolute highest level of ritual impurity in the entire Torah system. Here's how it spreads: (1) Direct contact — touching a corpse makes you impure for 7 days. (2) Carrying — carrying a corpse even without touching also causes 7-day impurity. (3) Tent impurity (ohel) — this is the most famous rule: anyone or anything INSIDE the same enclosed space as a corpse becomes impure, even without touching it! So if someone dies in a house, everything in that house — people, utensils, furniture — becomes impure simultaneously. This is why Kohanim (priests) are forbidden from entering a house with a corpse, or even from walking over a grave. Kohanim keep this law today — which is why you'll see a Kohen take a long detour in a hospital (not walking through the building if someone may have died inside). The purification method? The Parah Adumah (Red Heifer ash water) — sprinkled on day 3 and day 7 of the 7-day impurity period. Without it, you stay impure indefinitely. Since we have no Red Heifer today, all Jews are considered to have tumat meit.

pattern_issues.key: Learn the specific conditions under which speaking negatively about someone is PERMITTED — because lashon hara has impor
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn the specific conditions under which speaking negatively about someone is PERMITTED — because lashon hara has important exceptions! 👄 The Chofetz Chaim (Rabbi Yisrael Meir Kagan, the definitive authority on lashon hara law) rules that negative speech is permitted — and sometimes obligatory — when ALL seven conditions are met: (1) The information must be TRUE — you verified it yourself. (2) It must be NECESSARY — there's a genuine toelet (constructive purpose), not just sharing information. (3) You must first try to solve the problem by speaking directly to the person who wronged someone, before reporting to a third party. (4) The information shared must be PROPORTIONATE to the actual harm — don't exaggerate. (5) Your intention must be purely for the benefit, not for personal satisfaction or revenge. (6) The damage caused by revealing it must not be GREATER than the damage prevented. (7) There must be no other way to accomplish the goal. The classic permitted case: warning someone that their potential business partner is a known fraudster. You MUST tell them — withholding the information would cause harm. So the prohibition of lashon hara is not 'never say anything negative' — it's 'never say anything negative without a real, valid purpose.' Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Shabbat is the holiest day of the week — the Jewish Sabbath. It begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars app
pattern_issues.key_full:
Shabbat is the holiest day of the week — the Jewish Sabbath. It begins at sunset on Friday and ends when three stars appear on Saturday night (approximately 25 hours).

What to prepare before Shabbat:
1. Cooking: All cooking must be done before Shabbat begins. A blech (a metal sheet covering the stove flame) or a pre-set hot plate keeps food warm on Shabbat without cooking.
2. Timers: Electric lights, the hot plate, and other appliances can be left on or set to turn on/off using a Shabbos timer (pre-set before Shabbat).
3. Lay out what you need: siddur (prayer book), Chumash (Torah book), clothes, children's items — anything you will want.
4. Candles: Light Shabbat candles 18 minutes before sunset (see next item).

On Shabbat — things we do NOT do:
• Write, draw, or erase
• Cook, bake, or light fire
• Turn electricity on or off (including phones, lights, and appliances)
• Drive or ride in a motorized vehicle
• Handle money or engage in commerce

On Shabbat — what we DO:
• Attend synagogue for Friday night and Saturday morning services
• Say Kiddush — a blessing over wine sanctifying Shabbat
• Eat three festive meals (Friday night, Saturday afternoon, and a third before nightfall)
• Study Torah, sing Shabbat songs (zemiroth), rest, and spend time with family
pattern_issues.snippet:

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the divine command to have the best time ever! 🎊 The Torah doesn't just suggest you have fun on festival day
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the divine command to have the best time ever! 🎊 The Torah doesn't just suggest you have fun on festival days—it literally commands it with the mitzvah of Simchat Yom Tov! This applies to the "Big Three" festivals: Pesach, Shavuot, and Sukkot! 🗓️ Since "there is no real joy without meat and wine," the holiday menu is basically a holy duty. 🍷🥩 But the halacha knows that everyone finds joy differently so it breaks down the party plan for the whole family! For men the focus is on delicious meat and wine. For women the mitzvah is fulfilled through getting new clothes or jewelry specifically for the holiday! For the kids it’s all about the sugar rush—giving them treats and nuts to keep the festive vibes high! 🍬 The man of the house is required to provide these things for his family according to his means! Our sages also warn that if you’re just feasting with your own family and locking the door you’re not doing "holiday joy" but just "stomach joy." To do it right you’ve got to pull up an extra chair for the poor and the lonely because true happiness is only real when it’s shared! At the very least you should give tzedaka to a Jewish person in need so they can enjoy the festival too. Learn more and get ready for the next festival, whenever that may be!

pattern_issues.key: Kiddush Levana (Sanctification of the New Moon) — Birkat HaLevanah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzva
pattern_issues.key_full:
Kiddush Levana (Sanctification of the New Moon) — Birkat HaLevanah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt and the widespread custom is that women do not recite it at all (see Deracheha link below).

$waitLine

Deadline: The window ends at the moment of the full moon (roughly 14 days, 18 hours, and 22 minutes from the molad — about 14.75 days into the month). Saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late depending on the month. This app uses the Hebrew calendar day as a rough guide only — always check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location before the month ends.

When:
• After nightfall (tzeit), standing outdoors under the open sky.
• Ideally on Motzei Shabbat while still in nice clothes — a widespread custom because you are already dressed up.
• In Av, many wait until after Tisha B'Av; in Tishrei, many wait until after Yom Kippur — but say sooner on Motzei Shabbat if waiting risks cloudy nights.
• Not on Shabbat or Yom Tov itself.

How:
• The moon must be clearly visible — if clouds block it, wait for a clear night within the window.
• Use your siddur; it praises G-d for creation — not worship of the moon.

Window closes at the full moon (~14.75 days from the molad). Check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location.

pattern_issues.key: Prepare for the ultimate harvest parade! 🍇 Learn about Bikkurim, the first-fruit offering that was one of the most emoti
pattern_issues.key_full: Prepare for the ultimate harvest parade! 🍇 Learn about Bikkurim, the first-fruit offering that was one of the most emotional and high-energy ceremonies in the entire Temple service. When a farmer saw the very first ripening fruits of the Seven Species—wheat, barley, grapes, figs, pomegranates, olives, and dates—he would tie a reed around them to mark them as holy. 🎀 The journey to Jerusalem was a nationwide block party! 🥳 Pilgrims traveled together in massive groups, and as they entered the city, the local artisans and leaders would stop their work to stand up and welcome them! 🏛️ The streets filled with music from the flutes of the Levi'im as the parade headed toward the Temple Mount. The coolest part is the declaration the farmer made while holding his basket. He would recount the history of the Jewish people from the Exodus until that very moment of standing on his own land. 📜 Rich farmers brought their fruits in baskets of gold and silver, while the poor used simple wicker, but everyone’s offering was cherished. While we can't bring these physical fruits today without the Temple, this ceremony represents the ultimate goal of living with gratitude and national unity. It’s a beautiful preview of the celebrations we’ll see again when the third Temple is built! 🎊🍎.
pattern_issues.snippet: ¡Prepárense para el último desfile de cosechas! 🍇 Aprenda acerca de Bikkurim, la primera ofrenda de fruta que fue una de las ceremonias más emocionales y de alta energía en todo el servicio del Templo

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Torah obligation to TEACH — not just to learn — and the specific mitzvah of 'v'shinantam l'vanecha'! 📚 T
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Torah obligation to TEACH — not just to learn — and the specific mitzvah of 'v'shinantam l'vanecha'! 📚 The Torah says 'And you shall teach them carefully to your children' (Deuteronomy 6:7). This is a positive commandment on every Jewish adult to teach Torah — and specifically, the obligation is first to your own children. The Rambam rules (Hilchot Talmud Torah 1:1): a father is obligated to teach his son Torah. If the father cannot teach, he must hire a teacher. When does the obligation begin? As soon as the child can speak! The first words you teach a child should be Torah — traditionally the Torah verse 'Torah tzivah lanu Moshe' and Shema. Extended meaning: the Talmud rules 'l'vanecha' means both biological children AND students. Teaching students is like raising spiritual children. The mitzvah also applies to each person themselves: you must learn in order to be able to teach. One fascinating ruling: you are NOT permitted to charge money for teaching Torah itself. A teacher may charge for their time away from other work (as opportunity cost), but direct payment FOR Torah teaching is frowned upon and considered a form of desecrating the Torah. This is why many Talmud teachers historically were supported by community funds rather than student tuition.

pattern_issues.key: Candles are lit before sunset on Friday to usher in Shabbat and bring peace and warmth into the home. This is performed
pattern_issues.key_full:
Candles are lit before sunset on Friday to usher in Shabbat and bring peace and warmth into the home. This is performed by the woman of the household (or a man if no woman is present).

There are two customs regarding the blessing:
• Ashkenazi custom: Light the candles first, then cover the eyes, recite the blessing, and uncover. This is done because reciting the blessing is considered accepting Shabbat — after which lighting would be forbidden. Covering the eyes allows the blessing to precede benefiting from the light.
• Sephardi custom: Recite the blessing first, then light the candles.

Every woman should follow the custom of her family. When in doubt, ask your rabbi.

Timing:
Candles are lit before Shabbat begins (before sunset). The amount of time before sunset varies by community:
• Most Ashkenazi communities outside Israel: 18 minutes before sunset
• Jerusalem: 40 minutes before sunset
• Many other communities: 20–22 minutes, or other local customs
Always check your local community Shabbat calendar for the exact time in your area.

Note: There is a strict earliest boundary for this mitzvah. You cannot light candles prior to Plag HaMincha (approximately 1.25 halachic hours before sunset). Lighting before this time invalidates the mitzvah and makes the blessing unnecessary.

pattern_issues.key:
$bedikatLeadIn

This year, Erev Pesach is on Friday (14 Nisan) and the first day of Pesach is Shabbat (15 Nisan):

• Bed
pattern_issues.key_full:
$bedikatLeadIn

This year, Erev Pesach is on Friday (14 Nisan) and the first day of Pesach is Shabbat (15 Nisan):

• Bedikat chametz: Thursday night (night of 14 Nisan, after tzeit) — not Friday night. Recite the first bitul (Kol Chamira).
• Taanit Bechorot: Friday daytime (14 Nisan) — fast or attend a siyum.
• Biur chametz: Friday morning (14 Nisan) by the 5th halachic hour deadline. Both physical destruction and the final Kol Chamira nullification must be finished before this time.
• Mechirat chametz: must be entirely completed before Shabbat/Yom Tov candle lighting on Friday evening.
• First Seder: Friday night (commencing 15 Nisan).
• Second Seder (Diaspora only): Saturday night — transitioning directly from Shabbat into the second day of Yom Tov (not a regular weekend Motzei Shabbat). Kiddush includes Yaknehaz (integrated Havdalah: wine, Kiddush, candle, Havdalah text, Shehecheyanu — no spices/besamim).
• Preparation warning: you may NOT do any prep work (chopping, cooking, table setting) on Shabbat day for the second Seder. All preparations must wait until Shabbat ends at nightfall. Eruv tavshilin does not apply when Yom Tov falls on Shabbat — it is only for when Yom Tov immediately precedes Shabbat.

Confirm candle lighting, Yom Tov, and Seder times with your siddur and rav.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about tochachah — the Torah obligation to rebuke someone doing wrong — and the limits that make it extremely diffi
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about tochachah — the Torah obligation to rebuke someone doing wrong — and the limits that make it extremely difficult to fulfill in practice! 💬 The Torah commands: 'You shall surely rebuke your fellow' (Leviticus 19:17). The Rambam rules this is a positive commandment. But the halacha is full of conditions: (1) Tochachah must be given PRIVATELY — never in front of others. Shaming someone while rebuking them is such a serious violation that the Talmud compares it to murder. (2) You must rebuke in a CALM, gentle voice — not in anger or humiliation. (3) Here's the stunning rule: you must rebuke them again and again — 'even 100 times' if necessary — until either they accept it or they tell you to stop. (4) If the person will respond with insults and become MORE sinful, you are NOT obligated to rebuke. The point is to help, not to trigger. (5) If the person is committing the sin out of ignorance (they don't know it's wrong), there's an opinion that you should leave them be — 'it is better they sin in ignorance than sin defiantly.' The Talmud says: Rabbi Tarfon declared that 'I wonder if there is anyone in this generation who knows how to give tochachah' — implying that doing it correctly (without shaming, at the right time, in the right way) is extraordinarily difficult. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Giving charity (tzedakah — צְדָקָה) is a constant mitzvah. A foundational custom is maaser kesafim (מַעֲשֵׂר כְּסָפִים)
pattern_issues.key_full:
Giving charity (tzedakah — צְדָקָה) is a constant mitzvah. A foundational custom is maaser kesafim (מַעֲשֵׂר כְּסָפִים) — setting aside about ten percent of one's income for charity.

How to calculate:
Maaser is generally figured from net earnings, not gross: start with what you actually receive, then deduct taxes, necessary work-related expenses, and reasonable business overhead before taking ten percent. Ask your rabbi for guidance on your specific situation (self-employment, benefits, one-time windfalls, etc.).

Where to give:
Halachic priorities place local poor individuals, Torah institutions, and the needy of Israel at the top of the list. Excellent choices often include local family relief organizations, community food pantries, and trusted charities that serve those in need in your area and in Israel.

If money is tight:
Jewish law teaches that your own livelihood and basic needs come first. If you cannot comfortably afford the full ten percent without real hardship, you are not expected to compromise your financial stability. Give a minimal, symbolic amount when you can so you still take part in this vital mitzvah — and increase when circumstances allow.

This checklist item is a daily reminder to plan your giving with intention. Confirm amounts and recipients with your rabbi.

pattern_issues.key: Dive into the deep end and learn the kosher fish signs that determine if your seafood platter is a go! 🐟 For a fish to b
pattern_issues.key_full: Dive into the deep end and learn the kosher fish signs that determine if your seafood platter is a go! 🐟 For a fish to be kosher, it needs two specific features: fins and scales. While the fins help it navigate, the Talmud teaches that if a fish has valid scales, it is guaranteed to have fins too! These aren't just any scales, though; they must be visible to the naked eye and easily removable without tearing the skin. 🛡️ Symbolically, the Sages teach that scales represent a coat of armor for spiritual protection against outside influences, while fins represent the ambition and direction needed to swim against the current of life. Interestingly, some fish are masters of disguise! The swordfish is a major point of debate because it has scales as a juvenile but loses them as it grows up. ⚔️ Then you have the sturgeon, whose bony ganoid scales are so stuck on that most rabbis rule it non-kosher, which is why your high-end kosher caviar is usually from a different fish like paddlefish or salmon! 🥯 Did you know? Even though most blood of animals and birds is forbidden to eat (that's why we salt our meat, to remove the blood), fish blood is actually permitted to eat (you're just supposed to leave some fish scales in there so people don't think you're drinking forbidden blood)! Bottoms up! Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about 'avak lashon hara' — the 'dust of negative speech' — and the specific patterns the Chofetz Chaim identifies
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about 'avak lashon hara' — the 'dust of negative speech' — and the specific patterns the Chofetz Chaim identifies as violations! 🌫️ Even when speech isn't explicitly negative, the Chofetz Chaim forbids several 'borderline' patterns: (1) 'He turned out okay' — damning with faint praise. 'Well, I'm surprised he actually delivered the project on time' clearly implies you expected him to fail. (2) 'Oh, him? Don't ask me about him!' — pretending to hold back while making the listener think there's something terrible to say. (3) Mentioning context that makes someone look bad indirectly: 'You went to Shimon's house? You know where he lives — the neighborhood with all the... well.' (4) Praising someone in front of their enemies — knowing that the enemy will respond by saying bad things about them. You triggered the lashon hara even though you technically said something positive. (5) Talking about someone's embarrassing past after they've repented and changed — bringing up their old behavior is avak lashon hara even if technically true. The underlying principle: even speech that isn't explicitly negative can still damage someone's reputation, destroy relationships, or trigger further gossip. The Torah's standard is not just avoiding lies — it's avoiding damage through words, even indirect damage.

pattern_issues.key_full:
${arbaMinimSharedBody(profile)}

Men — Torah obligation:
• The first day of Sukkot worldwide (15 Tishrei) is the Torah-level day for men. In the Diaspora, the mitzvah continues rabbinically on the second day of Yom Tov and Chol HaMoed.
• Bracha: Men say Al netilat lulav every day when taking the lulav (except Shabbat). Shehecheyanu is on the first day only.
• ${arbaMinimMenWave(profile)}

The ownership rule (lakhem — u'lekachtem lakhem):
• In Israel: the strict requirement to fully own your lulav and etrog set applies on the first day of Sukkot (15 Tishrei; Shulchan Arukh O.C. 658:3). If you do not own a set, ask the owner for matana al menat lehachzir (gift on condition of return) before you wave.
• In the Diaspora: because the second day of Yom Tov is kept as a safek de'oraita, the same lakhem ownership requirement applies on both Day 1 and Day 2 — you cannot simply borrow a synagogue set; it must be given to you as matana al menat lehachzir.
• In the Diaspora from the third day onward (the first day of Chol HaMoed): you may borrow a shared or synagogue set without a formal gift — the rabbinic continuation no longer carries the Torah ownership requirement (Peninei Halakha, Laws of Sukkot 13:13).
• In Israel on Chol HaMoed: borrowing without a gift is permitted — ownership was required only on the first day.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Borer — the famous 'selecting' melacha that changes how you eat on Shabbat! 🥗 Borer forbids separating a mix
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Borer — the famous 'selecting' melacha that changes how you eat on Shabbat! 🥗 Borer forbids separating a mixture of different types of objects, specifically by picking what you don't want out of what you do want. For an act of selection to be completely permissible on Shabbat, it must fulfill three strict conditions simultaneously: you must select the 'good' from the 'bad' (Ochel Mitoch Psolet), you must do it by hand (B'yad), and it must be for immediate use (M'yad). The first condition means you can only pick out the items you actually want to use or eat right now, leaving the unwanted items behind in the mixture; pulling the unwanted items out to leave the desired ones clean is a direct violation. The second condition, B'yad, means the selection must be done with your hands or a standard eating utensil like a fork, rather than using a dedicated sorting tool like a strainer, sieve, or peeler, which automatically transforms the act into a forbidden creative labor. The third condition, M'yad, dictates that the selection must be done for immediate or upcoming use—specifically for the meal happening right now—meaning you cannot sort a bowl of food in the morning to serve for lunch hours later. The law of Borer doesn't only apply to food, but anything that's in a mixture, like clothes, toys, etc. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Menapetz—the art of combing and separating raw fibers—and how a textile technique from the an
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Menapetz—the art of combing and separating raw fibers—and how a textile technique from the ancient desert shapes what we can and cannot do on Shabbat! This creative labor traces back to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers had to aggressively brush and comb raw wool or flax to untangle the knots, separate the individual strands, and align them so they would be perfectly straight and ready for spinning into beautiful fabrics. On Shabbat, Menapetz prohibits any action that mimics this industrial refinement of raw, tangled materials into a workable form. While the primary focus of this Melacha is manufacturing textiles, it also creates fascinating guidelines for personal grooming. Because pulling a hard-bristled brush through tangled hair will almost inevitably tear out hairs—which touches on a different prohibition called Gozez, or shearing—the spirit of Menapetz reminds us that we treat ourselves gently on this holy day. Instead of heavy brushing that forcefully separates strands, people use soft brushes or wide-toothed combs gently on dry hair to avoid industrial-style detangling. It turns a simple morning routine into a mindful reminder of the ancient desert workshop, showing how even the way we manage a bad hair day can reflect the deep rest and tranquility of Shabbat Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Menapetz—the art of combing and separating raw fibers—and how a textile technique from the an
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Menapetz—the art of combing and separating raw fibers—and how a textile technique from the ancient desert shapes what we can and cannot do on Shabbat! This creative labor traces back to the construction of the Mishkan, where workers had to aggressively brush and comb raw wool or flax to untangle the knots, separate the individual strands, and align them so they would be perfectly straight and ready for spinning into beautiful fabrics. On Shabbat, Menapetz prohibits any action that mimics this industrial refinement of raw, tangled materials into a workable form. While the primary focus of this Melacha is manufacturing textiles, it also creates fascinating guidelines for personal grooming. Because pulling a hard-bristled brush through tangled hair will almost inevitably tear out hairs—which touches on a different prohibition called Gozez, or shearing—the spirit of Menapetz reminds us that we treat ourselves gently on this holy day. Instead of heavy brushing that forcefully separates strands, people use soft brushes or wide-toothed combs gently on dry hair to avoid industrial-style detangling. It turns a simple morning routine into a mindful reminder of the ancient desert workshop, showing how even the way we manage a bad hair day can reflect the deep rest and tranquility of Shabbat Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Mikra Megillah (hearing the Book of Esther) is a rabbinic mitzvah instituted for Purim (Esther 9:28; Megillah 19a). Men
pattern_issues.key_full:
Mikra Megillah (hearing the Book of Esther) is a rabbinic mitzvah instituted for Purim (Esther 9:28; Megillah 19a). Men and women are equally obligated.

When to hear it:
• Once on Purim evening — after nightfall (tzeit)
• Once on Purim day — you are halachically obligated to hear the Megillah during the daytime (usually right after morning Shacharit and before sunset). The daytime reading is the primary fulfillment of the mitzvah (mitzvat ha'yom); the night reading was instituted later — both are required.

How to fulfill:
• Hear every word read from a kosher megillah scroll by someone who can discharge your obligation (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 690)
• Stand for the blessings; customs when Haman's name is read vary by shul (many make noise; sitting vs standing — follow your community)
• Listen without talking — missed words may require hearing that passage again; ask your rabbi if unsure

Blessings before reading:
• Al mikra megillah
• She'asa nissim
• Shehecheyanu (recited on the first night; Ashkenazim recite it by day as well)

Machatzit haShekel: A widespread pre-Purim custom (not one of the four Purim mitzvot in the same way); many give before Megillah — follow your community. Confirm local reading times with your shul.

Prayers & meals:
• Insert Al HaNissim into every Amidah and into Birkat Hamazon (bentching) all day long on Purim.

pattern_issues.key: Yom HaZikaron (4 Iyar) is Israel's national day of remembrance for soldiers of the Israel Defense Forces and victims of
pattern_issues.key_full:
Yom HaZikaron (4 Iyar) is Israel's national day of remembrance for soldiers of the Israel Defense Forces and victims of terrorism who gave their lives for the State of Israel. It was established by the Knesset in 1963 and always falls the day before Yom Ha'atzmaut.

This is not a Yom Tov — melacha is fully permitted. It is a national civil observance.

Date adjustment: KosherJava adjusts the date when 4 Iyar falls near Shabbat, to keep Yom HaZikaron and Yom Ha'atzmaut together on consecutive days and not adjacent to Shabbat.

In Israel: Memorial sirens sound at 8:00 PM (start of the day, at nightfall) and again at 11:00 AM the following morning. Ceremonies are held at military cemeteries across the country. Flags fly at half-mast.

Prayers (Religious Zionist / Dati Leumi): Tachanun is recited fully at Shacharit — the day is one of solemn national memory. Tachanun is omitted only at Mincha as the calendar transitions into Yom Ha'atzmaut celebrations (Peninei Halakha 5:4:11; Koren Yom Ha'atzmaut mahzor — Mincha ketana before nightfall). Communities that do not treat Yom Ha'atzmaut as a religious day say Tachanun at Mincha too.

Most Charedi and Chabad communities: Do not observe as a religious day; regular weekday davening with Tachanun throughout the day.

The day ends at nightfall with the transition into Yom Ha'atzmaut celebrations.

pattern_issues.key:
$opener

Havdalah inside Kiddush (Yaknehaz):
• When a biblical holiday begins Saturday night (including Rosh Hashana), t
pattern_issues.key_full:
$opener

Havdalah inside Kiddush (Yaknehaz):
• When a biblical holiday begins Saturday night (including Rosh Hashana), that night's Kiddush incorporates havdalah for Shabbat. The order is: (1) wine blessing, (2) holiday Kiddush, (3) candle blessing, (4) havdalah blessing — with the Yom Tov text concluding bein kodesh l'kodesh (not bein kodesh l'chol), (5) Shehecheyanu for the festival.
• This is the YaKNeHaZ order (Yayin, Kiddush, Ner, Havdalah, Zeman) codified in Shulchan Arukh for Motzei Shabbat into Yom Tov.
• Spices are not used.
• Use the Machzor or siddur nusach for Rosh Hashana — do not rely on memory for the long havdalah text.

Before or at Maariv:
• You may say Baruch hamavdil bein kodesh l'kodesh to begin Yom Tov-permitted activities before Kiddush, or rely on Vatodi'enu in the Amidah of Maariv — follow your community.

$prepLead:
• Have round challah, honey, apples, and symbolic foods ready for the Yom Tov meals after Shabbat (minhag).
• Confirm shofar and Musaf times for the first day(s) of Rosh Hashana after Shabbat — shofar is not blown on Shabbat itself.
• Tashlich: when the first day of Rosh Hashana is Shabbat, it is postponed to Sunday (structural rule — avoiding carrying machzorim in public without an eruv).

Candles: after Shabbat ends, light Yom Tov candles from a flame lit before Shabbat began (pre-existing flame).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Birkat Kohanim — the priestly blessing — and the dramatic duchan ceremony that makes it so powerful! 🙌 The T
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Birkat Kohanim — the priestly blessing — and the dramatic duchan ceremony that makes it so powerful! 🙌 The Torah gives the Kohanim a specific three-verse blessing to bestow on Israel (Numbers 6:24-26). In the Ashkenazic tradition, Birkat Kohanim is generally performed only on Yom Tov in regular synagogues outside Israel. In Israel, and among Sephardim everywhere, it is performed every single day at Shacharit. The ceremony: the Kohanim (descendants of Aaron the High Priest) remove their shoes, wash their hands (washed by Levites in many communities — an echo of the Temple service!), and ascend the 'duchan' (raised platform). They cover themselves with their tallits, spread their fingers in the special pattern, and sing the three verses while the congregation faces them with eyes closed. The Talmud (Sotah 39a) says: while the Kohanim bless Israel, the congregation should not look at the Kohanim's hands, because the Divine Presence rests upon them. The fingers form specific shapes — the pattern many may recognize as the 'Live Long and Prosper' Vulcan salute from Star Trek (fun fact: Leonard Nimoy adapted it from his childhood memories of watching Kohanim duchan!). Another interesting halacha: a Kohen who has committed murder cannot perform Birkat Kohanim — 'hands soaked in blood cannot bless' (until he does teshuva and repents).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the prohibition of receiving lashon hara — which is equally serious to speaking it, and which is actually ha
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the prohibition of receiving lashon hara — which is equally serious to speaking it, and which is actually harder to avoid! 🤐 The Chofetz Chaim rules: it is forbidden to LISTEN to lashon hara and believe it. More specifically, it's forbidden to: (1) Listen attentively and encourage the speaker to continue; (2) Enjoy hearing the negative information; (3) Believe what you've heard as established fact. The Talmud says a person who listens to lashon hara is actually worse than the speaker, because the speaker can repent by telling the person he spoke to that he made it up — but the listener has already received the information into his mind and the damage to his perception is done. Practical implications: If someone starts telling you something negative about a third party and you're stuck in the conversation — you have two levels of obligation: (a) Try to redirect or exit. (b) If stuck, consciously decide NOT to believe what you're hearing. You can listen without believing. The Chofetz Chaim calls this 'hearing without accepting.' One key insight: we are commanded NOT to believe lashon hara about another Jew — even if we can't immediately disprove it. We must maintain the assumption of innocence. This is extremely counterintuitive to modern ears, but it's actual halacha. Source: Chofetz Chaim, Hilchot Rechilut; Talmud, Niddah 61a.

pattern_issues.key:
Simchat Torah (23 Tishrei in the Diaspora) — rejoicing with the Torah.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Kiddush, f
pattern_issues.key_full:
Simchat Torah (23 Tishrei in the Diaspora) — rejoicing with the Torah.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; Kiddush, festive meals, joy.
• Sukkah: Most Ashkenazim do not eat in the sukkah on Simchat Torah (it is no longer Sukkot) — Sefardic practice varies; follow your rav.
• Davening: Full Hallel and Musaf (like other Yom Tov days).

The mitzvah of the day — simcha and Torah:
• Hakafot — circling the bimah with Torah scrolls, singing and dancing (often many circuits).
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Complete the last parsha of Devarim and begin Bereshit — the Torah never ends.
• Aliyot — Kol HaNearim (all children together; an adult or bar mitzvah boy recites the blessing with them), Chatan Torah, Chatan Bereshit, and honors for members.
• Many shuls extend Hakafot to the evening or multiple sessions.

Practical tips:
• Dress festively; arrive early for a good spot.
• If you have a Torah honor, prepare your brachot.
• Celebrate responsibly — the day is about love of Torah, not excess.

Candle lighting last night of Yom Tov; havdalah when Yom Tov ends (often with Sukkot/Yom Tov additions in havdalah — use your siddur).

pattern_issues.key:
Erev Tisha B'Av prepares us for mourning the destruction of the Temple.

When the fast begins:
• Tisha B'Av starts at su
pattern_issues.key_full:
Erev Tisha B'Av prepares us for mourning the destruction of the Temple.

When the fast begins:
• Tisha B'Av starts at sunset tonight${sunset?.let { " (approx. $it)" } ?: ""} and continues until nightfall tomorrow night — not dawn to dusk like minor fasts.

Restrictions from chatzos (halachic midday) today:
• From chatzos onward: do not eat meat or drink wine (some extend other restrictions — follow your minhag).
• Reduce pleasurable activities; avoid laundry, haircuts, and swimming per mourning customs.

Seudah hamafseket — the final meal before the fast:
• Eat a simple meal alone, sitting on the floor or a low stool, with only one cooked dish (e.g. a hard-boiled egg dipped in ashes, or bread with cold water).
• Do not eat two cooked dishes together; do not recline; do not greet others with "hello."
• Finish before sunset; afterward, only water is permitted until the fast begins.
• Many recite Birkat HaMazon and then change into non-leather shoes before sunset.

After sunset tonight until nightfall tomorrow:
• Five afflictions apply (like Yom Kippur): no eating/drinking, no leather shoes, no bathing for pleasure, no anointing, no marital relations.
• Additionally: no Torah study except sad topics (Eichah, Job, mourning laws); many sit on the floor until chatzos tomorrow; kinot are recited.$shabbatNote

Ask your rav for details if you are ill, pregnant, or nursing.

pattern_issues.key: Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — Fast of the Firstborn — is observed on Erev Pesach day by firstborn males (and
pattern_issues.key_full:
Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — Fast of the Firstborn — is observed on Erev Pesach day by firstborn males (and some communities include firstborn females — ask your rav).

Why:
• Commemorates the plague of the firstborn in Egypt, when Jewish firstborn were spared.

The fast:
• When Erev Pesach is Shabbat (14 Nisan), Taanit Bechorot is moved to Thursday (12 Nisan) per Rama and Peninei Halakha — not Friday or Shabbat. Attend a siyum that day if that is your minhag.
• Many firstborns avoid fasting entirely by attending a siyum (completion of a Talmud tractate or similar Torah work) followed by a seudat mitzvah (festive meal). The exemption: To be exempt from the fast, you must not only hear the conclusion of the siyum but also participate in the seudat mitzvah by eating at least a kezayit (approx. 1 oz) of bread or cake. Leaving after the siyum without eating does not exempt you — you must still fast.
• If you fast: the fast runs from dawn (alot hashachar) until full nightfall (tzeit) — no eating or drinking in between.

Father of a firstborn son under bar mitzvah:
• If you are a man with a firstborn son below bar mitzvah age, the widespread custom is that you fast or attend a siyum yourself on his behalf — the young child does not observe the fast.

Plan ahead: locate a community siyum in advance if that is your minhag, or confirm fasting rules with your rav.
pattern_issues.snippet: Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — AYUNO of EL Firstborn — se observa en el día de Erev Pesach por hombres primogénitos (y algunas comunidades incluyen mujeres primogénitas — pregunte a su rav).

pattern_issues.key: Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — Fast of the Firstborn — is observed on Erev Pesach day by firstborn males (and
pattern_issues.key_full:
Taanit Bechorot (תַּעֲנִית בְּכוֹרוֹת) — Fast of the Firstborn — is observed on Erev Pesach day by firstborn males (and some communities include firstborn females — ask your rav).

Why:
• Commemorates the plague of the firstborn in Egypt, when Jewish firstborn were spared.

The fast:
• When Erev Pesach is Shabbat (14 Nisan), Taanit Bechorot is moved to Thursday (12 Nisan) per Rama and Peninei Halakha — not Friday or Shabbat. Attend a siyum that day if that is your minhag.
• Many firstborns avoid fasting entirely by attending a siyum (completion of a Talmud tractate or similar Torah work) followed by a seudat mitzvah (festive meal). The exemption: To be exempt from the fast, you must not only hear the conclusion of the siyum but also participate in the seudat mitzvah by eating at least a kezayit (approx. 1 oz) of bread or cake. Leaving after the siyum without eating does not exempt you — you must still fast.
• If you fast: the fast runs from dawn (alot hashachar) until full nightfall (tzeit) — no eating or drinking in between.

Father of a firstborn son under bar mitzvah:
• If you are a man with a firstborn son below bar mitzvah age, the widespread custom is that you fast or attend a siyum yourself on his behalf — the young child does not observe the fast.

Plan ahead: locate a community siyum in advance if that is your minhag, or confirm fasting rules with your rav.
pattern_issues.snippet: Taanit Bechorot (.... ) — Fast of the Firstborn — est observé le jour d'Erev Pesach par les mâles premiers-nés (et certaines communautés comprennent les femelles premiers-nés — demandez votre rava).

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Boneh—the deliberate act of building or assembling a structure—and discover how this manufact
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Boneh—the deliberate act of building or assembling a structure—and discover how this manufacturing boundary directly shapes how we handle household items, furniture, and temporary shelters on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled artisans erected the massive wooden wall panels and fit the heavy foundation sockets together to create a unified, sturdy Tabernacle. The core definition of Boneh is the creation, improvement, or assembly of any structure, vessel, or functional space by combining parts together into a stable unit. On Shabbat, this means that heavy construction, installing permanent shelving, or repairing a loose door handle that fell out of its socket is strictly forbidden. While a person is unlikely to be pouring concrete on Saturday, Boneh introduces highly practical guidelines for routine household adjustments. A classic example is setting up a pop-up canopy or a large, multi-panel tent for shade; because you are assembling a covered shelter that shields a significant area from the sun or rain, it can constitute the creation of an Ohel (tent/canopy), which falls under the restrictions of building. Similarly, snapping together modular plastic furniture or attaching/screwing parts that form a rigid, tight unit are all problems. Learn more!
pattern_issues.snippet: ¡Aprenda acerca de la Melacha de Boneh —el acto deliberado de construir o montar una estructura— y descubra cómo este límite de fabricación forma directamente cómo manejamos artículos de hogar, mueble

pattern_issues.key_full:
$intro

$sederNights
$secondSederHachana
$omerTrigger

Set up before Yom Tov:
• Matzah — shmurah matzah for motzi/matza mitzvah (three matzot on the plate per custom)
• Maror — romaine, horseradish, or bitter herbs per your minhag
• Four cups of wine per participant (grape juice is widely used if needed — ask your rav; not the same debate as Chol HaMoed wine)
• Haggadah for each person (or shared)
• Seder plate: zeroa (shankbone), beitzah (egg), karpas, charoset, maror, chazeret
• Seder plate prep: You should ideally roast your zeroa (shankbone) on Erev Pesach day before sunset. Because the shankbone is not eaten on Seder night, roasting it after the holiday begins violates Yom Tov cooking laws. The egg (beitzah), however, is traditionally eaten during the meal, so it may legally be boiled or roasted on Yom Tov night if needed.
• Reclining (hasebha): Recline to the left when drinking the four cups and eating matzah, korech, and afikoman — do not recline while eating maror or chazeret (they symbolize slavery).
• Festive table; candles for Yom Tov

Kitchen:
• Only kosher-for-Passover food and utensils from this point
• Warm food on a blech or pre-set timer for Yom Tov meals

At the Seder: follow your Haggadah step by step — Kiddush, the order of the night, brachot, and the four cups are all laid out there. ${if (profile.isInIsrael) "One" else "Two"} Seder night(s) this Pesach.

pattern_issues.key: Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when t
pattern_issues.key_full: Kiddush Levana (קִידּוּשׁ לְבָנָה — Sanctification of the Moon) is a monthly blessing said once each Hebrew month when the new moon is visible at night. It is not worship of the moon — it praises G-d for creation's cycles and Israel's renewal. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 42a) compares one who blesses the moon properly to greeting the Shechinah. Men are obligated in this time-bound positive mitzvah; women are exempt (like most time-bound mitzvot) and the widespread longstanding custom is that women do not recite Kiddush Levana at all — prominent authorities (Shelah, Magen Avraham O.C. 426:1) advise against it for kabbalistic reasons as well. Say it outdoors under the open sky when possible, standing, after nightfall. Ashkenazim and Chabad often wait at least three days after the new moon; most Sephardim wait seven (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 426:4); Moroccan and some North African kehillot may begin after three days (Peninei Halakha 05-01-18). Motzei Shabbat is a widespread preference when people are dressed up and the moon is visible — but if waiting risks a string of cloudy nights and missing the monthly window, say it on the first clear weeknight the mitzvah becomes possible. The window ends at the moment of the full moon (roughly 14.75 days into the month) — saying it on the night of the 15th may already be too late; check Sof Zman Kiddush Levana for your location. Also called Birkat HaLevanah.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Makeh B'patish—the final hammer blow that completes a craft—and discover how this creative bo
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Makeh B'patish—the final hammer blow that completes a craft—and discover how this creative boundary pauses the finishing touches on all our physical objects on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where skilled craftsmen would strike a final, decisive blow with a hammer on newly forged metal vessels to smooth out any rough imperfections and render the item fully functional. The core definition of Makeh B'patish is the intentional execution of any final action that takes an incomplete, flawed, or unusable object and transforms it into a finished, ready-to-use product. Under Torah law, this applies to any craft—whether it is snipping a loose, dangling thread off a freshly tailored shirt, inserting a screw to stabilize a table, bending a bent spoon back into position, or forcefully snapping a detached zipper pull back onto its track to make it work again. Similarly, fixing an item by removing a defect—such as removing a hazardous, protruding nail from a wall to make the surface safe and smooth—is viewed as a finishing act of repair. To navigate these boundaries smoothly on Shabbat, observant individuals leave all minor repairs, assembly projects, and finishing touches until after Havdalah, fully enjoying our physical environment exactly as it was prepared before the holy day! Learn more.
pattern_issues.snippet: ¡Aprenda acerca de la Melacha de Makeh B'patish —el golpe final de martillo que completa una artesanía— y descubra cómo este límite creativo detiene los toques finales en todos nuestros objetos físico

pattern_issues.key: Make an appointment for a private audience with the King of Kings! 👑 The Shemoneh Esrei—also known as the Amidah—is the
pattern_issues.key_full: Make an appointment for a private audience with the King of Kings! 👑 The Shemoneh Esrei—also known as the Amidah—is the heart of every Jewish prayer service. Our Sages composed it with "Ruach HaKodesh" (Divine Inspiration), meaning every word is a precise key designed to unlock heavenly gates and bring down spiritual and material blessings! 🔓 Because it is a direct conversation with Hashem, the Halacha has some "royal protocol" rules to keep it focused. First, you have to stand with your feet together, mimicking the angels who have "one leg," to show total devotion (and that you ain't goin' nowhere while talking to the King!). 😇 You also face Jerusalem—no matter where you are in the world—to point your heart toward the site of the Holy Temple! 🏛️ The prayer is split into three sections: three blessings of praise, thirteen requests for things like wisdom, health, and prosperity, and three final blessings of gratitude. On Shabbat and holidays, the format of the middle blessings are changed, so you make sure you're reading the correct Amidah from your siddur! 📜 While concentration (Kavanah) is great to have throughout, the Sages say it is most critical in the very first blessing of "Avot"—if you lose focus there, you’ve missed the core of the encounter (Ashkenazim don't go back to repeat, but Sephardim do)! 🕊️ Enter a different dimension and bring down all the blessings Hashem wants to give you! Learn more! 🎊🙏.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the gid ha-nasheh — the forbidden sciatic nerve — and the remarkable fact that this is one of the only mitzv
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the gid ha-nasheh — the forbidden sciatic nerve — and the remarkable fact that this is one of the only mitzvot the Torah explicitly gives a reason for! 🦵 The Torah says: 'Therefore, the children of Israel do not eat the gid ha-nasheh which is on the hollow of the thigh, to this day, because he (the angel) struck the hollow of Yaakov's thigh on the gid ha-nasheh' (Genesis 32:33). Yaakov wrestled with a divine being all night and was struck on the hip, leaving him limping. In commemoration of this struggle — and Yaakov's perseverance despite the injury — the Torah forbids eating this specific nerve. Two things make this unusual: (1) The mitzvah was actually commanded before Sinai — it appears in Genesis, not Leviticus! The Talmud discusses whether the formal prohibition began at Sinai or at the moment of Yaakov's struggle. (2) The Torah TELLS US WHY — most mitzvot don't come with explicit reasons. Practical kashrut implications: the gid ha-nasheh runs through the entire hindquarter of an animal. Removing it properly requires a highly trained specialist called a 'menaker' (deveiner). The process is so complex that many kosher operations in the Diaspora (especially Ashkenazic communities) simply don't bother — they sell the entire hindquarter to non-Jews rather than attempt to remove the nerve. In Israel, menakers are more commonly available, so hindquarter beef is more widely available in kosher markets there.

pattern_issues.key_full:
Shemini Atzeret / Simchat Torah (22 Tishrei in Israel) — one day combining both.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; festive meals with Kiddush and Shehecheyanu (if not said already).
• Sukkot has ended — in Israel, do not eat or sleep in the sukkah today; festive meals are indoors (not in the sukkah).

Davening highlights:
• Liturgical shift: During Musaf today, the entire Jewish world officially transitions to the winter prayer cycle, universally inserting "Mashiv HaRuach U'Morid HaGeshem" into the second blessing of the Amidah. Tefillat Geshem (the formal prayer for rain) is recited in Musaf.
• Yizkor — memorial prayer in many Ashkenaz communities.
• Hakafot — dancing with Torah scrolls; finish the annual Torah reading and begin Bereshit again.
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Full Hallel and Musaf; Yom Tov Amidah.

Simchat Torah joy:

Evening: Candle lighting and Yom Tov; morning services are long — plan accordingly.

pattern_issues.key_full:
Shemini Atzeret / Simchat Torah (22 Tishrei in Israel) — one day combining both.

Yom Tov:
• Full Yom Tov — no melacha; festive meals with Kiddush and Shehecheyanu (if not said already).
• Sukkot has ended — in Israel, do not eat or sleep in the sukkah today; festive meals are indoors (not in the sukkah).

Davening highlights:
• Liturgical shift: During Musaf today, the entire Jewish world officially transitions to the winter prayer cycle, universally inserting "Mashiv HaRuach U'Morid HaGeshem" into the second blessing of the Amidah. Tefillat Geshem (the formal prayer for rain) is recited in Musaf.
• Yizkor — memorial prayer in many Ashkenaz communities.
• Hakafot — dancing with Torah scrolls; finish the annual Torah reading and begin Bereshit again.
• Synagogue note: Because drinking often occurs during daytime hakafot, many synagogues move the Priestly Blessing (Birkat Kohanim) up to the early morning Shacharit service instead of keeping it in Musaf, so Kohanim are completely sober for the blessing.
• Full Hallel and Musaf; Yom Tov Amidah.

Simchat Torah joy:
• Everyone receives an aliyah in many shuls; Kol HaNearim (children's aliyah) with flags — an adult (or a boy over bar mitzvah) stands with the group and recites the Torah blessing aloud so the aliya is halachically valid.
• Singing, dancing — honor the day with Torah celebration.

Evening: Candle lighting and Yom Tov; morning services are long — plan accordingly.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Rambam's specific, practical description of what the Messianic era will actually look like — you might b
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Rambam's specific, practical description of what the Messianic era will actually look like — you might be surprised how down-to-earth it is! 🌍 The Rambam's famous ruling (Hilchot Melachim 11-12): 'The Messianic King will arise and restore the monarchy of David to its former glory... He will rebuild the Temple and gather all of Israel.' But then the Rambam adds something remarkable: 'Do not think the world will depart from its normal course in the Messianic era... A wolf will not dwell with the lamb — this is a metaphor for Israel dwelling peacefully among the nations.' The Rambam explicitly rules that the Messianic era will be a NATURAL time, not supernatural. Trees will still need to be planted, the sun will rise and set normally. The difference: Israel will be free from foreign oppression, and will be able to devote themselves to Torah study without distraction. 'In that time there will be no hunger, no war, no jealousy, no conflict — for good things will be plentiful, and all delicacies available like dust.' And the ultimate purpose: 'The sages and prophets did not yearn for the Messianic era in order to rule the world or to eat, drink, and be merry — but in order to be free to study Torah and wisdom, with no one oppressing them.' There are other rabbis who disagree and hold that the world will not be so normal, and instead that it will be a time of open miracles. Which one do you think would be better?

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the ultimate "handle with care" policy for Hashem's names! ✍️ The Torah commands "you shall not do so to Has
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the ultimate "handle with care" policy for Hashem's names! ✍️ The Torah commands "you shall not do so to Hashem your G-d," which the sages explain as a strict prohibition against erasing or destroying His holy Name. This isn't just about being careful—it’s about treating the physical representation of the Divine with the highest level of honor! 🏛️ The halacha identifies seven specific Names that are so holy they can never be erased. If a scribe accidentally writes one of these in a Torah scroll and makes a mistake, he can't just reach for an eraser; the entire section must be cut out and placed in a "Genizah" (a respectful storage for holy papers until they can be buried). 📜 This law also means you have to be mindful of where you write these Names. We avoid writing them on anything that might be thrown away or treated disrespectfully—which is exactly why you'll often see people write "G-d" with a dash or use abbreviations like 'יי' in Hebrew books. There have even been documented cases of people writing Hashem's name on blackboards in schools, and the rabbis ruled that the entire blackboard needed to be buried! 📧 Even the "holy nicknames" we use for Hashem, like "The Merciful One," must be treated with dignity, though they don't have the same strict prohibition against erasing as the core seven. 🕊️ It’s a powerful exercise in mindfulness, reminding us that even the letters on a page can carry a spark of the Infinite!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Soter—the deliberate act of dismantling or demolishing a structure—and discover how this crea
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Soter—the deliberate act of dismantling or demolishing a structure—and discover how this creative boundary guides how we handle household items, temporary setups, and container openings on Shabbat! This fundamental category of creative labor traces its origins straight to the construction of the Mishkan, where the Levites carefully disassembled the massive wooden panels and foundation sockets of the Tabernacle whenever the Cloud lifted, preparing the components to be transported and perfectly rebuilt at the next wilderness camp. The core definition of Soter is the intentional dismantling, breaking down, or undoing of any stable structure, vessel, or functional unit. Under Torah law, this applies when the deconstruction is done for a constructive purpose—specifically to rebuild, improve, or clear space for a new structure. However, the Sages extended this boundary to rabbinically restrict destructive dismantling as well, introducing highly practical guidelines for everyday household objects. For instance, halacha directly guides how we handle temporary shelters; dismantling a large outdoor shade canopy or taking down a multi-panel tent on Shabbat can violate Soter, as undoing a covered shelter reverses the act of building. Similarly, unscrewing a loose door handle completely or forcefully taking apart modular plastic furniture units that were tightly locked together falls under this restriction. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key:
The very first act of each day:

What it is:
Modeh Ani (מוֹדֶה אֲנִי — literally "I am grateful") is a two-line prayer s
pattern_issues.key_full:
The very first act of each day:

What it is:
Modeh Ani (מוֹדֶה אֲנִי — literally "I am grateful") is a two-line prayer said the moment you open your eyes. It is the most basic expression of Jewish gratitude — thanking G-d for returning your soul after sleep.

The tradition:
Jewish thought teaches that sleep is a nightly experience of near-death — our soul partially leaves the body each night and is returned in the morning. Modeh Ani acknowledges that G-d chose to give you another day.

The Hebrew text and translation:
"Modeh ani lifanecha, Melech chai v'kayam, shehechezarta bi nishmati b'chemla — raba emunatecha."
"I thank You, living and eternal King, for returning my soul to me with compassion — how great is Your faithfulness."

How to do it:
Say it while still in bed, before getting up, immediately upon waking from your primary nighttime sleep — before doing anything else. Modeh Ani is one of the only parts of davening you can say without washing your hands first — then proceed to Netilat Yadayim (negel vasser; see the next item).

Nighttime sleep vs daytime naps:
You may repeat these words anytime as personal gratitude or mindfulness, but that is not the official morning prayer and does not carry the same halachic status.

Traditional practice reserves Modeh Ani for waking from major nighttime sleep. After a daytime nap, it is not required and is typically not recited — simply wash your hands (negel vasser) per the rules in the next item.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about the Melacha of Matir—the deliberate act of untying a knot—and the fascinating halakhic symmetry that determi
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about the Melacha of Matir—the deliberate act of untying a knot—and the fascinating halakhic symmetry that determines when opening a bundle becomes a forbidden labor on Shabbat! As the direct counterpart to the Melacha of Koshair, untying is rooted in the construction of the Mishkan, where craftsmen had to untie the permanent knots securing the heavy tapestries and ropes whenever the camp packed up to travel through the desert. On Shabbat, Matir prohibits the dismantling of any knot that falls under the category of a permanent or long-term bond. The rule creates a mirror image of the tying laws: any knot that is a Torah-level violation to tie is a Torah-level violation to untie, and any knot that is Rabbinically forbidden to tie is likewise forbidden to untie. This means that if you encounter a tight, professional knot that was meant to stay tied indefinitely—such as a heavily bound cargo cord or a specialized knot on a leather strap—you cannot untie it on Shabbat, even if it was originally tied years ago during the week. This law has direct practical effects on everyday activities, from opening a stuck knot on a package of food to handling tangled shoelaces. If something tightens accidentally into a knot (on Shabbat or even before), we are generally lenient to untie it, because it was never intended to be a knot. Tying something not meant to last more than 24 hours like a simple single (not double) shoe lace is also permitted. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Shemittah — the seventh year when the land of Israel rests — and what this actually means for Jews living in
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Shemittah — the seventh year when the land of Israel rests — and what this actually means for Jews living in Israel today! 🌾 Every seven years, the Torah commands that the land of Israel must rest: no plowing, planting, pruning, or commercial harvesting. Generally you're only allowed to do basic things to keep plants alive but not to improve them. Produce that grows on its own during Shemittah has 'kedushat shevi'it' (seventh-year sanctity) and can't be sold normally. The challenge: how does a country of millions survive without farming for a year? Two main halachic solutions are used today in Israel: (1) 'Heter Mechira' — a legal sale of Israeli farmland to a non-Jew for the year, so technically a non-Jew 'owns' the land during Shemittah and can farm it. The Chief Rabbinate permits this; many orthodox authorities do not. (2) 'Otzar Beit Din' — a rabbinical court takes formal ownership of the crops and distributes them, charging only for labor costs, not for the produce itself. Products with Otzar Beit Din certification need to be treated with Shemittah sanctity in your home (eaten and not wasted or destroyed). If the produce rots on its own that's fine, but you can't add it to the garbage and thus intentionally make it inedible. Many people use special "Shemittah bins" that let the food decompose naturally if they're not going to eat it. Authorities debate whether it's a positive mitzvah to enjoy holy Shemittah fruit. Many say it is! Learn more.

pattern_issues.key: The Torah commands: "Be fruitful and multiply" — Pru u'Revu (פרו ורבו, Bereishit 1:28). Jewish men are obligated to marr
pattern_issues.key_full:
The Torah commands: "Be fruitful and multiply" — Pru u'Revu (פרו ורבו, Bereishit 1:28). Jewish men are obligated to marry and father children (Yevamot 61b; Shulchan Arukh, Even HaEzer 1:1).

Fulfilling the obligation:
A man fulfills pru u'revu once he has at least one son and one daughter who survive, each capable in principle of having children of their own (Yevamot 62a).

Marriage within the covenant:
The wife must be Jewish (Shulchan Arukh EH 2:1). Children born to a non-Jewish woman are not considered his under halacha; Jewish identity follows the mother (EH 4:19; Yevamot 17a). A man is expected to build a Jewish home — not to remain unmarried by choice (Rambam, Ishut 15:16).

A child born to a Jewish woman outside marriage may count toward the mitzvah in principle, but relations outside marriage are forbidden (EH 178; Devarim 23:18). Marriage is the proper path.

Beyond the minimum — la'arev ba'olam (לערב בעולם):
After the Torah obligation is met, the Sages teach a further mitzvah to continue having children — to populate the world with Jewish souls (Yevamot 62b; EH 1:5–8; Rema EH 1:8). How far to pursue this depends on health, circumstances, and personal psak.

When children are not possible:
One who cannot have children despite reasonable effort may be exempt — consult a qualified rav (EH 1:5–6). Poskim discuss infertility treatment, adoption, and other paths; rulings differ. The obligation to seek marriage remains even when fulfillment proves difficult.

pattern_issues.key:
This year, $chagName meets Shabbat on the calendar — you need eruv tavshilin. This doesn't happen every year.

Why (Peni
pattern_issues.key_full:
This year, $chagName meets Shabbat on the calendar — you need eruv tavshilin. This doesn't happen every year.

Why (Peninei Halakha 12:8):
• On Yom Tov, cooking is generally allowed only for that calendar day of Yom Tov, not for the next day.
• When Yom Tov is immediately followed by Shabbat, the Sages required eruv tavshilin so you may cook on Yom Tov for Shabbat, and so Shabbat is not forgotten amid festival preparations.

$whenLine

How (Peninei Halakha 12:8:2):
• Set aside a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (meat, fish, or unpeeled hard-boiled egg are common examples).
• Recite the blessing and declaration from your Machzor or siddur in any language you understand — use the printed wording; do not paraphrase from memory.
• One eruv per household is sufficient.
• Food must be appropriate to eat with bread; at least a kezayit of cooked food should remain until Shabbat prep is done.
• Storage warning: Put the designated eruv foods in a safe, clearly labeled spot. If the eruv foods are eaten or destroyed before you finish cooking for Shabbat on Friday afternoon, your permission to prepare food is canceled (ask your rav if this happens).

Limits:
• Permits cooking and food prep on Yom Tov for Shabbat only — not cooking on one day of Yom Tov for the next festival day.
• Food should be ready early enough Friday afternoon that it could theoretically be eaten before Shabbat.
• Does not permit non-food Shabbat prep (e.g. certain laundry) — ask your rav.

• On Yom Tov, cooking is generally allowed only for that calendar day of Yom Tov, not for the next day.
• When Yom Tov is immediately followed by Shabbat, the Sages required eruv tavshilin so you may cook on Yom Tov for Shabbat, and so Shabbat is not forgotten amid festival preparations.

$whenLine

How (Peninei Halakha 12:8:2):
• Set aside a baked food (challah or matzah) and a cooked food (meat, fish, or unpeeled hard-boiled egg are common examples).
• Recite the blessing and declaration from your Machzor or siddur in any language you understand — use the printed wording; do not paraphrase from memory.
• One eruv per household is sufficient.
• Food must be appropriate to eat with bread; at least a kezayit of cooked food should remain until Shabbat prep is done.
• Storage warning: Put the designated eruv foods in a safe, clearly labeled spot. If the eruv foods are eaten or destroyed before you finish cooking for Shabbat on Friday afternoon, your permission to prepare food is canceled (ask your rav if this happens).

Limits:
• Permits cooking and food prep on Yom Tov for Shabbat only — not cooking on one day of Yom Tov for the next festival day.
• Food should be ready early enough Friday afternoon that it could theoretically be eaten before Shabbat.
• Does not permit non-food Shabbat prep (e.g. certain laundry) — ask your rav.

pattern_issues.key:
Rosh Chodesh (ראש חודש) — the New Month — is a semi-holiday with extra prayer and customs.

Festive meal (mitzvah):
• It
pattern_issues.key_full:
Rosh Chodesh (ראש חודש) — the New Month — is a semi-holiday with extra prayer and customs.

Festive meal (mitzvah):
• It is a mitzvah to increase your meal on Rosh Chodesh — at minimum add an extra dish or special food in honor of the day (Shulchan Arukh O.C. 419:1).
• Have the meal during the day. Poskim write this commemorates the feast the Sanhedrin held at Beit Ya'zek for witnesses who came to testify they saw the new moon (Mishnah Rosh Hashanah 2:5; Orchos Chaim and Kol Bo, cited on O.C. 419).
• Money spent on Rosh Chodesh meals — like Shabbat and Yom Tov — is not deducted from the income allotted to you on Rosh Hashanah; if you spend more for these mitzvos, Heaven adds to your allotment (Pesikta de-Rav Kahana, cited in Tur O.C. 419 and Magen Avraham 419:1).

Davening today (listed in your Morning, Afternoon, and Evening Prayer sections):
• Yaaleh V'yavo in Shacharit, Mincha, and Maariv Amidah — and in bentching when you eat bread
• Half Hallel at Shacharit (Full Hallel if Rosh Chodesh falls during Chanukah; no Hallel on Rosh Chodesh Tishrei / Rosh Hashanah)
• Musaf after Shacharit — remove tefillin before Musaf
• Tachanun is omitted all day

Other customs:
• Fasting and eulogies are generally not done on Rosh Chodesh
• Widespread custom: married women refrain from certain melacha (needlework, laundry, etc.) as an extra mark of honor — ask your rav for details

When Rosh Chodesh spans two days (30th of the previous month and 1st), observances apply to both days.

pattern_issues.key: Learn about Tochen — grinding and reducing substances to small particles on Shabbat! ⚙️ In the Mishkan, this melacha was
pattern_issues.key_full: Learn about Tochen — grinding and reducing substances to small particles on Shabbat! ⚙️ In the Mishkan, this melacha was performed by crushing herbs and minerals into fine powders to create the vibrant dyes for the tapestries and components for the incense. Because the primary category of Tochen applies strictly to things that grow from the earth (Gedulei Karka), dicing vegetables like Israeli salad into exceptionally fine pieces on Shabbat can constitute a direct violation unless you deliberately chop them into significantly larger pieces than your standard weekday cut. This earth-only definition is also why you are forbidden from scraping or crushing hardened, dried mud off your shoes on Shabbat, as breaking down caked soil into dust directly mimics the agricultural act of grinding. Conversely, because there is no grinding restriction for items that do not grow from the ground, you are permitted to finely chop or grate foods like cooked meat or cheese for immediate use. For pre-ground earth products, the halacha provides the exemption that 'there is no grinding after grinding' (Ein Tochen Achar Tochen), allowing one to crush pre-baked items like matzah or crackers into crumbs. This is why people rely on pre-ground coffee, avoid using blenders, change their vegetable chopping sizes, and handle muddy boots carefully on Shabbat, transforming meal preparation and daily upkeep into a profound lesson in refraining from dismantling the structural integrity of creation. Learn more!

pattern_issues.key: A series of short blessings said at the beginning of the morning prayer service, thanking G-d for things we tend to take
pattern_issues.key_full:
A series of short blessings said at the beginning of the morning prayer service, thanking G-d for things we tend to take completely for granted.

What they are:
Birchot HaShachar (בִּרְכוֹת הַשַּׁחַר — Blessings of the Dawn) is a sequence of ~15 blessings at the start of Shacharit. They originated as blessings said at each stage of waking up and preparing for the day — as you open your eyes, stand up, put on clothes, and step outside.

What you thank G-d for:
• Giving intelligence to the rooster to distinguish day from night
• Not making you a non-Jew, a slave, or a woman (men's version; women say "for making me according to His will")
• Opening the eyes of the blind
• Clothing the naked
• Straightening those who are bent
• Spreading the earth over the waters
• Giving strength to the weary
• And more...

What comes before Birchot HaShachar:
In the full morning service, Korbanot (passages about Temple offerings) typically come before or after these blessings. While these may be skipped if time is short, saying them is an important part of the complete service.

Timing:
• The earliest time to say Birchot HaShachar is from chatzos (halachic midnight / chatzos halayla).
• L'chatchila (ideally), say the first blessing — HaNoten LaSechvi Vinah (who gives the rooster understanding to distinguish day from night) — after alot hashachar (dawn). If you said it after chatzos, you have still fulfilled your obligation.
• The other blessings in the sequence may be said from chatzos onward if you wake up very early.
